title: What fails in a “lower-sum-only” extension of Riemann integration?
question:
In Riemann integration, one defines both lower and upper sums
$
L(f,P), U(f,P),
$

and declares a bounded function
$f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} $
to be integrable if

$
\sup_P L(f,P)=\inf_P U(f,P).
$

On the other hand, Lebesgue integration for nonnegative measurable functions is fundamentally built using only lower approximations by simple functions:

$
\int f
=
\sup\left\{
\int \phi : 0\le \phi\le f,\ \phi \text{ simple}
\right\}.
$

For signed functions, one then writes

$
f=f^+-f^-,
$

and integrates the positive and negative parts separately. This separation is necessary because otherwise linearity fails (for instance, integrating only via lower approximations would generally give

$
I(-f)\neq -I(f)).
$

My question is the following.

Motivated by the Lebesgue philosophy, why did people not try to generalize Riemann integration directly in the following way:

a) use only lower sums/approximations,

b) split functions into positive and negative parts as in Lebesgue theory,

c) but continue working purely with intervals/step functions,

thereby avoiding the introduction of measurable sets and abstract measure theory altogether?

Conceptually, this feels more elementary:

A) intervals already have a natural notion of length,

B) step functions on intervals are easy to understand,

C) and one avoids assigning ``measures'' to pathological sets.

So:

Did people attempt such a theory? If yes, what are its drawbacks? What fundamentally fails if one tries to build integration purely from interval-based lower approximations together with the decomposition
$f=f^+-f^-$
? and lower approxmation alone.

Thanks in advance!
answer:
What fails in a lower sum is that just because you decide to ignore pathological sets, does not mean that pathological sets will decide to ignore you. With a lower sum only, you'll get the same answer for the function that is 1 on the rationals and 0 otherwise, and the reverse that is 1 on the irrationals and 0 otherwise. So far, all well and good. But the integral of the sum of these two functions, is very much not the sum of the integrals!

That said, it is possible to generalize the Riemann integral to cover everything that the Lebesgue integral does, without having to introduce measures. This generalization has various names. I learned it as the generalized Riemann integral, then the gauge integral. Wikipedia describes it as the
Henstock-Kurzweil integral
.

The idea is this. We don't need to worry about upper or lower sums. We just need to generalize the idea of "small enough" intervals in a Riemann sum.

The set up is that we want to integrate some function
$f(x)$
over the interval from
$a$
to
$b$
. We'll partition it into intervals that look like
$a = y_0 \le x_1 \le y_1 \le \ldots \le y_{n-1} \le x_n \le y_n = b$
. Our approximation for the first piece will be
$f(x_1) * (y_1 - y_0)$
. For the second piece will be
$f(x_2) (y_2 - y_1)$
. And so on.

Resulting in
$\int_a^b f(x) dx \approx \sum\limits_{i=1}^n f(x_i)(y_i - y_{i-1})$
.

So far this looks like the Riemann integral, except that the pieces can be different sizes. Now we need to define how small the pieces are in a useful way.

We do that by adding
gauge

$\delta$
that maps
$[a, b]$
to the positive reals. We say that the partition is
$\delta$
-fine if
$|y_i - y_{i-1}| < \delta(x_i)$
for each
$i$
. For any gauge
$\delta$
, there exist
$\delta$
-fine partitions. (You can prove this with the lead upper bound principle.) There is a partial order on gauges one gauge is
$\le$
another if it is
$\le$
at every point. The minimum of two gauges is their pointwise minimum. And so on.

Now the idea is that we say that the integral is
$I$
if, for every
$\epsilon > 0$
, there is a gauge
$\delta$
such that all
$\delta$
-fine partitions create a sum within
$\epsilon$
of
$I$
. That is, picking the partition fine enough, gets the sum close enough to the answer.

With constant gauge functions, we get the Riemann integral. But from an open cover of a set of measure 0, we can create a gauge that causes that set to contribute very little to the integral. In this way we can handle all of the pathological cases that the Lebesgue integral does. And some that it does not!

Now you can say, "We just hid measure theory inside of the gauge." You would be correct. But those concepts are not required to understanding how this integral is defined, and why that definition makes sense.

title: How to complete a synthetic proof that a quadrilateral with sides $3$, $5$, $5$, $8$ and diagonal $7$ is cyclic?
question:
I have the following problem:

A convex quadrilateral
$ABCD$
is such that
$AB=3$
,
$BC=CD=5$
,
$AD=8$
,
$AC=7$
. Prove that
$ABCD$
is a cyclic quadrilateral.

I constructed this quadrilateral with compass and straightedge, so it exists and is well defined.

One proof I know uses the law of cosines:

A convex quadrilateral is cyclic iff the measures of a pair of opposite interior angles add up to
$180^\circ$
(for instance,
$\angle B$
and
$\angle D$
).

Because the quadrilateral is convex,
$0 < \angle B < 180^\circ$
and
$0 < \angle D < 180^\circ$
, so the condition in (1) is equivalent to
$\cos\angle B = -\cos\angle D$
.

From the law of cosines in
$\triangle ABC$
:

$$\cos\angle B = \frac{AB^2 + BC^2 - AC^2}{2 \cdot AB \cdot BC} = \frac{3^2 + 5^2 - 7^2}{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5} = -\frac{15}{30} = -\frac12.$$


From the law of cosines in
$\triangle ADC$
:

$$\cos\angle D = \frac{AD^2 + CD^2 - AC^2}{2 \cdot AD \cdot CD} = \frac{8^2 + 5^2 - 7^2}{2 \cdot 8 \cdot 5} = \frac{40}{80} = \frac12.$$


Thus
$\cos\angle B = -\cos\angle D$
, so
$ABCD$
is cyclic.
$\blacksquare$






I would like to find a proof that does not rely on trigonometry.

The fact that
$\cos\angle D = \frac12$
immediately suggests
$\angle D = 60^\circ$
, which gave me the following idea:




Let
$K$
be the point on
$AD$
such that
$AK = AB = 3$
and
$KD = CD = 5$
.


Draw segment
$CK$
.








Here is where I am stuck.
If I can show that
$\triangle CDK$
is an isosceles triangle with a
$60^\circ$
angle, then it is equilateral, so
$CK = 5 = CB$
.

Then
$\triangle ABC \cong \triangle AKC$
by SSS, giving
$\angle ABC = \angle AKC$
.

The angles
$\angle AKC$
and
$\angle CKD = \angle CDK$
are supplementary, so
$\angle ABC + \angle D = 180^\circ$
, proving
$ABCD$
is cyclic.
$\blacksquare$




How can I complete this synthetic proof? In particular, how can I deduce that
$\angle CDK = 60^\circ$
(or directly that
$CK = 5$
) using only the given lengths and the construction, without resorting to the law of cosines?

Other elegant proofs (synthetic or otherwise) are also very welcome.
answer:
We will start from your partial solution and show
$CK = 5$
synthetically.

Let
$K$
be the point on
$AD$
such that

$$
AK=3, \quad KD=5.
$$

Construct an equilateral triangle
$KDE$
on the same side of
$AD$
as
$C$
.
We will show that the points
$E$
and
$C$
are the same, proving that
$\triangle CKD$
is equilateral.

Since
$KD = DE = 5$
and
$AK = 3$
, let
$M$
be the midpoint of
$KD$
. Then

$$
AM = 3 + \frac{5}{2} = \frac{11}{2}, \quad EM^2 = 5^2-\left(\frac{5}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{75}{4}.
$$

Hence

$$
AE^2 = AM^2 + EM^2 = \frac{121}{4} + \frac{75}{4} = 49 \implies AE = 7.
$$

Also
$DE=5$
. Thus
$E$
satisfies

$$
AE = 7, \quad DE=5.
$$

But
$C$
also satisfies

$$
AC = 7, \quad DC = 5.
$$

Given this, consider the two circles centered at
$A$
and
$D$
with radii
$7$
and
$5$
. Since
$C$
and
$E$
are intersections of these circles on the same side of
$AD$
, we must have
$C = E$
.

Therefore
$\triangle CKD$
is equilateral, giving

$$
CK = 5.
$$

Hence
$ABCD$
is cyclic. QED.

Hope this helps!

$ABCD$
is cyclic.
$\blacksquare$




How can I complete this synthetic proof? In particular, how can I deduce that
$\angle CDK = 60^\circ$
(or directly that
$CK = 5$
) using only the given lengths and the construction, without resorting to the law of cosines?

Other elegant proofs (synthetic or otherwise) are also very welcome.

We will start from your partial solution and show
$CK = 5$
synthetically.

Let
$K$
be the point on
$AD$
such that

$$
AK=3, \quad KD=5.
$$

Construct an equilateral triangle
$KDE$
on the same side of
$AD$
as
$C$
.
We will show that the points
$E$
and
$C$
are the same, proving that
$\triangle CKD$
is equilateral.

Since
$KD = DE = 5$
and
$AK = 3$
, let
$M$
be the midpoint of
$KD$
. Then

$$
AM = 3 + \frac{5}{2} = \frac{11}{2}, \quad EM^2 = 5^2-\left(\frac{5}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{75}{4}.
$$

Hence

$$
AE^2 = AM^2 + EM^2 = \frac{121}{4} + \frac{75}{4} = 49 \implies AE = 7.
$$

Also
$DE=5$
. Thus
$E$
satisfies

$$
AE = 7, \quad DE=5.
$$

But
$C$
also satisfies

$$
AC = 7, \quad DC = 5.
$$

Given this, consider the two circles centered at
$A$
and
$D$
with radii
$7$
and
$5$
. Since
$C$
and
$E$
are intersections of these circles on the same side of
$AD$
, we must have
$C = E$
.

Therefore
$\triangle CKD$
is equilateral, giving

$$
CK = 5.
$$

Hence
$ABCD$
is cyclic. QED.

Hope this helps!

title: How to complete a synthetic proof that a quadrilateral with sides $3$, $5$, $5$, $8$ and diagonal $7$ is cyclic?
question:
I have the following problem:

A convex quadrilateral
$ABCD$
is such that
$AB=3$
,
$BC=CD=5$
,
$AD=8$
,
$AC=7$
. Prove that
$ABCD$
is a cyclic quadrilateral.

I constructed this quadrilateral with compass and straightedge, so it exists and is well defined.

One proof I know uses the law of cosines:

A convex quadrilateral is cyclic iff the measures of a pair of opposite interior angles add up to
$180^\circ$
(for instance,
$\angle B$
and
$\angle D$
).

Because the quadrilateral is convex,
$0 < \angle B < 180^\circ$
and
$0 < \angle D < 180^\circ$
, so the condition in (1) is equivalent to
$\cos\angle B = -\cos\angle D$
.

From the law of cosines in
$\triangle ABC$
:

$$\cos\angle B = \frac{AB^2 + BC^2 - AC^2}{2 \cdot AB \cdot BC} = \frac{3^2 + 5^2 - 7^2}{2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5} = -\frac{15}{30} = -\frac12.$$


From the law of cosines in
$\triangle ADC$
:

$$\cos\angle D = \frac{AD^2 + CD^2 - AC^2}{2 \cdot AD \cdot CD} = \frac{8^2 + 5^2 - 7^2}{2 \cdot 8 \cdot 5} = \frac{40}{80} = \frac12.$$


Thus
$\cos\angle B = -\cos\angle D$
, so
$ABCD$
is cyclic.
$\blacksquare$






I would like to find a proof that does not rely on trigonometry.

The fact that
$\cos\angle D = \frac12$
immediately suggests
$\angle D = 60^\circ$
, which gave me the following idea:




Let
$K$
be the point on
$AD$
such that
$AK = AB = 3$
and
$KD = CD = 5$
.


Draw segment
$CK$
.








Here is where I am stuck.
If I can show that
$\triangle CDK$
is an isosceles triangle with a
$60^\circ$
angle, then it is equilateral, so
$CK = 5 = CB$
.

Then
$\triangle ABC \cong \triangle AKC$
by SSS, giving
$\angle ABC = \angle AKC$
.

The angles
$\angle AKC$
and
$\angle CKD = \angle CDK$
are supplementary, so
$\angle ABC + \angle D = 180^\circ$
, proving
$ABCD$
is cyclic.
$\blacksquare$




How can I complete this synthetic proof? In particular, how can I deduce that
$\angle CDK = 60^\circ$
(or directly that
$CK = 5$
) using only the given lengths and the construction, without resorting to the law of cosines?

Other elegant proofs (synthetic or otherwise) are also very welcome.
answer:
Redraw triangle
$ACD$
so that
$CD$
is transported to
$CB$
(see figure below). What you ask is equivalent to proving that points
$ABA'$
are aligned.

Let
$H$
be the projection of
$C$
onto line
$A'B$
. From:

$$
BH^2+CH^2=BC^2\\
A'H^2+CH^2=A'C^2
$$

one gets
$BH=5/2$
and
$CH=5\sqrt3/2$
.

Let then
$H'$
(not shown in the figure) be the projection of
$C$
onto line
$AB$
. From:

$$
BH'^2+CH'^2=BC^2\\
AH'^2+CH'^2=AC^2
$$

one gets
$BH'=5/2$
and
$CH'=5\sqrt3/2$
.

As lines
$AB$
and
$A'B$
have the same distance from
$C$
, we conclude that they are the same line, as it was to be proved.

title: Determinant of a $2026\times 2026$ sine matrix from the Mexican University Math Olympiad [closed]
question:
Closed.
This question does not meet
Mathematics Stack Exchange guidelines
. It is not currently accepting answers.

Please
provide additional context
, which ideally explains why the question is relevant to you and our community. Some forms of context include: background and motivation, relevant definitions, source, possible strategies, your current progress, why the question is interesting or important, etc.

Closed
14 hours ago
.

Improve this question

Determine the determinant of the
$2026 \times 2026$
matrix
$A$
whose entries are defined by

$$
A_{i,j}=\sin(2026i+j),
$$


for
$1\le i\le2026$
and
$0\le j\le2025$
.

This problem appeared in the first round of the 2026 Mexican University Math Olympiad. I would be interested in seeing different approaches or structural observations about the matrix.
answer:
See:
Why do the determinants of the types $\det|f(i)+g(j)|$ and $\det|f(i)g(j)|$ vanish?

Similarly, it follows that
$\det|f(i)g(j)+g(i)f(j)|_{n\times n}=0, n>2, i,j \in [1;n]$

So it follows that
$\det[\sin[ai+bj]_{n\times n}=0, n>2$

title: If polygons approach circles as sides approach infinity, why are circles considered vertex-free?
question:
If a regular polygon with more and more sides increasingly resembles a circle, and in the limit of infinitely many sides becomes indistinguishable from one geometrically and physically, why is a circle formally defined as having no vertices instead of infinitely many infinitesimal vertices?

Since any physical circle is ultimately composed of discrete matter at sufficiently small scales, and since an infinite-sided polygon appears to converge toward a circle, is the distinction between “smooth curve” and “infinitely dense vertices” primarily a consequence of mathematical definitions and continuity theory rather than an unavoidable geometric reality?

More broadly, how do mathematics and physics interpret the relationship between discrete approximations and continuous objects? Are circles fundamentally continuous entities in modern mathematics or can they meaningfully be interpreted as emergent limits of discrete polygonal structures?
answer:
I started with the intuition that a circle could perhaps be viewed as a polygon with infinitely many vertices, since regular polygons with more and more sides begin to visually approach a circle more and more closely. It feels quite normal to think that the vertices do not truly disappear, but instead become infinitely dense or infinitesimally small in the limiting process.

From there, with the help of fellow commentators I began thinking more deeply about limits and infinity. If every finite polygon possesses vertices, then intuitively it feels strange that the limiting object could completely lose that structure.
However, I learned that the limit of a sequence is not treated as the “last approximation,” but as a new object defined through convergence. Because of this, properties shared by every finite approximation do not necessarily survive in the limit itself.

For example, every number in the sequence 0.9, 0.99, 0.999, ... is strictly less than 1, yet the limit equals 1. Mathematics handles this rigorously through the definition of convergence: even though no finite term equals 1, the sequence can get arbitrarily close to 1, so 1 is defined as the limit of the sequence.

But this is also what makes the polygon-circle situation feel different to me intuitively, because unlike decimal expansions, no polygon approximation ever truly “closes” into a circle at any finite stage. No matter how many sides are added, the figure still remains a polygon with straight edges and vertices. The circle only appears in the infinite limiting process itself.

From all of this, my current understanding is that the circle in mathematics is ultimately treated as an idealized abstract shape defined through smoothness and continuity. Polygons can approximate it arbitrarily closely, but in standard mathematics the circle itself is not considered a polygon, even though the intuition of “infinitely dense vertices” naturally arises from the limiting process.

At last this all seems to bring us back again to the long unanswered question of what infinity itself truly is in mathematics.

As in physics infinity is often approximated and used in many plausible ways, in mathematics the nature of infinity seems very different from simply being an extremely large finite number. It appears to possess its own unique properties, one possible outcome of which may be the emergence of the circle through polygonal approximation.

Please tell me whether the path I am going towards is correct or flawed.

title: Why is this curve almost a circle? $~\sec x+\sec y=\sec\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
question:
The blue curve below is the circle
$x^2+y^2=\dfrac{\pi^2}{2}$
. The red curve is
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
.

$~~~~~$

Below they are shown on the same set of axes. (It looks like a single purple curve.)

What is the intuition why
$x^2+y^2=\dfrac{\pi^2}{2}$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
are so similar?

Remarks:

The two curves are not exactly the same. For example,
$\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt2},\frac{\sqrt3\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)$
fits
$x^2+y^2=\frac{\pi^2}{2}$
, but
$\sec\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)+\sec\left(\frac{\sqrt3\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)>-0.65>\sec\left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}\right)+1$
.

Consider the first quadrant. Both curves contain
$\left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2},0\right)$
and
$\left(0,\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}\right)$
. And both curves contain a point, or limiting point, at
$\left(\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
. I wonder if these shared points (at which they have the same gradient or limiting gradient) alone "force" the two curves to be very similar at all other
$x$
-values also. [
EDIT:
@heropup's
answer
led me to find that
$x^2+y^2=\left(\frac{3\pi}{\sqrt2}-2\pi\right)^2$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\frac{3\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
are
somewhat similar
(but not nearly as similar as my original two curves); their lack of extreme similarity may be because they do not share a point, or limiting point, at polar angle
$\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.]

I made a
Desmos graph
of
$x^2+y^2=R^2$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec R+1$
where you can vary
$R$
.

$x^2+y^2=R^2$
can be rearranged to
$y=\pm\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$
. Then we can try to show that
$\sec x+\sec\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$
is nearly constant if
$R=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}$
. I tried power series, without success. (Note that, for both curves, there are no points where
both

$x$
and
$y$
are close to
$0$
, so we need to be careful when using power series approximations.)

I also found that
$x^2+y^2=R^2$
and
$\cos x+\cos y=2-\frac{R^2}{2}$
, where
$R$
is small, are very similar, and I can explain why. From the second equation, we see that both
$x$
and
$y$
must be close to
$0$
, so
$\cos x=\sqrt{1-\sin^2 x}\approx\sqrt{1-x^2}$
and
$\cos y=\sqrt{1-\sin^2 y}\approx\sqrt{1-y^2}$
. Then take
$\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}\approx 2-\frac{R^2}{2}$
and square both sides to get
$x^2+y^2\approx R^2$
.

Context
: I stumbled upon this circle-like curve when I was investigating geometric properties of the yin-yang symbol.
answer:
I took a different approach and parametrized the circle:
$$(x,y) = (r \cos \theta, r \sin \theta),$$
with
$r = \pi/\sqrt{2}$
.  This gives us a plot of the deviation of the implicit equation
$\sec x + \sec y = 1 + \sec r$
from the circle in terms of
$\theta$
as

$$\delta(\theta) = \sec (r \cos \theta ) + \sec ( r \sin \theta ) - 1 - \sec r.$$

Plotting this deviation over
$\theta \in [0,\pi/2]$
gives a maximum at
$\theta = \pi/4$
:

$$\lim_{\theta \to \pi/4} \delta(\theta) = -\frac{2}{\pi} - 1 - \sec r \approx 0.0143629.$$
Of course, the minimum absolute deviation is
$0$
.  So this
quantitatively
characterizes the similarity of the two curves.  But I still feel there is something missing.  Let's look at the function
$\delta$
more closely.  If we think of
$r$
as variable, then for different choices of
$r$
,
$\delta$
behaves in an interesting way in the neighborhood of
$\pi/\sqrt{2}$
:  it nearly flattens out.  Why?

A hint to this behavior comes from the 3D plot of
$z(x,y) = \sec x + \sec y$
.  In the region where the circle is observed, we have the obvious singularities along the planes
$x = \pm \pi/2$
,
$y = \pm \pi/2$
.  So for instance, in the square
$[-\pi/2, \pi/2]^2$
,
$z$
is positive.  Here is a plot of the regions for which
$z \ge 0$
:

So for the circle with radius
$r = \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}$
, by passing through those singularities, we go through regions for which
$z$
is relatively flat--this behavior being inherited from the secant function.  This is why this particular choice of
$r$
works.

That said, I can't say I find this explanation entirely satisfying.

conversation.content:
The blue curve below is the circle
$x^2+y^2=\dfrac{\pi^2}{2}$
. The red curve is
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
.

$~~~~~$

Below they are shown on the same set of axes. (It looks like a single purple curve.)

What is the intuition why
$x^2+y^2=\dfrac{\pi^2}{2}$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
are so similar?

Remarks:

The two curves are not exactly the same. For example,
$\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt2},\frac{\sqrt3\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)$
fits
$x^2+y^2=\frac{\pi^2}{2}$
, but
$\sec\left(\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)+\sec\left(\frac{\sqrt3\pi}{2\sqrt2}\right)>-0.65>\sec\left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}\right)+1$
.

Consider the first quadrant. Both curves contain
$\left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2},0\right)$
and
$\left(0,\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}\right)$
. And both curves contain a point, or limiting point, at
$\left(\frac{\pi}{2},\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
. I wonder if these shared points (at which they have the same gradient or limiting gradient) alone "force" the two curves to be very similar at all other
$x$
-values also. [
EDIT:
@heropup's
answer
led me to find that
$x^2+y^2=\left(\frac{3\pi}{\sqrt2}-2\pi\right)^2$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec\frac{3\pi}{\sqrt2}+1$
are
somewhat similar
(but not nearly as similar as my original two curves); their lack of extreme similarity may be because they do not share a point, or limiting point, at polar angle
$\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.]

I made a
Desmos graph
of
$x^2+y^2=R^2$
and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec R+1$
where you can vary
$R$
.

$x^2+y^2=R^2$
can be rearranged to
$y=\pm\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$
. Then we can try to show that
$\sec x+\sec\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$
is nearly constant if
$R=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}$
. I tried power series, without success. (Note that, for both curves, there are no points where
both

$x$
and
$y$
are close to
$0$
, so we need to be careful when using power series approximations.)

I also found that
$x^2+y^2=R^2$
and
$\cos x+\cos y=2-\frac{R^2}{2}$
, where
$R$
is small, are very similar, and I can explain why. From the second equation, we see that both
$x$
and
$y$
must be close to
$0$
, so
$\cos x=\sqrt{1-\sin^2 x}\approx\sqrt{1-x^2}$
and
$\cos y=\sqrt{1-\sin^2 y}\approx\sqrt{1-y^2}$
. Then take
$\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}\approx 2-\frac{R^2}{2}$
and square both sides to get
$x^2+y^2\approx R^2$
.

Context
: I stumbled upon this circle-like curve when I was investigating geometric properties of the yin-yang symbol.

I took a different approach and parametrized the circle:
$$(x,y) = (r \cos \theta, r \sin \theta),$$
with
$r = \pi/\sqrt{2}$
.  This gives us a plot of the deviation of the implicit equation
$\sec x + \sec y = 1 + \sec r$
from the circle in terms of
$\theta$
as

$$\delta(\theta) = \sec (r \cos \theta ) + \sec ( r \sin \theta ) - 1 - \sec r.$$

Plotting this deviation over
$\theta \in [0,\pi/2]$
gives a maximum at
$\theta = \pi/4$
:

$$\lim_{\theta \to \pi/4} \delta(\theta) = -\frac{2}{\pi} - 1 - \sec r \approx 0.0143629.$$
Of course, the minimum absolute deviation is
$0$
.  So this
quantitatively
characterizes the similarity of the two curves.  But I still feel there is something missing.  Let's look at the function
$\delta$
more closely.  If we think of
$r$
as variable, then for different choices of
$r$
,
$\delta$
behaves in an interesting way in the neighborhood of
$\pi/\sqrt{2}$
:  it nearly flattens out.  Why?

A hint to this behavior comes from the 3D plot of
$z(x,y) = \sec x + \sec y$
.  In the region where the circle is observed, we have the obvious singularities along the planes
$x = \pm \pi/2$
,
$y = \pm \pi/2$
.  So for instance, in the square
$[-\pi/2, \pi/2]^2$
,
$z$
is positive.  Here is a plot of the regions for which
$z \ge 0$
:

So for the circle with radius
$r = \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}$
, by passing through those singularities, we go through regions for which
$z$
is relatively flat--this behavior being inherited from the secant function.  This is why this particular choice of
$r$
works.

That said, I can't say I find this explanation entirely satisfying.

-values also. [

I made a
Desmos graph
of

and
$\sec x+\sec y=\sec R+1$
where you can vary
$R$
.

can be rearranged to
$y=\pm\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$

$\sec x+\sec\sqrt{R^2-x^2}$

both

$x$
and
$y$
are close to
$0$

and

$R$
is small, are very similar, and I can explain why. From the second equation, we see that both
$x$
and
$y$
must be close to

Context
: I stumbled upon this circle-like curve when I was investigating geometric properties of the yin-yang symbol.
answer:
Consider the two functions

$$y_1=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a-\sec (x)}\right)\qquad \text{and} \qquad y_2=\sqrt{\frac{\pi ^2}{2}-x^2}$$
Expand as a series around
$x=0$


$$y_1-y_2=\left(\cos
^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a-1}\right)-\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2 (a-1) \sqrt{(a-2) a}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi }\right)\,x^2+O(x^4)$$

The constant term cancel for

$$a=1+\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$
and, for this value, the next coefficient is

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi }-\frac{1}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cot\left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-5.45722\times 10^{-3}$$

The next coefficient is

$$\small \frac{1}{96} \left(\frac{24 \sqrt{2}}{\pi ^3}+\left(-10-15 \cos \left(\frac{\pi
}{\sqrt{2}}\right)+3 \cos \left(\frac{3 \pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)+10 \cos \left(\sqrt{2}
\pi \right)\right) \cot ^2\left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right) \csc \left(\frac{\pi
}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)$$
which is
$5.39210\times 10^{-3}$

If we compute

$$\Phi(a)=\int_0^{\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}} \big(y_1-y_2\big)^2\,dx$$
it is a real number as long as
$a \ge -0.6509$
; for this value
$\Phi=6.56814\times 10^{-6}$
.

Notice that
$1+\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=-0.650983$
.

Context
: I stumbled upon this circle-like curve when I was investigating geometric properties of the yin-yang symbol.
answer:
Taking

$$
\cases{
x^2+y^2 = r^2\\
\sec x+\sec y=\sec\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt2}+1
}\ \ \ \ \ (1)
$$

after
$y$
substitution we get

$$
r^2 = x^2+\sec ^{-1}\left(1-\sec (x)+\sec \left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)^2=f(x)
$$

The stationary points for
$f(x)$
are given by the solutions to
$f'(x)=0$
. Those points are tangent points between the two curves
$(1)$
. As

$$
f'(x) = 2 x-\frac{2 \tan (x) \sec ^{-1}\left(1-\sec (x)+\sec \left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)
\sec (x)}{\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{\left(1-\sec (x)+\sec \left(\frac{\pi
}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)^2}} \left(1-\sec (x)+\sec \left(\frac{\pi
}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)^2}
$$

a solution is
$x=0$
which gives

$$
r^2 = 0^2+\sec ^{-1}\left(1-\sec (0)+\sec \left(\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right)^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{2}
$$

From the aspect of
$f(x)$
we can conclude that four solutions can be obtained.

title: PDE with mixed Dirichlet-Neumann conditions on sphere with Dirac Delta Neumann condition on vanishing cap
question:
Is there a known closed form solution to the following PDE and its derivative in the direction of the surface normal, or a standard set of tools I might use to derive such?

The PDE is something akin to a Green's function for a ball
$B$
with surface
$S$
, except that it is harmonic everywhere in the interior of the ball rather than having Laplacian equal to the Dirac delta. My notion is that the 'concentrater' point is represented instead in the boundary conditions. The PDE has mixed Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions. The Neumann condition applies over a vanishing spherical 'lens' or 'cap' region
$S_N$
centered at a 'concentrater' point
$y$
, with remainder portion
$S_D$
s.t.
$S = S_N \cup S_D$
. Is there a closed form solution
$G(x)$
for
$x \in B$
as well as its normal derivative
$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')}G(y')$
for
$y' \in S$
, as the measure
$|S_N|$
of the cap goes to zero? The PDE is

$\Delta G(x) = 0 $
for
$x \in B$

$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G(y') = \frac{1}{|S_N|}$
for
$y' \in S_N$
as
$|S_N| \to 0$

$G(y') = 0$
for
$y' \in S_D$

I presume this would be equivalent to framing the Neumann condition in terms of an N dimensional Dirac delta:

$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G(y') = \delta_y(y')$
for
$y' \in S_N$
as
$|S_N| \to 0$

An immediate observation to characterize the normal derivative
$\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G(y')$
of the solution is that it cannot equal 0 everywhere in
$S_D$
since
$\int\limits_{S_N}\frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')}G(y')\ dy' = 1$
and, by the divergence theorem,
$0 = \int\limits_{B} \Delta G = \int\limits_{S_N} \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G + \int\limits_{S_D} \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G$
, so
$\int\limits_{S_D} \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{n}(y')} G = -1$
.
answer:
Yes, you can actually recover what you want by taking the limit a a usual Green's function of the Poisson problem. Recall that it is defined by:

$$
\Delta G +\delta(x-y) =0\\
G(x\in\partial\Omega) = 0
$$

and you just need to send
$y\to y'\in\partial\Omega$
. The issue is that:

$$
G(x,y')=0
$$

due to the Dirichlet boundary conditions. You need to rescale things appropriately to get a finite limit. Intuitively, this is motivated by dimensional analysis. Thinking electrostatically, the source of the Poisson problem goes from volume charge density to surface charge density, so there is a dimension mismatch. This is why you rather need to divide or equivalently take the derivative:

$$
G_B(x,y') = -\partial_nG(x,y')
$$

where
$G_S$
is the sought after "boundary" Green's function. The sign in front depends on the convention of the normal. I chose the usual convention that it points outwards, away from the domain's interior. Continuing the electrostatic analogy, you are asking to find the potential of an ideal dipole located at the boundary, oriented along its normal.

In the case of the unit ball, you can even compute things explicitly. Recall that by the method of images, using a suitable inversion:

$$
G(x,y) = \frac1{4\pi|x-y|}-\frac{1/|y|}{4\pi|x-y/|y|^2|}
$$

or in spherical coordinates assuming with no loss of generality that
$y$
is at position
$(R,0,0)$
and
$x$
at
$(r,\theta,\phi)$
:

$$
G = \frac1{4\pi\sqrt{r^2+R^2-2rR\cos\theta}}-\frac1{4\pi\sqrt{R^2r^2+1-2rR\cos\theta}}
$$

so as expected:

$$
G(R\to 1^-)=0.
$$

However, you do recover:

$$
\begin{align}
G_B(x,y') &= -\partial_R G(R=1)\\
&= -\frac{R-r\cos\theta}{4\pi\sqrt{r^2+R^2-2rR\cos\theta}^3}+\frac{Rr^2-r\cos\theta}{4\pi\sqrt{R^2r^2+1-2rR\cos\theta}^3}\\
&= \frac{r^2-1}{4\pi\sqrt{r^2+1-2r\cos\theta}^3}\\
&= \frac{|x|^2-1}{4\pi|x-y'|^3}
\end{align}
$$

and recognize the signature dipole field in the limit
$x\to y'$
. Note that this potential can also be used when studying the Green's function in the domain complement to the unit ball.

Hope this helps.

title: Intuition for good properties of finitely generated projective modules
question:
Finitely generated projective modules have a number of "nice" properties that are similar to finite-dimensional vector spaces. To give some examples:

The dual of a finitely generated projective module is a finitely generated projective module;

If
$M$
is a finitely generated projective module, the canonical homomorphism
$M \to M^{**}$
is an isomorphism;

Let
$L$
be a left
$R$
-module,
$M$
be a
$(R,S)$
-bimodule and
$N$
be a left
$S$
-module. If either
$L$
or
$N$
is a finitely generated projective module, the canonical homomorphism
$\operatorname{Hom}_R(L,M) \otimes_S N \to \operatorname{Hom}_R(L, M \otimes_S N)$
is an isomorphism;

If
$M$
is a finitely generated projective right-
$R$
module and
$N$
is a left
$R$
-module, the canonical homomorphism
$M \otimes_R N \to \operatorname{Hom}_R(M^*,N)$
is an isomorphism;

As a corollary, if
$R$
is a commutative ring, the trace of an endomorphism of a finitely generated projective module can be defined in a similar fashion as matrices or endomorphisms of a finite-dimensional vector space.

It is intuitive that finitely generated
free
modules have properties similar to vector spaces, as most of the above theorems restricted to finite-dimensional vector spaces are proved using bases. However I do not immediately see that weakening the assumption to projectivity preserves these properties, especially because injective modules are not as well-behaved despite categorically being the dual concept of projective modules.

I have two questions:

Is there any good intuition for why finitely generated projective modules behave so similar to finite-dimensional vector spaces? For example is there a categorical property of finite-dimensional projective modules that these all come from?

Are there any examples of properties that are particular to injective modules and do not have a "dual property" that applies to projective modules?
answer:
The collection of finitely generated projective left
$R$
-modules is the smallest full subcategory of the category of left
$R$
-modules that

contains
$R$

is closed under finite direct sums

is closed under direct summands (i.e., retracts)

The same is true for right modules. Notice that there is no analogous characterization for injective modules. This is not too surprising: for the duality principle to apply, one would need an analogous characterization for projective objects in every abelian category, which is not available.

It follows that once you prove your favorite linear algebra statement for the left
$R$
-module
$R$
and prove that it remains true under finite direct sums and direct summands, it holds for all finitely generated projective left
$R$
-modules. This is actually the standard way of proving such statements, just formulated in a slightly more systematic way.

But there is no need to know the linear algebra fact in advance, since what you do there ("choose a basis...") is essentially the same reduction argument: a basis expresses a vector space over
$K$
as a direct sum of copies of
$K$
.

This observation is especially useful in the following situation, which generalizes most of the examples you mentioned (and covers many more).

Lemma.
Let
$F,G : \mathcal{C} \rightrightarrows \mathcal{D}$
be two functors. Let
$\alpha : F \to G$
be a morphism (aka
natural transformation
). Consider the full subcategory
$\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$
defined by

$$\mathcal{C}' := \bigl\{X \in \mathcal{C} : \alpha_X \text{ is an isomorphism } \bigr\}.$$

Then
$\mathcal{C}'$
is closed under retracts. Moreover, if
$F$
and
$G$
preserve colimits of a certain type, then
$\mathcal{C}'$
is closed under colimits of that type. (For example, if
$F$
and
$G$
preserve finite coproducts, then
$\mathcal{C}'$
is closed under finite coproducts.)

The proof is straightforward. (That's the beauty of category theory: in the right generality, every result becomes trivial.) By duality, the lemma also holds for limits.

Corollary.
Let
$F,G : \mathbf{Mod}_R \rightrightarrows \mathbf{Mod}_S$
be two additive functors. Let
$\alpha : F \to G$
be a morphism. If
$\alpha_R$
is an isomorphism, then
$\alpha_P$
is an isomorphism for every finitely generated projective
$R$
-module
$P$
.

For functors in several variables, simply use parameters or apply this result one variable at a time.

Example.
Fix a left
$R$
-module
$N$
. Consider the two functors

$$\begin{align*} - \otimes_R N &: \mathbf{Mod}_R \to \mathbf{Ab},\\
\mathrm{Hom}((-)^*,N) &: \mathbf{Mod}_R \to \mathbf{Ab}.\end{align*}$$

Both are additive. Consider the morphism
$\alpha$
defined by

$$\alpha_M : M \otimes_R N \to \mathrm{Hom}(M^*,N), \quad m \otimes n \mapsto (\varphi \mapsto \varphi(m) n).$$

It is an isomorphism for
$M = R$
. (Notice that it is
not
sufficient to show that both sides of
$\alpha_R$
are isomorphic to the additive group of
$N$
. One must also check what the map
$\alpha_R$
actually does.) Hence, it is an isomorphism for every finitely generated projective right
$R$
-module
$M$
.

This POV is also useful for developing intuition about whether these morphisms remain isomorphisms for more general modules. This ultimately comes down to which types of colimits the functors on both sides preserve. For example,
$- \otimes_R N$
preserves all direct sums, and indeed all colimits (it is a left adjoint). Starting from
$R$
, one can generate all
$R$
-modules using colimits. However,
$\mathrm{Hom}((-)^*,N)$
does
not
preserve infinite direct sums. Therefore, one should expect that
$\alpha$
is not an isomorphism for
$M = R^{\oplus \mathbb{N}}$
, which is indeed the case for most choices of
$R$
and
$N$
.

title: Compass-only test for parallelism of two lines
question:
Let
$A,B,C,D$
be four distinct points in the Euclidean plane. I would like to decide, using only a compass, whether the lines
$AB$
and
$CD$
are parallel.

I am aware of the
Mohr–Mascheroni theorem
, so in principle many constructions that are usually done with a straightedge can be carried out with a compass alone. In particular, I know that one can construct the intersection point of two non-parallel lines using only a compass.

What interests me here is a much more specific decision problem: I do not need the intersection point itself. I only want to know whether
$AB∥CD$
or not, and I would like to do this in the simplest possible way.

I would be especially happy to see a solution that uses a collapsing compass, in the strict sense that each circle is determined only by its center and one point on it.

I also value elegant and creative constructions, even if they are not the absolutely shortest ones. So if there is a beautiful compass-only criterion for parallelism, I would be very interested in seeing it.

Has such a direct test been studied, and if so, what is the cleanest known construction?
answer:
We use the fact that the midpoint of a segment can be constructed using compass only. For the construction see
here
.

Let
$K$
,
$L$
,
$M$
be the midpoints of
$AD$
,
$AC$
,
$BC$
, respectively. These can be constructed using compass only. Note that
$AB \parallel CD$
if and only if
$K$
,
$L$
,
$M$
are collinear.

But the collinearity of them can be checked using compass only: just draw circles with centers
$K$
,
$L$
, both passing through
$M$
. The points
$K$
,
$L$
,
$M$
are collinear if and only if
$M$
is the only common point of drawn circles.

or not, and I would like to do this in the simplest possible way.

I would be especially happy to see a solution that uses a collapsing compass, in the strict sense that each circle is determined only by its center and one point on it.

I also value elegant and creative constructions, even if they are not the absolutely shortest ones. So if there is a beautiful compass-only criterion for parallelism, I would be very interested in seeing it.

answer:
Here's a pretty straightforward way to do it.

If you start with the two (near) horizontal blue lines and construct points and circles in the order shown here...

making sure that the yellow circle passes through the left-hand intersection of the orange with the lower blue line, then the blue lines are parallel exactly when the yellow, orange and blue ALSO meet that the right-hand intersection of orange and (lower) blue. (They don't quite meet up in this figure, because I drew it freehand!)

You can play with this construction using Desmos Geometry (
https://www.desmos.com/geometry
); I've done so, and here are two versions of the construction, one where the lines are parallel and one where they're not; the critical intersection is in the red circle. I had to use black instead of yellow for the large circle, alas. BTW, the red circle is just to mark the important area -- it's not part of the construction at all.

title: Rigorous solution of the ODE $(1+x)y' = y$
question:
Suppose we want to find all differentiable functions
$y \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$
satisfying the equation
$(1+x)y' = y.$
This is usually done by sloppy "separation of variables" calculations. Here is my attempt to make the argument rigorous. I'd like to know if my reasoning is sound and whether there is an easier (but still rigorous) way to go about this.

First assume
$y \not= 0$
on
$I,$
meaning
$y$
vanishes nowhere on
$I.$
Then necessarily
$-1 \notin I$
(because
$y(-1) = 0$
for every solution
$y$
), so we can legitimately write

$$ \frac{y'}{y} = \frac{1}{1+x}, \quad x \in I. $$
This can be rewritten as

$$ (\log|y|)' = (\log|1+x|)', \quad x \in I. $$

Two functions having the same derivative on an interval means they differ by a constant, so there is a constant
$C$
such that
$\log |y| = \log|1+x| + C$
for every
$x \in I,$
which implies there is a constant
$C >0$
such that
$|y| = C|1+x|$
for every
$x \in I.$
Since
$y \not=0$
on
$I$
and
$y$
is continuous,
$y$
cannot change sign on
$I,$
and since
$-1 \notin I,$
neither can
$1+x$
. Therefore, whatever the combination of signs of
$y$
and
$1+x,$
there exists a constant
$C \not=0$
such that

$$ y = C(1+x), \quad x \in I.$$


We must now consider the possibility that
$y$
has zeroes on
$I.$
Assume that
$y$
has only isolated zeroes (i.e., each zero
$x_0 \in I$
of
$y$
has a neighbourhood in
$I$
where there are no zeroes other than
$x_0$
) and let
$x_0 \in I$
be one of them. Without loss of generality, assume
$x_0$
is an interior point of
$I.$
Then there exists
$r >0$
such that
$(x_0-r, x_0+r) \subseteq I$
and
$y(x) \not=0$
for every
$x \in (x_0-r, x_0+r) \setminus \{x_0\}.$
In particular, the restriction of
$y$
to
$(x_0-r, x_0)$
is a solution to the equation that never vanishes, so there is a constant
$C_1 \not=0$
such that
$y = C_1(1+x)$
for
$x_0-r<x<x_0$
and, analogously, there is a constant
$C_2 \not=0$
such that
$y = C_2(1+x)$
for
$x_0<x<x_0+r.$
By continuity at
$x_0,$

$C_1(1+x_0) = 0 = C_2(1+x_0).$
We conclude that the only possible isolated zero of a solution is
$x_0 = -1.$
That is, if
$y \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$
is a solution with isolated zeroes, then
$-1 \in I$
and the only isolated zero is
$-1.$
In this case, assuming again without loss of generality that
$-1$
is an interior point, we will have again
$y = C_1(1+x)$
for
$x<-1$
and
$y = C_2(1+x)$
for
$x>-1.$
But in order that
$y$
be differentiable at
$x = -1,$

$C_1 = C_2 = C,$
so actually
$y = C(1+x)$
for every
$x \in I.$


So far, we have proven that if
$y \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$
is a solution without zeroes or with only isolated zeroes, then
$y = C(1+x)$
for some
$C \not=0$
(and in the latter case, the only isolated zero is
$-1$
). We still have to consider the case where
$y$
has non-isolated zeroes; indeed,
$y \equiv 0$
is obviously a solution.


Let us see that it is not possible for a solution
$y$
to have non-isolated zeroes and not be the constant solution
$y \equiv 0.$
We will proceed by contradiction. Assume
$y \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$
is a solution with some non-isolated zero and
$y(x_0) \not=0$
for some
$x_0 \in I.$
Let
$J$
be the union of all intervals
$K$
such that
$x_0 \in K \subseteq I$
and
$K$
contains no non-isolated zeroes. Clearly,
$J$
is an interval with
$x_0 \in J \subseteq I$
and
$J$
contains no non-isolated zeroes. In particular,
$J \not=I$
and
$J$
can be left-prolonged or right-prolonged or both within
$I.$
Assume, for example,
$J$
can be right-prolonged. Then
$\sup J$
must be a non-isolated zero of
$y,$
because if it were an isolated zero or not a zero,
$J$
could be enlarged without touching any non-isolated zero, contradicting its maximality. So
$\sup J$
is a non-isolated zero. Put
$z_0 = \sup J.$
We have
$y(z_0) = 0$
and we can find a decreasing sequence
$z_k \to z_0$
such that
$y(z_k) = 0.$
Because the limit
$y'(z_0) = \lim_{x \to z_0} \frac{y(x)-y(z_0)}{x-z_0}$
is assumed to exist, we can compute it through any particular sequence, and taking
$x = z_k \to z_0$
we get
$y'(z_0) = 0.$
But, on the other hand,
$y = C(1+x)$
with
$C \not=0$
for
$x \in J, x < z_0,$
so
$y'(z_0) = C \not=0.$
This contradiction shows that the only solution with non-isolated zeroes is
$y \equiv 0.$


In conclusion, the solutions are the functions
$y = C(1+x)$
with
$C \not=0$
and
$y \equiv 0,$
which can be unified as
$y = C(1+x)$
with
$C \in \mathbb{R}.$
answer:
Assume
$I\subseteq \mathbb{R}$
is an interval. Let
$z:I \to \mathbb{R}$
be a solution.
Then

$$f:=\frac{z}{1+x} : I\smallsetminus{\{-1\}} \to \mathbb{R}$$

is well-defined and differentiable with

$$f' = \left(\frac{z}{1+x}\right)' = \frac{-z}{(1+x)^2} + \frac{z'}{1+x} = \frac{-(1+x)z'}{(1+x)^2} + \frac{z'}{1+x} = 0$$
for all
$x \in I\smallsetminus{\{-1\}}$
. Hence there are constants
$c_1,c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$
with

$$f(x) = \begin{cases}c_1 & \text{ for } x \in I, x < -1\\ c_2 & \text{ for } x \in I, x > -1\end{cases} \iff z(x) = \begin{cases}c_1(1+x) & \text{ for } x \in I, x < -1\\ c_2(1+x) & \text{ for } x \in I, x > -1\end{cases}$$
If
$-1 \notin I$
it immediately follows
$z(x) = c(1+x)$
for some
$c \in \mathbb{R}$
and all
$x \in I$
. If
$ -1 \in I$
then it is
$c_1=z'(-1) = c_2$
since left and right derivatives must coincide. Hence also in this case
$z(x) = c(1+x)$
for some
$c \in \mathbb{R}$
and all
$x \in I$
.

It is easy to check that for all
$c \in \mathbb{R}$
the functions
$x \mapsto c(1+x)$
for
$x \in I$
define solutions. Thus these are all solutions whose domain is an interval
$I\subseteq \mathbb{R}$
.

title: Is every simplicial set $X$ a homotopy colimit of the constant simplicial sets $X_n$ of its $n$-simplices?
question:
Let
$X$
be a simplicial set. May I write

$$
X \simeq \operatorname{hocolim} X_n,
$$

i.e. is
$X$
weakly equivalent to a homotopy colimit (in the usual simplicial-set model structure) of the constant simplicial sets
$X_n$
of its
$n$
-simplices?


(More precisely, I mean that there is some diagram, the homotopy colimit is taken over, which takes values only in the constant simplicial sets given by the simplices of
$X$
.)
answer:
Think of
$X$
as a diagram of shape
$\mathbf{\Delta}^\textrm{op}$
.
Then
$X$
(as a simplicial set) is the homotopy colimit of
$X$
(as a diagram).

If this is still unclear, perhaps it helps to know that (the usual model of) the homotopy colimit of a diagram
$F : \mathbf{\Delta}^\textrm{op} \to \textbf{sSet}$
is the diagonal simplicial set, i.e. the simplicial set whose set of
$n$
-simplices is
$F ([n])_n$
.

title: The probability of drawing $1$ blue marble, then $1$ red marble (with & without replacement)
question:
A jar containing
$10$
marbles:
$7$
blue and
$3$
red. You want to find the probability of drawing
$1$
blue marble, then
$1$
red marble (with and without replacement).

My attempt

I assume that when an item is replaced, the draws are independent. The total number of marbles stays the same. Therefore

Draw 1 (Blue): There are
$7$
blue marbles out of
$10$
.

$$P(\text{Blue})=\frac{7}{10}=0.7$$


Draw 2 (Red): The blue marble is put back, so there are still
$3$
red marbles out of
$10$
.


$$P(\text{Red})=\frac{3}{10}=0.3$$


Therefore, we multiply the probabilities of each independent event.


$$P(\text{Blue then Red})=\frac{7}{10}\times \frac{3}{10}=\frac{21}{100}=\boxed{0.21}$$


When an item is
not
replaced, I assume the draws are dependent. The total number of items decreases by
$1$
for every subsequent draw. Therefore


Draw 1 (Blue): There are
$7$
blue marbles out of
$10$
.


$$P(\text{Blue})=\frac{7}{10}=0.7$$


Draw 2 (Red): The blue marble is kept out. There are now only 9 marbles left in the jar, but still 3 red marbles.


$$P(\text{Red})=\frac{3}{9}=\frac{1}{3}\approx 0.33$$


Therefore, we multiply the probabilities, adjusting for the new sample space.


$$P(\text{Blue then Red})=\frac{7}{10}\times \frac{3}{9}=\frac{21}{90}=\frac{7}{30}\approx \boxed{0.233}$$

Would this be correct?
answer: The solution is correct. The image below is an exhaustive Python search for confirmation (with code). As Andre suggests in the comments, learning to write simulations in Python (likely the easiest for beginners) would be helpful if you indulge in these kinds of problems.

title: A nonzero finite sum including prime numbers and binomial coefficients.
question:
Let
$p\ge 3$
be a prime number and let
$b$
be a positive integer. Prove that

$$\sum_{k=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}(-1)^k \binom{p}{2k}b^{2k} \ne 0.$$

(University of Tehran, IMS TST 2023, 1st Exam, Problem 4/6, Proposed by Amir Ghadermarzi)




My attempt:


Assume for the sake of constradiction that the sum equals zero. We have

$$0=\sum_{k=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}(-1)^k \binom{p}{2k}b^{2k} =\sum_{k=1}^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\binom{p}{2k}(ib)^{2k} \\=\Re((ib+1)^p-1)$$

thus

$$(ib+1)^p-1=ci$$

for some integer
$c$
. By taking conjugates in the last equality we get

$$(-ib+1)^p-1=-ci$$

now multiply last two equalities to get

$$(1+b^2)^p=1+c^2$$

which I failed to solve although I obtained some result by taking different moduli. Any ideas how to continue?


Another approach:


According to the proposer, one can show that among the terms on left hand side, one has its power of
$2$
in its prime factorization less than others, hence the result.

I have not really worked on the second approach because the first one seems much more natural to me and I have not yet given up on that. But Any solution using either of methods or any other method or any idea on this problem is appreciated.
answer:
COMMENT.-For sake of contradiction the expansion of the algorithm yields after division by
$b^2$
and because all the involved binomial coefficients
$\dbinom{p}{k}$
are divisible by
$p$
the relation
$$p-1\equiv0\pmod{b^2}\implies p-1=b^2m$$

This shows that for all
$p$
there are  a lot of integers
$b$
making true the statement and so is for infinitely many primes (those which are not of the form
$b^2m+1$
)

However there are primes, like
$19$
that with
$b=3$
the reasonig above is not sufficient because
$19-1=3^2\times2$
so the case
$(p,b)=(19,3)$
and analogues remain to be proved.

title: Show that for any $x \in \mathbb{R}$, there exist $a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $|b - a| \leq \frac{1}{2}$ and $f(a) < x < f(b)$.
question:
The problem

Let
$f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$
be a function such that the set
$A = \{x \in \mathbb{R} \mid f(x) = 0\}$
is finite, and for all
$x, y \in \mathbb{R}$
, the following relation holds:

$$f(x^3 + y^3) + f(x^3 - y^3) = 2x \left(f^2(x) + f^2(y)\right) - 2yf(x)f(y)$$


Show that for any
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
, there exist
$a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$
such that
$|b - a| \le \frac{1}{2}$
and
$f(a) < x < f(b)$
.


My tries


Let the given functional equation be:

\begin{equation}
f(x^3 + y^3) + f(x^3 - y^3) = 2x \left(f^2(x) + f^2(y)\right) - 2yf(x)f(y) \label{eq:main}
\end{equation}




Finding
$f(0)$
:

Substituting
$x = 0$
and
$y = 0$
gives:

$$f(0) + f(0) = 0 \implies 2f(0) = 0 \implies f(0) = 0$$




Proving
$f$
is Odd:

Substituting
$x = 0$
for an arbitrary
$y \in \mathbb{R}$
:

$$f(y^3) + f(-y^3) = 2(0) - 2yf(0)f(y) = 0 \implies f(-y^3) = -f(y^3)$$

Since the mapping
$y \mapsto y^3$
is a bijection over
$\mathbb{R}$
, it follows that
$f$
is an \textbf{odd function}:

\begin{equation}
f(-t) = -f(t), \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R} \label{eq:odd}
\end{equation}




Deriving the Power Relation:

Substituting
$y = 0$
for an arbitrary
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
:

$$f(x^3) + f(x^3) = 2x\left(f^2(x) + f^2(0)\right) - 0 \implies 2f(x^3) = 2xf^2(x)$$

Dividing by 2 yields the fundamental power relation:

\begin{equation}
f(x^3) = xf^2(x), \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R} \label{eq:power}
\end{equation}








Let
$A = \{x \in \mathbb{R} \mid f(x) = 0\}$
, which is given to be a finite set.


if
$f(x) = 0$
, then
$f(x^3) = x \cdot 0^2 = 0$
, meaning
$x^3 \in A$
. By induction, if
$x \in A$
, then
$x^{3^n} \in A$
for all
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
.
\item If there existed a root
$x_0 \in A$
such that
$0 < |x_0| < 1$
or
$|x_0| > 1$
, the sequence
$(x_0^{3^n})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$
would generate infinitely many distinct roots, contradicting the finiteness of
$A$
.
\item Thus, the only possible elements of
$A$
belong to the set
$\{-1, 0, 1\}$
.


Let us test if
$1 \in A$
(i.e.,
$f(1) = 0$
). By parity, this would imply
$f(-1) = 0$
as well. Setting
$x = 1$
yields:

$$f(1 + y^3) + f(1 - y^3) = 2(1)\left(f^2(1) + f^2(y)\right) - 2yf(1)f(y)$$

Assuming
$f(1) = 0$
, this reduces to
$f(1 + y^3) + f(1 - y^3) = 2f^2(y)$
. Choosing
$y = 1$
gives:

$$f(2) + f(0) = 2f^2(1) \implies f(2) + 0 = 0 \implies f(2) = 0$$

If
$2 \in A$
, then
$2^3 = 8 \in A$
,
$8^3 = 512 \in A$
, etc., which again yields infinitely many distinct roots, a contradiction.


Hence,
$1 \notin A$
and
$-1 \notin A$
, concluding that:

$$A = \{0\} \implies f(x) = 0 \iff x = 0$$

answer:
Here is a partial solution which reduces the problem to showing a certain set is dense.

First, note that
$f(0)=0$
. You have shown that
$f(1)\ne 0$
and
$f(1)=f(1^3)=1f(1)^2$
so
$f(1)=1$
.

Second, we can evaluate at
$x=\pm y$
, giving
$f(2x^3)=2xf(x)^2$
. Combining with the relation
$f(x^3)=xf(x)^2$
, we get a homogeneity relation
$f(2x)=2f(x)$
. This implies that
$f(\frac{x}{2})=\frac{x}{2}$
iff
$f(x)=x$
. Also, we know
$f$
is odd so that
$f(-x)=-x$
iff
$f(x)=x$
.

Next, we can take a symmetric sum to find

$$ f\big( x^3+y^3) = (x+y) (f(x)^2+f(y)^2-f(x)f(y)).$$

Using relationship
$xf(x)^2=f(x^3)$
, which you have already found, this becomes
$$f(x^3+y^3) =f(x^3)+f(y^3)+(f(x)-f(y)(yf(x)-xf(y)).$$

Thus if
$f(x)=x$
and
$f(y)=y$
, we can deduce that
$f(x^3+y^3)=x^3+y^3$
.

Suppose
$x>0$
. We seek to approximate
$x$
by certain rationals. Let
$S$
be a set defined inductively as follows.

$0\in S$
and
$1\in S$
.


$x \in S \iff -x\in S \iff \frac{x}{2} \in S $


$x \in S$
and
$y\in S$
implies
$x^3+y^3\in S$
.

Clearly
$S$
contains only rationals. Using the rules we derived above, we further have that
$f(s)=s$
for all
$s\in S$
.

If we make the assumption that
$S$
is dense in
$\mathbb{R}$
, then we can find a pair
$a,b\in S$
with
$x-\frac{1}{4}<a<x<b<x+\frac{1}{4}$
. Then
$|b-a|<|(x+\frac{1}{4})-(x-\frac{1}{4})|=1/2$
and
$f(a)=a<x<b=f(b)$
.

I am not sure how to prove that
$S$
itself is dense.

The set
$S$
must contain all rationals of the form
$\pm\frac{(1+\frac{1}{2^{3k}})^{3^n}}{2^l}$
where
$k,l\in\mathbb{Z}$
and
$n\in\mathbb{N}$
. It would suffice to show that the values
$\frac{(1+\frac{1}{2^{3k}})^{3^n}}{2^l}$
are dense in
$\mathbb{R_{>0}}$
. Taking logs, we can further reduce to showing that
$3^n\log_2 (1+\frac{1}{2^{3k}})-l$
is dense in
$\mathbb{R}$
. So to solve our original problem it suffices to show that
$3^n\log_2 (1+\frac{1}{2^{3k}}) \mod 1$
is dense in
$[0,1)$
.

Note the expression
$\alpha = \log_2 (1+\frac{1}{2^{3k}})$
is irrational for all
$k\ge 1$
. It would suffice to show the sequence
$3^n\alpha \mod 1$

here

$\alpha = \log_2 (9/8)$

$3$
.

title: Is it known that Delta1 of prime power sequences has remainders exclusively in class {1} modulo 3?
answer:
The key fact is that
$a^{k(p-1)+1} \equiv a\pmod p$
for all primes
$p$
, all integers
$a$
, and all positive integers
$k$
. The case
$k=1$
is exactly Fermat’s little theorem, but one can quickly deduce the general case from the
$k=1$
case. (The easiest way: if
$p\mid a$
then
$0\equiv 0\pmod p$
is obvious; otherwise
$a^{k(p-1)} = (a^{p-1})^k \equiv 1^k \equiv 1\pmod p$
.)

Taking
$p=3$
, we see that
$a^{2k+1}\equiv a\pmod 3$
for all integers
$a$
and positive integers
$k$
. Consequently, if
$n$
is odd (prime isn’t necessary), then
$\{1^n,2^n,3^n,\ldots\} \equiv \{1,2,3,\ldots\} \pmod3$
, from which it’s clear that all differences are
$1\pmod3$
.

$1\pmod3$
.

title: Cover a $10*10$ board with the L shaped without overlapping
question:
Is it possible to cover
$10*10$
board with the
$L$
shaped without overlapping(
$L$
shaped means 3 blocks vertical and 1 at bottom towards right)?

(The figure can also be rotated.)

My solution-

These type of questions are generally solved using invariance but here's another way I solved it

(I don't consider it as fully solved as this uses a conjecture I made up-not sure if it's true)

Consider a rectangle made by the L shape which will be of dimension
$2*4$
made from the L shape we are gives as it contains 3 blocks vertical and 1 at bottom towards right.

But the grid is actually
$10*10$
and
$100\equiv4$
(mod 8) hence there will be 4 1*1 blocks will be left which we can't cover.

Now here's the conjecture -- It's always possible for a shape (In this instance just consider blocks of dim 1*1 joined in some manner) to be turned into rectangles by minimizing number of that shape by rotating. Then we can take tose rectangle and place them in the grid.
answer:
If we color every odd row of the board white and every even row black.

If we place an
$L$
-tetromino on the board, it will cover

either
$3$
black and
$1$
white square

or
$1$
black and
$3$
white squares.

Thus, the number of black/white squares covered by one tetrominoe is odd.

Since
$25$

$L$
-tetrominoes are needed to cover all
$100$
squares, the total number of black/white squares covered by all the tetrominoes must be odd.

However, this is not the case, as there are
$50$
black and
$50$
white squares and therefore it is not possible to cover the
$10\times 10$
board.

title: How can there be finite descent from a transfinite ordinal?
question:
If I understand the proof of Goodstein's theorem correctly, it goes this way:

Each step in the sequence can be paired with a step in a parallel sequence that (1) is strictly decreasing and (2) uses transfinite ordinals.

The proof that the initial sequence terminates lies in the fact that every strictly decreasing sequence of ordinals terminates and the sequence that the initial sequence is in one-to-one correspondence with is strictly decreasing.

So my question is this: if the initial sequence is paired with a sequence that starts with
$\omega$
, isn't this effectively the same as saying that the initial sequence doesn't terminate because it would take infinitely many steps to terminate?

I'm sure I'm missing something here, can someone help clarify my understanding?
answer:
Let’s say we have a strictly decreasing sequence of ordinals starting with
$\omega$
. You seem to believe that this sequence may be infinite.

Well, what is the next element of the sequence? It is some ordinal strictly less than
$\omega$
. These are exactly the natural numbers, so the second element of the sequence is some natural
$n$
. Now the sequence must terminate in at most
$n$
more steps. So the whole sequence terminates in at most
$n+1$
steps, in particular it terminates.

Maybe your confusion is based on the fact that we have no finite bound on the length of
all
decreasing sequences starting with
$\omega$
. But this is irrelevant. For any particular decreasing sequence starting with
$\omega$
, it terminates in finitely many steps.

title: Isometricity of injective $^*$-homomorphisms of $C^*$-algebras [duplicate]
question:
This question already has answers here
:

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

(3 answers)

Closed
22 hours ago
.

This post was edited and submitted for review
16 hours ago
and failed to reopen the post:

Original close reason(s) were not resolved

I am looking for an
elementary
proof of the property in the title. The (well-known) proposition is as follows:

Let
$\mathcal A$
,
$\mathcal B$
be unital
$C^*$
-algebras and
$\varphi: \mathcal A\to\mathcal B$
an injective unital
$^*$
-homomorphism; then
$\varphi$
is an isometry.

Since there are possibly many ways to prove this, at different levels of prior knowledge, I consider it appropriate to set the boundaries for a possible answer. Here are the facts of which I am aware, and I willingly accept in the proof without further explanations:

$\sigma_{\mathcal B}(\varphi(x))\subset \sigma_{\mathcal A}(x)$
;

$\|\varphi(x)\|\le \|x\|$
;

If
$x$
is normal:
$\|x\|=r(x) $
, where
$r(x)$
is the spectral radius; in general
$\|x\|^2=r(x^*x) $
.

I also know the spectral permanence property, and I would appreciate a proof based on that, if nothing more elementary is available.

Addendum.

Based on the feedback by the community, I reorient a bit my question. The users have provided the link

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

Where the closedness of the image of the homomorphism is proved. This suffices to the main purpose, because the injective homomorphism becomes an isomorphism w.r.t. a sub-algebra of
$\mathcal B$
.
The proof appears to be based on functional calculus.
It would be interesting if someone would be aware of alternative and more 'elementary' proof.
answer:
I write this "answer to myself" as a way to test my understanding, since it is for me impossible to do this in a comment. I didn't find a more elementary tool than the functional calculus (i.e. Gelfand isomorphism) suggested at

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

According to a standard argument, it is sufficient to prove isometry on normal elements (for
$\|c\|=\|c^*c\|^{1/2}$
,
$c^*c$
normal). By the spectral radius theorem, we just need the spectral equality
$Y:=\sigma_{\mathcal B}(\varphi(a))= \sigma_{\mathcal A}(a)=:X$
. Now,
$Y\subset X $
holds for any
$*$
-homomorphism, so one is led to disprove
$Y\subsetneq X$
. Assume, by contradiction, that
$Y$
is a (closed)
proper
subset of
$X$
. Now, for
$a$
normal, we have the Gelfand
$*$
-isomorphisms

$$C^*(a)\cong C(X),\quad  C^*(\varphi(a))\cong C(Y)$$

such that
$a\mapsto \mathrm{id}_X $
and
$ \varphi(a)\mapsto \mathrm{id}_Y $
.
The injective
$*$
-homomorphism
$\varphi: C^*(a)\to C^*(\varphi(a))$
is translated into an injective
$*$
-homomorphism

$$
\psi:C(X)\to C(Y), \quad \textrm{Ker}\,\psi=(0)
$$

such that
$\mathrm{id}_X\mapsto \mathrm{id}_Y$
. By the homomorphism property, for any polynomial function
$P$
on
$X$
,
$\psi: P\mapsto P|_Y$
. By continuity and the Weierstrass theorem, the same holds for any continuous function, i.e.
$\psi: f\mapsto f|_Y$
. So the
$^*$
homorphism
$\varphi$
on
$C^*(a)$
is simply represented by a
restriction map
.
By Urysohn's lemma there is
$f\in C(X)$
such that
$f\neq 0$
and
$f|_Y=0$
. Then
$\psi(f)=f|_Y=0$
, so
$\psi$
would not be injective (contradiction).

This question already has answers here
:

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

(3 answers)

Closed
22 hours ago
.

This post was edited and submitted for review
16 hours ago
and failed to reopen the post:

Original close reason(s) were not resolved

I am looking for an
elementary
proof of the property in the title. The (well-known) proposition is as follows:

Let
$\mathcal A$
,
$\mathcal B$
be unital
$C^*$
-algebras and
$\varphi: \mathcal A\to\mathcal B$
an injective unital
$^*$
-homomorphism; then
$\varphi$
is an isometry.

Since there are possibly many ways to prove this, at different levels of prior knowledge, I consider it appropriate to set the boundaries for a possible answer. Here are the facts of which I am aware, and I willingly accept in the proof without further explanations:

$\sigma_{\mathcal B}(\varphi(x))\subset \sigma_{\mathcal A}(x)$
;

$\|\varphi(x)\|\le \|x\|$
;

If
$x$
is normal:
$\|x\|=r(x) $
, where
$r(x)$
is the spectral radius; in general
$\|x\|^2=r(x^*x) $
.

I also know the spectral permanence property, and I would appreciate a proof based on that, if nothing more elementary is available.

Addendum.

Based on the feedback by the community, I reorient a bit my question. The users have provided the link

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

Where the closedness of the image of the homomorphism is proved. This suffices to the main purpose, because the injective homomorphism becomes an isomorphism w.r.t. a sub-algebra of
$\mathcal B$
.
The proof appears to be based on functional calculus.
It would be interesting if someone would be aware of alternative and more 'elementary' proof.

I write this "answer to myself" as a way to test my understanding, since it is for me impossible to do this in a comment. I didn't find a more elementary tool than the functional calculus (i.e. Gelfand isomorphism) suggested at

Sufficient condition for a *-homomorphism between C*-algebras being isometric

According to a standard argument, it is sufficient to prove isometry on normal elements (for
$\|c\|=\|c^*c\|^{1/2}$
,
$c^*c$
normal). By the spectral radius theorem, we just need the spectral equality
$Y:=\sigma_{\mathcal B}(\varphi(a))= \sigma_{\mathcal A}(a)=:X$
. Now,
$Y\subset X $
holds for any
$*$
-homomorphism, so one is led to disprove
$Y\subsetneq X$
. Assume, by contradiction, that
$Y$
is a (closed)
proper
subset of
$X$
. Now, for
$a$
normal, we have the Gelfand
$*$
-isomorphisms

$$C^*(a)\cong C(X),\quad  C^*(\varphi(a))\cong C(Y)$$

such that
$a\mapsto \mathrm{id}_X $
and
$ \varphi(a)\mapsto \mathrm{id}_Y $
.
The injective
$*$
-homomorphism
$\varphi: C^*(a)\to C^*(\varphi(a))$
is translated into an injective
$*$
-homomorphism

$$
\psi:C(X)\to C(Y), \quad \textrm{Ker}\,\psi=(0)
$$

such that
$\mathrm{id}_X\mapsto \mathrm{id}_Y$
. By the homomorphism property, for any polynomial function
$P$
on
$X$
,
$\psi: P\mapsto P|_Y$
. By continuity and the Weierstrass theorem, the same holds for any continuous function, i.e.
$\psi: f\mapsto f|_Y$
. So the
$^*$
homorphism
$\varphi$
on
$C^*(a)$
is simply represented by a
restriction map
.
By Urysohn's lemma there is
$f\in C(X)$
such that
$f\neq 0$
and
$f|_Y=0$
. Then
$\psi(f)=f|_Y=0$
, so
$\psi$
would not be injective (contradiction).

title: Distributing a for all quantifier over a union of disjoint sets ( for proving well-definedness of functions defined piecewise)
question:
my book features an exercise aboout functions I can't really complete. This is about rigorously showing that a function defined piecewise is unique and well-defined (Tao Analysis I 3.3.8(d))
This exercise has already been given an answer in
Show that there exists a unique function $h:X\cup Y\to Z$ such that $h\circ\iota_{X\to X\cup Y}=f$ and $h\circ\iota_{Y\to X\cup Y}=g$.
, however, it misses a crucial part of showing that the function is well defined.
The proposition goes as follows:

Let
$f:X \to Z,g:Y \to Z$
be functions with
$X\cap Y=\emptyset$
. Show that there exists a unique
$h:X\cup Y \to Z$
such that
$h\circ\iota_{X\to X\cup Y}=f$
and
$h\circ\iota_{Y\to X\cup Y}=g$

Define
$h$
piecewise. We need to show well-definedness, which is equivalent to
$\forall x \in X\cup Y, \exists!y \in Z:h(x)=y$

Intuitively it is obvious that this follows from well definedness of the other two functions, however, I don't know how to show this rigorously. I need a way to distribute the for all quantifier. Normally you get something like this:
let
$P(x):\exists!y \in Z:h(x)=y$

$$\forall x \in X\cup Y:P(x) \iff$$


$$\forall x:x \in X\cup Y \implies P(x) \iff$$


$$\forall x:(x \in X \lor x \in Y) \implies P(x) \iff$$


$$\forall x:(x \in X \implies P(x)) \land \forall x:(x \in Y \implies P(x)) \iff$$


$$\forall x \in X : P(x)\land \forall x \in Y : P(x)$$


What confuses me is that it seems like this actually works(if we plug in what P(x) is then the statement is clearly true), however I never used disjointness in the derivation before, and it is definitely crucial for well definedness. I think there must be a mistake in my reasoning.


Also when sets are disjoint we get
$x\in X\cup Y \iff x\in X \oplus x\in Y$
which is clearly different from what I used for any general sets
$x\in X\cup Y \iff x\in X \land x\in Y$
. Also there isn't a handy way to distribute implication over XOR, however I found

$$(A\oplus B) \implies C \iff (A\implies C) \leftrightarrow (B\implies C)$$

but this doesn't seem particularly useful since I don't know how to distribute for all quantifier over iff.

Thanks for any help because I become a little hopeless with getting over this tiny step.
answer:
The equivalence is correct:

$\forall a \in X \cup Y: P(a)$
is equivalent to
$(\forall a \in X: P(a)) \land (\forall a \in Y: P(a))$
.

The issue here is the definition of "
$h(a) = b$
", which is

$$(a \in X \land b = f(a)) \lor (a \in Y \land b = g(a)).$$

When you wrote
$P(a) = \exists ! b : h(a) = b$
, this formula has
$X$
and
$Y$
in inside it, so perhaps
$P(a,X,Y)$
would be better notation to show all the parameters.

The equivalence becomes

$$\forall a \in X \cup Y: P(a,X,Y) \Leftrightarrow (\forall a \in X: P(a,X,Y)) \land (\forall a \in Y: P(a,X,Y)).$$

The two halves on the right side don't "split" into an
$X$
case and a
$Y$
case as cleanly as the original notation made it appear.

To see how this matters for the problem at hand, suppose
$X \cap Y \not = \emptyset$
,
$f(a) = 0$
for
$a \in X$
and
$g(a) = 1$
for
$a \in Y$
. Then
$h$
would not be well defined. In that case,

$(\forall a \in X: P(a))$
and
$(\forall a \in Y: P(a))$
would both be false, although the overall equivalence would still be correct.

It is easier to separate the "existence" from the "uniqueness" part of showing
$h$
is well defined. The notation "
$h(a) = b$
" when
$h$
may not be a function is also a little confusing to me, but I will try to mirror it below.

For uniqueness, suppose
$h(a) = b$
and
$h(a) = b'$
. If
$a \in X$
, then we have
$h(a) = f(a)$
so
$f(a) = b$
and
$f(a) = b'$
.
This is where we use
$X \cap Y = 0$
, to evaluate the definition of
$h$
using the assumption
$a \in X$
.
We then get
$b = b'$
because
$f$
is a function.  If
$a \not \in X$
then
$a \in Y$
, so we have
$g(a) = b$
and
$g(a) = b'$
, and again
$b = b'$
because
$g$
is a function.
