title: What exactly does “strictly greater consistency strength” mean?
question:
I am trying to understand the precise meaning of "greater consistency strength."

On Wikipedia's article on
equiconsistency
, I found the following formulation:

If
$T$
is consistent relative to
$S$
, but
$S$
is not known to be consistent relative to
$T$
, then we say that
$S$
has greater consistency strength than
$T$
.

I do not understand exactly what "is not known to be consistent relative to" means here. Is this only an epistemic statement about our current knowledge? If later someone proves the relative consistency of
$S$
from
$T$
, would the statement "
$S$
has greater consistency strength than
$T$
" change its truth value? That seems strange to me.

I found another, more formal, definition in Joel David Hamkins' paper
Nonlinearity and illfoundedness in the hierarchy of large cardinal consistency strength
:

Definition 1. Theory
$S$
is below theory
$T$
in consistency strength, written
$S \leq T$
, if the implication
$\operatorname{Con}(T) \to \operatorname{Con}(S)$
is provable in a base theory, fixed for the purpose of this relation. The theory
$S$
is strictly below
$T$
, written
$S<T$
, if
$S\leq T$
but not conversely; and the theories are equiconsistent, written
$S\equiv T$
, when
$S\leq T$
and
$T\leq S$
.

This definition is much clearer, but then I am confused about how strictness is proved.

For example, suppose the base theory is
$\mathrm{PA}$
, and suppose I want to reason only finitistically or in a very weak metatheory. To prove
$S<T$
, I would need to prove not only

$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(T)\to\operatorname{Con}(S),
$$


but also


$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash \operatorname{Con}(S)\to\operatorname{Con}(T).
$$


But the second assertion is a non-provability statement about
$\mathrm{PA}$
. Proving such a statement seems to require some metatheory strong enough to prove the consistency of
$\mathrm{PA}$
. If my metatheory is so weak that it cannot even prove
$\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{PA})$
, how can it prove such a strictness statement?


For instance, people often say that


$$
\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"}
$$


has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
. I understand that


$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"})
\to
\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})
$$


is easy. But in what precise sense do we prove that the converse does not hold? Is the intended claim really something like

$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})\to
\operatorname{Con}\bigl(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{''there is an inaccessible cardinal''}\bigr)
$$


when set theorists say that one theory has "strictly greater consistency strength" than another, what are the implicit background assumptions?


So my questions are:




In the formal definition
$S<T$
, how is the "not conversely" part usually established?




What is the precise formal statement behind claims such as "
$\mathrm{ZFC}+$
an inaccessible cardinal has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
"?

I would appreciate a careful clarification of what is meant here.
answer:
All
strict
consistency strength results are indeed conditional.

Most obviously, they require the consistency of the "main" base theory itself: in order to establish
$\mathsf{PA}\not\vdash[\mathit{something}]$
, we need to be able to prove that
$\mathsf{PA}$
is consistent. So automatically our strictness-proving base theory has to increase a bit to
$\mathsf{PA}+\mathit{Con}(\mathsf{PA})$
in order to prove
any

$\mathsf{PA}$
-unprovability statements.

But it's even worse than that: strict comparisons are also conditional on the consistency of the systems (or at least the weaker of the two systems) in question themselves, which can be arbitrarily strong. If
$A,B$
are much stronger than
$\mathsf{PA}$
(and all three are consistent in the first place) then
$T:=\mathsf{PA}+\mathit{Con}(\mathsf{PA})+\neg\mathit{Con}(A)+\neg\mathit{Con}(B)$
is consistent and so even
$\mathsf{PA}+\mathit{Con}(\mathsf{PA})$
cannot possibly prove the strictness claim that
$\mathsf{PA}\not\vdash\mathit{Con}(A)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(B)$

(Why? Well, otherwise
$T$
would prove that statement too, being a stronger theory. But
$T$

also
proves that
$\mathsf{PA}$
is
$\Sigma_1$
-complete, and so
$T$
, believing the
$\Sigma_1$
statement
$\neg\mathit{Con}(A)$
to be true, would think that
$\mathsf{PA}$
must prove
$\mathit{Con}(A)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(B)$
vacuously.)

So to be fully precise, when we say "
$B$
has greater consistency strength than
$A$
" (and take
$\mathsf{PA}$
as our "standard" base theory) what we are claiming is:

$\mathsf{PA}\vdash\mathit{Con}(B)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(A)$
, and

$\mathsf{PA}+\mathit{Con}(\mathsf{PA})+\mathit{Con}(A)\vdash [\mathsf{PA}\not\vdash\mathit{Con}(A)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(B)]$
,

$\mathsf{PA}\vdash\mathit{Con}(B)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(A)$
, and

$\mathsf{PA}+\mathit{Con}(\mathsf{PA})+\mathit{Con}(A)\vdash [\mathsf{PA}\not\vdash\mathit{Con}(A)\rightarrow\mathit{Con}(B)]$
,

with the second bulletpoint implicitly involving some
arithmetization
of provability within
$\mathsf{PA}$
(or more broadly, "internalization" of provability inside whatever the main base theory is). But usually the subtleties around the second bulletpoint are ignored here.

question:
I am trying to understand the precise meaning of "greater consistency strength."

On Wikipedia's article on
equiconsistency
, I found the following formulation:

If
$T$
is consistent relative to
$S$
, but

$T$

has greater consistency strength than
$T$

I do not understand exactly what "is not known to be consistent relative to" means here. Is this only an epistemic statement about our current knowledge? If later someone proves the relative consistency of
$S$
from
$T$

$T$

I found another, more formal, definition in Joel David Hamkins' paper
Nonlinearity and illfoundedness in the hierarchy of large cardinal consistency strength

Definition 1. Theory
$S$
is below theory
$T$
in consistency strength, written

, if the implication
$\operatorname{Con}(T) \to \operatorname{Con}(S)$
is provable in a base theory, fixed for the purpose of this relation. The theory

is strictly below
$T$

This definition is much clearer, but then I am confused about how strictness is proved.

$$

but also

But the second assertion is a non-provability statement about
$\mathrm{PA}$

$\mathrm{PA}$

$\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{PA})$

For instance, people often say that


$$

$$


has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$

$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"})
\to
\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})
$$


is easy. But in what precise sense do we prove that the converse does not hold? Is the intended claim really something like

$$

$$


when set theorists say that one theory has "strictly greater consistency strength" than another, what are the implicit background assumptions?


So my questions are:




In the formal definition
$S<T$
, how is the "not conversely" part usually established?




What is the precise formal statement behind claims such as "
$\mathrm{ZFC}+$
an inaccessible cardinal has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
"?

I would appreciate a careful clarification of what is meant here.
answer:
Unfortunately it's not possible to avoid a certain amount of contingency here when talking about "strictly higher consistency strength".

If we fix
$\sf PA$
as the base theory, we can certainly just assume it is consistent (or moreover, we can assume it is sound), but that doesn't address the fundamental issue. We know we can prove
$\sf Con(ZFC+I)\to Con(ZFC)$
, but we don't know if we can prove
$\sf Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I).$
The obstacle isn't not knowing if
$\sf PA$
is consistent, it is not knowing if
$\sf ZFC $
is consistent.

Or to put it another way, if an inconsistency were proven tomorrow from
$\sf ZFC$
or some large cardinal theory, then we'd know its consistency strength is
$\bot$
(
$\sf PA$
or whatever base theory would then be able to prove
$\sf \lnot Con(T)$
), and so would be the consistency strength of all theories that we currently accept as strictly stronger. So the assertion that any two theories have a strict relationship is contingent on the "presumption of innocence" of the weaker of the two.

So then how do we come to the conclusion in practice that
$\sf ZFC + I$
is strictly stronger than
$\sf ZFC$
? Usually that comes through a stronger notion, e.g. we have
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)$
. So if in our base theory we had a proof of
$\sf Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I)$
, then we'd certainly have
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I),$
so
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC+I),$
and hence by the second incompleteness theorem,
$\sf \lnot Con(ZFC+I)$
(and hence by our assumption,
$\sf \lnot Con(ZFC)$
). So we have basically have all the evidence we can muster against the proposition that
$\sf PA \vdash Con(ZFC) \to Con(ZFC + I)$
given the above considerations.

(Note also basic triviality considerations surrounding this are why we want to use a base theory
weaker
than the ones we care about. Of course
$\sf ZFC+ I\vdash Con(PA) \to Con(ZFC)$
since
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)$
, but that's not really that interesting, is it?)

but not conversely; and the theories are equiconsistent, written
$S\equiv T$
, when
$S\leq T$
and
$T\leq S$
.

This definition is much clearer, but then I am confused about how strictness is proved.

For example, suppose the base theory is
$\mathrm{PA}$
, and suppose I want to reason only finitistically or in a very weak metatheory. To prove
$S<T$
, I would need to prove not only

$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(T)\to\operatorname{Con}(S),
$$


but also


$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash \operatorname{Con}(S)\to\operatorname{Con}(T).
$$


But the second assertion is a non-provability statement about
$\mathrm{PA}$
. Proving such a statement seems to require some metatheory strong enough to prove the consistency of
$\mathrm{PA}$
. If my metatheory is so weak that it cannot even prove
$\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{PA})$
, how can it prove such a strictness statement?


For instance, people often say that


$$
\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"}
$$


has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
. I understand that


$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"})
\to
\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})
$$


is easy. But in what precise sense do we prove that the converse does not hold? Is the intended claim really something like

$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})\to
\operatorname{Con}\bigl(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{''there is an inaccessible cardinal''}\bigr)
$$


when set theorists say that one theory has "strictly greater consistency strength" than another, what are the implicit background assumptions?


So my questions are:




In the formal definition
$S<T$
, how is the "not conversely" part usually established?




What is the precise formal statement behind claims such as "
$\mathrm{ZFC}+$
an inaccessible cardinal has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
"?

I would appreciate a careful clarification of what is meant here.

Unfortunately it's not possible to avoid a certain amount of contingency here when talking about "strictly higher consistency strength".

If we fix
$\sf PA$
as the base theory, we can certainly just assume it is consistent (or moreover, we can assume it is sound), but that doesn't address the fundamental issue. We know we can prove
$\sf Con(ZFC+I)\to Con(ZFC)$
, but we don't know if we can prove
$\sf Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I).$
The obstacle isn't not knowing if
$\sf PA$
is consistent, it is not knowing if
$\sf ZFC $
is consistent.

Or to put it another way, if an inconsistency were proven tomorrow from
$\sf ZFC$
or some large cardinal theory, then we'd know its consistency strength is
$\bot$
(
$\sf PA$
or whatever base theory would then be able to prove
$\sf \lnot Con(T)$
), and so would be the consistency strength of all theories that we currently accept as strictly stronger. So the assertion that any two theories have a strict relationship is contingent on the "presumption of innocence" of the weaker of the two.

So then how do we come to the conclusion in practice that
$\sf ZFC + I$
is strictly stronger than
$\sf ZFC$
? Usually that comes through a stronger notion, e.g. we have
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)$
. So if in our base theory we had a proof of
$\sf Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I)$
, then we'd certainly have
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)\to Con(ZFC+I),$
so
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC+I),$
and hence by the second incompleteness theorem,
$\sf \lnot Con(ZFC+I)$
(and hence by our assumption,
$\sf \lnot Con(ZFC)$
). So we have basically have all the evidence we can muster against the proposition that
$\sf PA \vdash Con(ZFC) \to Con(ZFC + I)$
given the above considerations.

(Note also basic triviality considerations surrounding this are why we want to use a base theory
weaker
than the ones we care about. Of course
$\sf ZFC+ I\vdash Con(PA) \to Con(ZFC)$
since
$\sf ZFC + I \vdash Con(ZFC)$
, but that's not really that interesting, is it?)

title: What exactly does “strictly greater consistency strength” mean?
question:
I am trying to understand the precise meaning of "greater consistency strength."

On Wikipedia's article on
equiconsistency
, I found the following formulation:

If
$T$
is consistent relative to
$S$
, but
$S$
is not known to be consistent relative to
$T$
, then we say that
$S$
has greater consistency strength than
$T$
.

I do not understand exactly what "is not known to be consistent relative to" means here. Is this only an epistemic statement about our current knowledge? If later someone proves the relative consistency of
$S$
from
$T$
, would the statement "
$S$
has greater consistency strength than
$T$
" change its truth value? That seems strange to me.

I found another, more formal, definition in Joel David Hamkins' paper
Nonlinearity and illfoundedness in the hierarchy of large cardinal consistency strength
:

Definition 1. Theory
$S$
is below theory
$T$
in consistency strength, written
$S \leq T$
, if the implication
$\operatorname{Con}(T) \to \operatorname{Con}(S)$
is provable in a base theory, fixed for the purpose of this relation. The theory
$S$
is strictly below
$T$
, written
$S<T$
, if
$S\leq T$
but not conversely; and the theories are equiconsistent, written
$S\equiv T$
, when
$S\leq T$
and
$T\leq S$
.

This definition is much clearer, but then I am confused about how strictness is proved.

For example, suppose the base theory is
$\mathrm{PA}$
, and suppose I want to reason only finitistically or in a very weak metatheory. To prove
$S<T$
, I would need to prove not only

$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(T)\to\operatorname{Con}(S),
$$


but also


$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash \operatorname{Con}(S)\to\operatorname{Con}(T).
$$


But the second assertion is a non-provability statement about
$\mathrm{PA}$
. Proving such a statement seems to require some metatheory strong enough to prove the consistency of
$\mathrm{PA}$
. If my metatheory is so weak that it cannot even prove
$\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{PA})$
, how can it prove such a strictness statement?


For instance, people often say that


$$
\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"}
$$


has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
. I understand that


$$
\mathrm{PA}\vdash \operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{"there is an inaccessible cardinal"})
\to
\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})
$$


is easy. But in what precise sense do we prove that the converse does not hold? Is the intended claim really something like

$$
\mathrm{PA}\nvdash\operatorname{Con}(\mathrm{ZFC})\to
\operatorname{Con}\bigl(\mathrm{ZFC}+\text{''there is an inaccessible cardinal''}\bigr)
$$


when set theorists say that one theory has "strictly greater consistency strength" than another, what are the implicit background assumptions?


So my questions are:




In the formal definition
$S<T$
, how is the "not conversely" part usually established?




What is the precise formal statement behind claims such as "
$\mathrm{ZFC}+$
an inaccessible cardinal has strictly greater consistency strength than
$\mathrm{ZFC}$
"?

I would appreciate a careful clarification of what is meant here.
answer:
You're right about the Wikipedia definition. The use of "known" makes it an informal definition, unless a mathematical definition of "known" has been given.

As for the definition you quoted from Joel Hamkins, the first thing I should point out is that the fixed base theory mentioned there is intended to be what you call the metatheory. So in your example, taking your metatheory to be finitistic and taking Joel's base theory to be PA is not the sort of thing that Joel intended.

Let me try to formalize and discuss Joel's definition. Assume we are given two theories,
$S$
and
$T$
, with computable axiomatizations. (Computable axiomatizations allow us to express Con(
$S$
) and Con(
$T$
) in weak languages like arithmetic.) Assume we are also given a consistent (base or meta-)theory
$B$
, in which Con(
$S$
) and Con(
$T$
) can be expressed. Then
$S$
is below
$T$
in consistency strength if
$B$
proves that Con(
$T$
) implies Con(
$S$
), and strictly below if, in addition,
$B$
doesn't prove the converse, that Con(
$S$
) implies Con(
$T$
). Your question is how does one establish that "in addition" part.

To clear out some underbrush, notice that, if
$B$
proves that
$S$
is inconsistent, then it also proves that Con(
$S$
) implies (anything you want, in particular) Con(
$T$
), and so
$S$
can't be strictly below anything in consistency strength. That is,
$S$
is a maximal element in the consistency strength pre-ordering. [Digression: In fact, it's a maximum element; all other theories are below it.]

Next, there's a weird case:
$S$
is inconsistent but
$B$
can't prove that. I'm going to ignore this case for two reasons: (1)
$B$
would have to be absurdly weak. It couldn't express even finitary reasoning, because, if
$S$
is inconsistent, you could exhibit a proof in
$S$
of
$\bot$
and then finitarily check that it is a proof. Once
$B$
gets too weak for that, it's difficult to figure out what the sentence Con(
$S$
) should be; two trivially equivalent formulations might not be
$B$
-provably equivalent. (2) I don't know enough about such weak theories to say anything intelligent here.

So from now on, I'll assume that
$S$
is consistent and
$B$
doesn't prove otherwise. Returning to the problem of proving something of the form
$B\not\vdash\text{ Con}(S)\implies \text{ Con}(T)$
, I'll first rewrite it in the form
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S)\}\not\vdash\text{ Con}(T)$
.

I don't know any more to say in this generality, but I can say more with an additional assumption about
$B$
, namely that
$B$
contains "enough arithmetic" in the sense usually assumed for Gödel's 2nd incompleteness theorem. (Formally,
$B$
should satisfy the Hilbert-Bernays derivability conditions.) For example,
$B$
could be PA, but far weaker theories would suffice. Then
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S\}$
can't prove its own consistency. So we could show that
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S)\}\not\vdash\text{ Con}(T)$
by proving in
$B$
that
$\text{Con}(T)$
implies the consistency of
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S)\}$
.

Usually (as far as I know) this is done by giving an interpretation of
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S)\}$
in
$T$
. In some set-theoretic cases, the interpretation is Boolean-valued, and in many cases it's an interpretation in an extension of
$T$
by definitions, but even then, the consistency of
$T$
implies that of
$B\cup\{\text{Con}(S)\}$
by a finitary argument, formalizable in any reasonable
$B$
.

For example, if
$S$
is ZFC and
$T$
is ZFC+"There exists an inaccessible cardinal," and if I take
$B$
to be ZFC, then the desired interpretation of ZFC+Con(ZFC) in
$T$
is given by interpreting "set" in the former as "set of rank below the first inaccessible" in the latter. This gives us that ZFC can't prove that Con(ZFC) implies Con(ZFC+inaccessible). (Of course if ZFC can't prove this implication, then neither can weaker theories like PA.)

title: Asymptotic behavior of $\varphi(k\#) \over k\#$
question:
While studying certain algorithms involving wheel factorization, I encountered the function
$\varphi(k\#) / k\#$
, where
$k$
is a positive integer,
$\varphi$
is Euler's totient function, and
$k\#$
is the primorial of
$k$
(the product of the first
$k$
primes). I've computed the first few values of this function and collected the results in this table:

$k$

$k\#$

$\varphi(k\#)$

$\varphi(k\#)/k\#$
(approximate)

1

2

1

0.5000

2

6

2

0.3333

3

30

8

0.2667

4

210

48

0.2286

5

2310

480

0.2078

6

30030

5760

0.1918

7

510510

92160

0.1805

8

9699690

1658880

0.1710

I stopped at
$k=8$
, as for bigger
$k$
s, the numbers involved begin to get quite enormous.

It is known
that
$\varphi(k\#) / k\# < \varphi(m) / m$
for any integer
$m < k\#$
. Since
$k\#$
strictly increases with
$k$
, this means that
$\varphi(k\#) / k\#$
is strictly decreasing. Based on this and an informal inspection of the computed values,
I hypothesize that
$\varphi(k\#) \over k\#$
is
$\Theta(k^{-\alpha})$
for some positive real number
$\alpha$
.
This would seem to be supported by regression analysis: a power regression of the eight
$(k, {\varphi(k\#) \over k\#})$
pairs listed in the table places
$\alpha$
at approximately 0.536199 with
$R^{2}=0.9963$
.

Is this hypothesis true? If so, what is
$\alpha$
?
answer:
I'm just going to expand the hints in the comments. Let
$p_1,p_2,\cdots$
be the sequence of primes. Then

$$\varphi(p_1\cdots p_k)=\prod_{i\leq k}(p_i-1)$$

and so

$$\frac{\varphi(k\#)}{k\#}=\prod_{i\leq k}\left(1-\frac1{p_i}\right)$$

by
Mertens' third
theorem we have

$$\frac{\varphi(k\#)}{k\#}\sim\frac{e^{-\gamma}}{\log p_k}$$

and since
$p_k\sim k\log k$
we therefore have

$$\frac{\varphi(k\#)}{k\#}\sim\frac{e^{-\gamma}}{\log k}$$

In particular, it decays logarithmically, not polynomially.
Furthermore, by
this answer
together with standard prime number estimates we can get higher order terms, for instance

$$\frac{\varphi(k\#)}{k\#}=\frac{e^{-\gamma}}{\log k}-\frac{e^{-\gamma}\log\log k}{\log^2 k}+O\left(\frac{(\log\log k)^2}{\log^3 k}\right)$$

and were one to use the sharpest estimates for Mertens' theorem and the prime number theorem one could extract a few more terms

Improve this question

The acute angle of a right triangle are complementary
answer:
This is kind of trivial but anyway,

let the non right angles of the triangle be
$A$
and
$B$
.

Since the triangle is right-angled, one angle is
$90^\circ$
, hence the other two angles must be acute.

By the angle sum property of a triangle,

$$
A+B+90^\circ=180^\circ.
$$

Hence,

$$
A+B=90^\circ.
$$

Therefore, the acute angles of a right triangle are complementary.

title: How can one modify only the diagonal entries of a matrix so that one eigenvector becomes orthogonal to a given vector?
question:
Let
$A=[a_{ij}]\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$
be a real symmetric positive definite matrix, and let
$b\in\mathbb{R}^n$
be a given vector. Assume that no eigenvector of
$A$
is orthogonal to
$b$
, i.e., for every eigenvector
$v$
of
$A$
,

$$ \langle v,b\rangle \neq 0. $$


I would like to modify
$A$
only by changing its diagonal entries. Equivalently, I want to find a diagonal matrix
$D\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$
such that

$ A_1 = A + D $

has at least one eigenvector
$v_1$
satisfying

$$ \langle v_1,b\rangle=0. $$


There's no requirement that
$A_1$
remain positive definite.


Is it always possible to choose such a diagonal perturbation
$D$
? If so, how can one construct it?
answer:
This is not always possible if
$b$
is a nonzero multiple of some standard basis vector. For instance, let
$A=\pmatrix{\ast&1\\ 1&\ast}$
and
$b=\pmatrix{1\\ 0}$
. For every diagonal matrix
$D$
, the sum
$A+D$
is in the same form as
$A$
. Hence
$\{b\}^\perp$
is the linear span of
$\pmatrix{0\\ 1}$
and no nonzero vector in
$\{b\}^\perp$
is an eigenvector of
$A$
or
$A+D$
. In other words, no eigenvector of
$A$
or
$A+D$
is orthogonal to
$b$
.

(More generally, if
$n\ge2$
and
$A\in M_n(\mathbb R)$
is a symmetric tridiagonal matrix whose first superdiagonal is entrywise nonzero, then no eigenvector of
$A$
or
$A+D$
is orthogonal to
$b=(1,0,\ldots,0)^T$
.)

However, what you ask for is possible when
$b$
has at least two nonzero elements. In this case, there always exists some entrywise nonzero vector
$v\perp b$
. For instance, if
$n=2$
and
$b=(b_1,b_2)$
is entrywise nonzero, we may take
$v=(b_2,-b_1)$
; if
$n\ge3$
and
$b_1$
and
$b_2$
are nonzero, we may take

$$
v_3=\cdots=v_n=1,
\quad v_2\not\in\left\{0,-\frac{b_3+\cdots+b_n}{b_2}\right\},
\quad v_1=-\frac{b_2v_2+b_3+\cdots+b_n}{b_1}.
$$

Pick any
$\lambda\in\mathbb R$
, set
$w=(\lambda I-A)v$
and
$D=\operatorname{diag}\left(\frac{w_1}{v_1},\ldots,\frac{w_n}{v_n}\right)$
. Then
$(A+D)v=Av+w=\lambda v$
.

title: Finding the closed form of spectral density for a locally-periodic/quasi-periodic Gaussian process kernel
question:
Say we have two 1-dimensional covariance functions (Gaussian process kernels) defined over
$\tau$
, which is the distance between two points (i.e.
$\tau = |x - x'|$
. First, we have a squared-exponential kernel with scale
$\alpha_\text{sqexp}$
and length-scale
$\ell_\text{sqexp}$
:

$$
k_\text{sqexp}(\tau) = \alpha_\text{sqexp}\exp\left(\frac{-\tau^2}{2\ell_\text{sqexp}^2}\right),
$$

and second, a periodic kernel with scale
$\alpha_\text{per}$
, length-scale
$\ell_\text{per}$
and period
$P$
:

$$
k_\text{per}(\tau) = \alpha_\text{per} \exp\left(-\frac{2}{\ell_\text{per}^2}\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi\tau}{P}\right)\right)
$$

If we take the product of the two kernels and simplify, we get the following locally-periodic (sometimes called quasi-periodic) kernel:

$$
k_\text{lp}(\tau)=\alpha \exp\left(\frac{-\tau^2}{2\ell_\text{sqexp}^2} -\frac{2}{\ell_\text{per}^2}\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi\tau}{P}\right) \right)
$$

Noting that the two scaling terms have been combined here with
$\alpha=\alpha_\text{sqexp}\alpha_\text{per}$
.


Following the approach described in
Riutort-Mayol 2023
, I know that the spectral density of our (stationary) covariance function is its Fourier transform:

$$
s(\omega) = \int k(\tau ) e^{-2\pi i\omega\tau} \,\textrm{d}\tau
$$

If this can be derived, then the
Hilbert Space Gaussian process (HSGP) approximation
could be used with this locally periodic kernel (which is why I would love to know!)


Can a closed form be derived for the spectral density
$s(\omega)$
? If so, how? I have made some attempts but my background in this field is fairly limited.
answer:
With simpler notations and using
$2\sin^2 x=1-\cos 2x$
your second stationary process has the covariance
$$Be^{b(e^{i\tau}+e^{-i\tau})/2}=\sum_{-\infty}^{\infty}BJ_n(b)e^{in\tau}$$
where
$J_n(b)$
are the Bessel coefficients (See Watson , a Treatise of the Theory of Bessel Functions, page 15). This means that the spectral measure of your second process is discrete and made  of the following infinite sum of Dirac measures
$$\sum_{-\infty}^{\infty}BJ_n(b)
\delta_n(dx)$$
since
$\int_{\infty}^{\infty}e^{i\tau x}\delta_a(dx)=e^{ia\tau}.$

The first process has covariance
$Ae^{-\tau^2/2\sigma^2}/(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi})$
and therefore has spectral measure
$Ae^{-\sigma^2x^2/2}dx.$
As a consequence, the new stationary Gaussian process that you get by taking the product of the  two covariances has spectral measure obtained by the convolution of the two spectral measures (fortunately, they are bounded, so this convolution makes sense). Now the convolution of
$\delta_n$
and of
$e^{-\sigma^2x^2/2}$
is nothing but

$e^{-\sigma^2(x-n)^2/2}.$
As a result the new stationary process has the spectral density
$$ \sum_{-\infty}^{\infty}ABJ_n(b)e^{-\sigma^2(x-n)^2/2}$$

title: If a function applied to partition parts always results in a partition, is that function is merely renaming elements?
question:
IF when a function is applied to all the parts in a partition,
the combined results of applying that function always form partition, THEN is that function is merely a renaming of the elements in the parts of the partition? (This assumes that the function has the same domain and codomain, but I think generalizations exist.) Is there a known theorem that I could appeal to for this or a theorem of which this is a corollary?

Stated more formally, if
$\{f(q) \mid q \in P\}$
is always a partition (and none of the
$f(q)$
overlap) when
$P$
is a partition, then does there exist a bijective function
$g$
such that
$\{f(q) \mid q \in P\}$
=
$\{\{g(x) \mid x \in q\} \mid q \in P\}$
?

For example, if
$f$
might be
$f(\emptyset)=\emptyset$
,
$f(\{a\})=\{b\}$
,
$f(\{b\})=\{a\}$
,
$f(\{a, b\})=\{a,b\}$
. Then
$g$
would be
$g(a)=b$
,
$g(b)=a$
.

Generalizations:

Does this still hold if we allow the domain and codomain of
$f$
to differ (e.g.,
$f : \wp(M) \rightarrow \wp(N)$
)?

(Of course,
$g$
would have to be
$M \rightarrow \wp(N)$
instead of
$M \rightarrow N$
and outputs of
$g$
would be both non-overlapping and cover N (i.e., both
$\forall x,y\in M.g(x) \cap g(y) = \emptyset$
and
$\bigcup_{x\in M}g(x) = N$
).)

Does this still hold if
$f$
is a partial function, and we require results of applying
$f$
to form a partition only when
$f$
is defined on all the input partition parts. For example, if
$f$
might be
$f(\emptyset)=\emptyset$
,
$f(\{a\})=\{b\}$
,
$f(\{b\})=undefined$
,
$f(\{a, b\})=\{a,b\}$
. Then
$g$
would be
$g(a)=b$
,
$g(b)=a$
.

Update:

Based on the example from Jayanth R Varma, I have found the following counter example to
$g$
being a simple renaming. However, if we let
$g$
be a multivalued-function as in the first bullet of the generalzation above, then I think the theorem still applies. For example, the generalized
$g$
that still works with this counter example is
$g(a)=\{a,b\}$
,
$g(b)=\emptyset$
,
$g(c)=\{c\}$
,
$g(d)=\{d\}$
.

Counter example: Let
$f$
be the following mapping where the four columns of input represent
$a$
,
$b$
,
$c$
, and
$d$
. E.g. "0110=>0010" means
$f(\{b,c\})=\{c\}$
.

1000=>1100
0100=>0000
0010=>0010
0001=>0001

0111=>0011
1011=>1111
1101=>1101
1110=>1110

1100=>1100
0110=>0010
0011=>0011
1001=>1101

1010=>1110
0101=>0001
0000=>0000
1111=>1111

```
answer:
Updated answer to OP's revised question

The answer is YES (as shown below) if we allow
$g$
to be multi-valued (as you have stated) and also do not require
$g$
to be injective (probably implicit in your statement). In other words,
$g$
may map an element of its domain to multiple elements of the codomain (multi-value) and may map multiple elements of its domain to the same element of the codomain (non injective).

First of all the function
$f$
is necessarily injective because if
$f(q_1)=f(q_2)$
, then
$P'=\{f(q) \mid q \in P\}$
would contain the same part
$f(q_1)$
twice and this violate the requirement that
$P'$
is a partition, since any two parts of a partition should have an empty intersection.

This implies that
$P$
and
$P'$
have the same number of parts. We then construct the multi-valued function
$g$
on each part separately. In other words, we first construct
$g_{\mid q}: q \to f(q)$
(the restriction of
$g$
to the subset
$q$
of the domain) for each
$q$
and then define
$g$
to be the union of all these restrictions:
$g(x) = g_{\mid q(x)}(x)$
where
$q(x)$
is the (unique) part of
$P$
that contains
$x$
.

So consider an arbitrary
$q$
and
$f(q)$
. Let the cardinalities of these two sets be
$m_q$
and
$n_q$
respectively and let
$k_q = \min(m_q, n_q)-1$
. Since
$P$
and
$P'$
are partitions, neither
$q$
nor
$f(q)$
can be empty and so
$k_q \ge 0$
. We consider two cases:

Case 1:
$k_q=0$

In this case, at least one of
$q$
or
$f(q)$
is a singleton.

If
$q$
is the singleton
$\{x_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x_0)=f(q)$
. This will be a multi-value unless
$f(q)$
also happens to be a singleton

If
$f(q)$
is the singleton
$\{y_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x)=y_0 \, \forall x \in q$
. This function
$g_{\mid q}$
will not be injective unless
$q$
also happens to be a singleton.

If
$q$
is the singleton
$\{x_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x_0)=f(q)$
. This will be a multi-value unless
$f(q)$
also happens to be a singleton

If
$f(q)$
is the singleton
$\{y_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x)=y_0 \, \forall x \in q$
. This function
$g_{\mid q}$
will not be injective unless
$q$
also happens to be a singleton.

Case 2:
$k_q>0$

In this case we partition
$q$
into two sets
$q_1$
containing
$k_q$
elements and
$q_2$
containing
$m_q-k_q$
elements (this can be done in any arbitrary way). Similarly, we partition
$f(q)$
into two sets
$f(q)_1$
containing
$k_q$
elements and
$f(q)_2$
containing
$n_q-k_q$
elements.

The two sets
$q_1$
and
$f(q)_1$
have the same number
$k_q$
of elements and therefore we can let the restriction of
$g$
to
$q_1$

$\left(g_{\mid q_1}\right)$
be an arbitrary bijection between the two sets
$q_1$
and
$f(q)_1$
.

It remains to define the restriction of
$g$
to
$q_2$

$\left(g_{\mid q_2}\right)$
. But this reduces to
Case 1
above. Either
$q_2$
or
$f(q)_2$
is a singleton. If
$q_2$
contains only
$x_0$
we let
$g$
map
$x_0$
to the (multi-value)
$f(q)_2$
. If
$f(q)_2$
contains only
$y_0$
we let
$g$
map all elements of
$q_2$
to
$y_0$
.

Generalizations

Different domain and codomain:
The proof above did not assume anywhere that the domain and codomain are the same or that they have the same cardinality. So the answer to this question is also YES.

Partial function:
Let
$P_0 = \{q \mid f(q) \text{ is defined}\}$
and let
$M_0=\bigcup_{q \in P_0}q$
. Observe that
$P_0$
is a partition of
$M_0$
and that
$f_{\mid P_0}$
is a total function. We can now apply the proof for total functions.

Different domain and codomain:
The proof above did not assume anywhere that the domain and codomain are the same or that they have the same cardinality. So the answer to this question is also YES.

Partial function:
Let
$P_0 = \{q \mid f(q) \text{ is defined}\}$
and let
$M_0=\bigcup_{q \in P_0}q$
. Observe that
$P_0$
is a partition of
$M_0$
and that
$f_{\mid P_0}$
is a total function. We can now apply the proof for total functions.

Original Answer to OP's original question

No this is not true because the cardinalities of the parts of the partition
$P'$
created by
$f$
can differ from the cardinalities of the parts of the original partition
$P$
.

For each
$q$
,
$\{g(x) \mid x \in q\}$
will give a part that has the same cardinality as
$q$
because
$g$
is a bijection. On the other hand,
$f(q)$
might not have the same cardinality as
$q$
and in fact, there may be a
$q\ast$
such that
$\{f(q) \mid q \in P\}$
does not contain a single part with the same cardinality as
$q\ast$
.

For example let
$P$
be the partition of
$\{a, b, c, d\}$
consisting of
$\{a\}$
and
$\{b, c, d\}$
with cardinalities
$1$
and
$3$
respectively. The problem is that
$f$
may create a partition whose parts have cardinalities of
$2$
and
$2$
. For example
$f(\{a\})=\{a,b\}$
and
$f(\{b, c, d\})= \{c, d\}$
. The cardinalities of the parts of
$P$
are
$\{1,3\}$
while the cardinalities of the parts of
$P'$
are
$\{2,2\}$
.

If the two multisets of cardinalities are equal, then a bijection
$g$
can always be found.
```

If
$f(q)$
is the singleton
$\{y_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x)=y_0 \, \forall x \in q$
. This function
$g_{\mid q}$
will not be injective unless
$q$
also happens to be a singleton.

If
$q$
is the singleton
$\{x_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x_0)=f(q)$
. This will be a multi-value unless
$f(q)$
also happens to be a singleton

If
$f(q)$
is the singleton
$\{y_0\}$
, we define
$g_{\mid q}(x)=y_0 \, \forall x \in q$
. This function
$g_{\mid q}$
will not be injective unless
$q$
also happens to be a singleton.

Case 2:
$k_q>0$

In this case we partition
$q$
into two sets
$q_1$
containing
$k_q$
elements and
$q_2$
containing
$m_q-k_q$
elements (this can be done in any arbitrary way). Similarly, we partition
$f(q)$
into two sets
$f(q)_1$
containing
$k_q$
elements and
$f(q)_2$
containing
$n_q-k_q$
elements.

The two sets
$q_1$
and
$f(q)_1$
have the same number
$k_q$
of elements and therefore we can let the restriction of
$g$
to
$q_1$

$\left(g_{\mid q_1}\right)$
be an arbitrary bijection between the two sets
$q_1$
and
$f(q)_1$
.

It remains to define the restriction of
$g$
to
$q_2$

$\left(g_{\mid q_2}\right)$
. But this reduces to
Case 1
above. Either
$q_2$
or
$f(q)_2$
is a singleton. If
$q_2$
contains only
$x_0$
we let
$g$
map
$x_0$
to the (multi-value)
$f(q)_2$
. If
$f(q)_2$
contains only
$y_0$
we let
$g$
map all elements of
$q_2$
to
$y_0$
.

Generalizations

Different domain and codomain:
The proof above did not assume anywhere that the domain and codomain are the same or that they have the same cardinality. So the answer to this question is also YES.

Partial function:
Let
$P_0 = \{q \mid f(q) \text{ is defined}\}$
and let
$M_0=\bigcup_{q \in P_0}q$
. Observe that
$P_0$
is a partition of
$M_0$
and that
$f_{\mid P_0}$
is a total function. We can now apply the proof for total functions.

Different domain and codomain:
The proof above did not assume anywhere that the domain and codomain are the same or that they have the same cardinality. So the answer to this question is also YES.

Partial function:
Let
$P_0 = \{q \mid f(q) \text{ is defined}\}$
and let
$M_0=\bigcup_{q \in P_0}q$
. Observe that
$P_0$
is a partition of
$M_0$
and that
$f_{\mid P_0}$
is a total function. We can now apply the proof for total functions.

Original Answer to OP's original question

No this is not true because the cardinalities of the parts of the partition
$P'$
created by
$f$
can differ from the cardinalities of the parts of the original partition
$P$
.

For each
$q$
,
$\{g(x) \mid x \in q\}$
will give a part that has the same cardinality as
$q$
because
$g$
is a bijection. On the other hand,
$f(q)$
might not have the same cardinality as
$q$
and in fact, there may be a
$q\ast$
such that
$\{f(q) \mid q \in P\}$
does not contain a single part with the same cardinality as
$q\ast$
.

For example let
$P$
be the partition of
$\{a, b, c, d\}$
consisting of
$\{a\}$
and
$\{b, c, d\}$
with cardinalities
$1$
and
$3$
respectively. The problem is that
$f$
may create a partition whose parts have cardinalities of
$2$
and
$2$
. For example
$f(\{a\})=\{a,b\}$
and
$f(\{b, c, d\})= \{c, d\}$
. The cardinalities of the parts of
$P$
are
$\{1,3\}$
while the cardinalities of the parts of
$P'$
are
$\{2,2\}$
.

If the two multisets of cardinalities are equal, then a bijection
$g$
can always be found.

title: How can we get integer solution of the equation $W^4+X^4=Y^4+Z^4=P^4+Q^4$?
question:
It is already known to us that Euler had solved
$W^{4}$
+
$X^{4}$
=
$Y^{4}$
+
$Z^{4}$
,
$(W, X, Y, Z)=(133, 134, 158, 59)$
. There exists parametric solution to get various rational (particular integer) solutions.
I am further interested to solve whether there exists a number which can expressed in three or many ways solution like (here in particular three)
$W^4+X^4=Y^4+Z^4=P^4+Q^4$
. I can consider it first rearrange three simultaneous equation

$W^{4}-Y^{4}=Z^{4}-X^{4}\tag1$

$W^{4}-P^{4}=Q^{4}-X^{4}\tag2$

$Y^{4}-P^{4}=Q^{4}-Z^{4}\tag3$

Put
$W=(a+b)$
,
$Y=(a-b)$
,
$Z=(c+d)$
,
$X=(c-d)$
,
$P=(a-d)$
,
$Q=(b+c)$
Now we will make a little bit reduction of this complex type simultaneous equation system. How can I solve this type of equation ?
answer:
I. Sum

The smallest integer
$M$
that is the
sum
of two
$4$
th powers in
more than one way
is
$M=635318657$
,

$$M=59^4+158^4=133^4+134^4=635318657$$

The list of the first 30000
$M$
is given by
OEIS A018786
. Any
$M$
that is a sum in
three
ways,

$$M=A^4+B^4=C^4+D^4=E^4+F^4\quad$$

would be notable. But there is
NO
mention of any such
$M$
so, as far as we know, the
the equation above has no solution
.

II. Difference

On the other hand, there are
infinitely
many primitive solutions to,

$$N=A^4-B^4=C^4-D^4=E^4-F^4\quad$$

or an integer
$N$
that is the
difference
of two
$4$
th powers in
more than
$n$
ways
, even
$n=3$
. It was discussed in "
Quartic Diophantine Chains
" (2007) by Choudhry and Wroblewski. The smallest for
$n=3$
ways, given in this
2016 MSE post
is,

$$N = 4860992489864937000960\approx 4\times10^{21}$$

hence,

$$N=335084^4-296668^4 =265076^4-93436^4 = 264047^4-1169^4$$

More solutions can be found in this recent
2026 MSE post
.

title: How can we get integer solution of the equation $W^4+X^4=Y^4+Z^4=P^4+Q^4$?
question:
It is already known to us that Euler had solved
$W^{4}$
+
$X^{4}$
=
$Y^{4}$
+
$Z^{4}$
,
$(W, X, Y, Z)=(133, 134, 158, 59)$
. There exists parametric solution to get various rational (particular integer) solutions.
I am further interested to solve whether there exists a number which can expressed in three or many ways solution like (here in particular three)
$W^4+X^4=Y^4+Z^4=P^4+Q^4$
. I can consider it first rearrange three simultaneous equation

$W^{4}-Y^{4}=Z^{4}-X^{4}\tag1$

$W^{4}-P^{4}=Q^{4}-X^{4}\tag2$

$Y^{4}-P^{4}=Q^{4}-Z^{4}\tag3$

Put
$W=(a+b)$
,
$Y=(a-b)$
,
$Z=(c+d)$
,
$X=(c-d)$
,
$P=(a-d)$
,
$Q=(b+c)$
Now we will make a little bit reduction of this complex type simultaneous equation system. How can I solve this type of equation ?
answer:
I think it is enough to test whether the proposed substitution can keep the three sums equal without forcing two of the pairs to be the same in disguise.

From

$$W^4+X^4=Y^4+Z^4$$

we get

$$(a+b)^4-(a-b)^4=(c+d)^4-(c-d)^4.$$

After expansion this becomes

$$ab(a^2+b^2)=cd(c^2+d^2).$$

Now compare the first and third pairs. The needed condition is

$$(a+b)^4-(a-d)^4=(b+c)^4-(c-d)^4.$$

This factors exactly as

$$\boxed{(a-c)(b+d)\bigl(2a^2+3ab+2ac-3ad+2b^2+3bc-2bd+2c^2-3cd+2d^2\bigr)=0.}$$

The last factor is never negative. Indeed, putting

$$u=a+b,\qquad v=c-a,\qquad w=d-a,$$

it becomes a sum of squares:

$$2u^2+3uv-2uw+2v^2-3vw+2w^2.$$

So it can vanish only in the collapsed case. Hence every solution coming from this substitution must satisfy either

$$a=c,\qquad\text{or}\qquad b=-d,$$

or else fall into the collapsed case.

If
$a=c$
, then the first equality forces
$b=d$
, apart from the trivial zero collapse. Thus the two pairs are only the same pair written again.

If
$b=-d$
, then the first equality forces
$a=-c$
, apart from the same trivial collapse. Again the equality is only a sign change or a repetition.

Therefore the substitution does not produce a genuine three-way representation. It only gives repeated, signed, or degenerate versions of an already existing equality. Thus this method cannot solve the original problem.

Hope this is useful!

title: Polynomial P(x) such that every tangent intersects P at least twice
question:
I'm doing math problems for fun. Currently stuck on this problem. My most urgent questions are listed first. Then i'll sketch what I've thought of so far. In the end, I list a few options that I suspect might be resourceful directions to work out. Would love feedback and ideas for solving this.

Open questions in this process

Given
$i > 2$
, how do I find values of
$\alpha \neq -1$
so that
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
is satisfied?

Proof that every polynomial P(x) of odd degree has one or more inflection points, of which there is at least one inflection point in which the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else.

Main idea

Goal is to find (or disprove the existence of) polynomials
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
such that any tangent line intersects
$P$
at least twice. In other words, the following equation must have multiple distinct real roots for every
$n \in$
ℝ:

$\hspace{2em} P(x)-L_n(x) = 0$

Here
$L_n(x)$
is the tangent to
$P$
in
$(n,P(n))$
, which we can write as
$L_n(x) = P'(n)x + P(n) - nP'(n)$
. This changes the equation above to

$\hspace{2em} \space \space P(x) - P'(n)x - P(n) + nP'(n) = 0$

Rewriting using
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i - (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space x  - \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i n^i + (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space n = 0$

which is equivalent to

(I)
$\hspace{1em} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i (x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)) = 0$

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
:

In what follows we will focus on the polynomial inside the summands. The goal is find roots of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
. For convenience we call this polynomial
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Because the tangent in
$(n,P(n))$
intersects
$P(x)$
for
$x=n$
, we know that
$z_{n,i}(n)=0$
. This means we can factor out
$(x-n)$
and rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as follows:

$\hspace{2em}  z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$

To determine the value of the
$b_j$
's we can rewrite
$(x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$
by taking the
$(x-n)$
inside the summand and reordering the terms according to the powers of
$x$
:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^{j+1} - n b_j x^j = b_{i-1} x^i + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} x^j (-nb_j + b_{j-1})$

Comparing the corresponding coefficients of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
gives, using
$b_{-1} = 0$
,

$ b_{-1} -nb_0 =  n^i(i-1)$

$ b_0 - nb_1 = -in^{i-1}$

$ b_j - n b_{j+1} = 0 $
for
$0 < j < i-1$

$ b_{i-1} = 1$

This nicely reduces to
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
for all
$0 < j \leq i-1$
. Thus we have successfully factored out
$(x-n)$
, leaving us with
$z_{n,i}(x)= (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j $
.

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
again:

Actually, we can factor out
$(x-n)$
again. This can be seen when solving
$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = 0$
by plugging in the values of the
$b_j$
's and checking that
$x=n$
is indeed a solution.
Plugging in
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j$

The equation now becomes:

$\hspace{2em}  -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = 0$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = in^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{-j} x^j = i$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} (\frac{x}{n})^j = i$

Plugging in
$x = n$
solves the equation, meaning that
$x = n$
is a double root of
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Therefore we can factor out
$(x-n)$
once again.

Using a similar procedure as described above, we can rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as
$ (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_k x^k$
. The
$c_k$
are conditioned by the following equality:

$\hspace{2em} (x-n)\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_kx^k = \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} b_kx^k$

Comparing coefficients of the same powers of x on both sides, gives:

$-nc_0 = b_0$

$c_0-nc_1 = b_1$

$c_k - nc_{k+1} = b_{k+1}$

$c_{i-2} = b_{i-1}$

Using values of the
$b_k$
's we derived earlier, we end up with
$c_k = n^{i-k-2}(i-k-2)$
for
$ 0 < k \leq i-2$
. Resultingly,

$\hspace{2em} z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} n^{i-k-2}(i-k-1) x^k$
.

Note:
If we would substitute this expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in equation (I), we would seem to run into problems with the upper bound of the sum in
$z_{n,i}(x)$
for
$i = 1$
, as the upperbound of
$i-2$
would be below the lower bound
$k = 0$
. However, this problem solves itself because
$z_{n,1}(x) = x^1-1\cdot n^{1-1}x + n^1(1-1) = x -x + 0 = 0$
. Therefore, we can substitute our expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in (I) which gives us after simplifying:

(II)
$\hspace{1em} \frac{(x-n)^2}{n^2} \sum_{i=2}^{N} i a_i  \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0 $

Looking for more roots

We are trying to find polynomials with the property that every tangent intersects the polynomial at least twice. This is equivalent to finding multiple distinct solutions for equation (I) and (II). We have found the double solution
$x = n$
, but it remains to be seen if we can find other real roots - irrespective of the initial choice of
$n$
. In what follows I will describe the (dead) ends that I have explored already.

First option:
The best option seems to be to look at the (possibly real) solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0$
. Note that for
$x \geq 0$
every summand is nonnegative, the first summand being
$(i-1)$
(which is always greater or equal than 1 as
$i$
ranges from 2 to N) so that the sum cannot possibly equal
$0$
. Therefore, take
$x < 0$
, Make sure
$x \neq n$
as we are looking for other solutions than
$x = n$
. Note that every negative
$x$
can be written as
$x = - \alpha n$
with
$\alpha > 0$
. This change of variables gives

$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1)  = \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (-\alpha)^{k}(i-k-1)$
. If we find an
$\alpha$
for which this equation holds, then
$x = -\alpha n$
is a solution of II.

If we call this last summation
$S$
, we get:

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2}$

Multiplying
$S$
with
$\alpha$
gives

$\hspace{2em} 0 = \alpha S = \alpha (i-1) - \alpha^2 (i-2) + \alpha^3 (i-3) - ... + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

Adding
$S$
and
$\alpha S$
together makes

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S(1+\alpha) = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha (i-1) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - \alpha^2 (i-2) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2} - (-1)^{i-3}\alpha^{i-2}\cdot 2 + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} = i - 1 + \alpha - \alpha^2 + \alpha^3 - ... - (-\alpha)^{i-1} $

$\hspace{2em} = i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k$

Which is equivalent to

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
with
$\alpha > 0$
.

This geometric series adds up to
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha}$
. Finding the solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
therefore means we need to find solutions of
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha} = i$
. Depending on whether
$i$
is even or odd, the resulting equation factor as follows:

\begin{cases}
\alpha^i+i\alpha+(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1}-\alpha^{i-2}+\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots-\alpha+(i-1)\right) & \text{for } i \text{ is even} \\
\alpha^i-i\alpha-(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1} -\alpha^{i-2} +\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots+\alpha-(i-1)\right) &\text{for } i \text{ is odd.}
\end{cases}

which in both cases can be written as

$(\alpha + 1)(i\cdot (-1)^{i-1} + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-1)^k\alpha^{i-1-k})$

The obvious root of this polynomial would be
$\alpha = -1$
, this however contradicts the definition that
$a > 0$
. Equating the other factor with
$0$
gives, after some relabeling, the equation that we started with:
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
.

"Every polynomial of odd degree has one or more inflection points" is a false statement since
$P(x)=x$
, which has odd degree
$1$
satisfies
$P''
(x)=0$
for all
$x$
. This implies that the graph never changes concavity, so it has no inflection points. I am guessing you probably have the polynomial degree bigger than one as your constraint. With this additional condition, the statement (2) is true.

Since
$P(x)$
is an odd degree polynomial,
$P'(x)$
is an even degree polynomial. Without loss of generality, assume the coefficient of the largest degree term in
$P'$
is positive. Since
$P'(x) \to \infty$
as
$x\to \pm \infty$
,
$P'$
must achieve a global minimum. Let
$x=a$
be the point where
$P'(x)$
achieves the global minimum, that is,
$P'(x)-P'(a) \ge 0$
for all
$x\in \mathbb{R}$
. The tangent line at that point is
$L(x)=P(a)+P'(a)(x-a)$
. I now claim that
$F(x) = P(x)-L(x)$
is zero only at
$x=a$
and
$a$
is an inflection point.

$F'(x)=P'(x)-P'(a) \geq 0$
. Moreover,
$F'(x)$
is not identically zero on any interval, since
$P'$
is a non-constant polynomial. Therefore, for
$x>a$
,
$F(x)=\int_a^x F^{\prime}(t) d t>0$
and for
$x<a$
,
$F(x)=-\int_x^a F^{\prime}(t) d t<0$
. Thus,
$F(x)=0$
only when
$x=a$
.

$F'(x)=P'(x)-P'(a) \geq 0$
. Moreover,
$F'(x)$
is not identically zero on any interval, since
$P'$
is a non-constant polynomial. Therefore, for
$x>a$
,
$F(x)=\int_a^x F^{\prime}(t) d t>0$
and for
$x<a$
,
$F(x)=-\int_x^a F^{\prime}(t) d t<0$
. Thus,
$F(x)=0$
only when
$x=a$
.

Proof of
$a$
being an inflection point:

Since
$F'(x)\ge 0$
,
$F'(x) = P'(x)-P'(a) = (x-a)^{2\ell}R(x)$
for some natural number
$\ell$
and some polynomial
$R(a) > 0$
. Now
$F''(x)=P''(x)=(x-a)^{2\ell-1}Q(x)$
where
$Q(a)\ne 0$
. Thus
$P''(a)$
changes sign at
$x=a$
implying that
$a$
is an inflection point.

Since
$F'(x)\ge 0$
,
$F'(x) = P'(x)-P'(a) = (x-a)^{2\ell}R(x)$
for some natural number
$\ell$
and some polynomial
$R(a) > 0$
. Now
$F''(x)=P''(x)=(x-a)^{2\ell-1}Q(x)$
where
$Q(a)\ne 0$
. Thus
$P''(a)$
changes sign at
$x=a$
implying that
$a$
is an inflection point.

conversation.content:
I'm doing math problems for fun. Currently stuck on this problem. My most urgent questions are listed first. Then i'll sketch what I've thought of so far. In the end, I list a few options that I suspect might be resourceful directions to work out. Would love feedback and ideas for solving this.

Open questions in this process

Given
$i > 2$
, how do I find values of
$\alpha \neq -1$
so that
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
is satisfied?

Proof that every polynomial P(x) of odd degree has one or more inflection points, of which there is at least one inflection point in which the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else.

Main idea

Goal is to find (or disprove the existence of) polynomials
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
such that any tangent line intersects
$P$
at least twice. In other words, the following equation must have multiple distinct real roots for every
$n \in$
ℝ:

$\hspace{2em} P(x)-L_n(x) = 0$

Here
$L_n(x)$
is the tangent to
$P$
in
$(n,P(n))$
, which we can write as
$L_n(x) = P'(n)x + P(n) - nP'(n)$
. This changes the equation above to

$\hspace{2em} \space \space P(x) - P'(n)x - P(n) + nP'(n) = 0$

Rewriting using
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i - (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space x  - \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i n^i + (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space n = 0$

which is equivalent to

(I)
$\hspace{1em} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i (x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)) = 0$

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
:

In what follows we will focus on the polynomial inside the summands. The goal is find roots of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
. For convenience we call this polynomial
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Because the tangent in
$(n,P(n))$
intersects
$P(x)$
for
$x=n$
, we know that
$z_{n,i}(n)=0$
. This means we can factor out
$(x-n)$
and rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as follows:

$\hspace{2em}  z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$

To determine the value of the
$b_j$
's we can rewrite
$(x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$
by taking the
$(x-n)$
inside the summand and reordering the terms according to the powers of
$x$
:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^{j+1} - n b_j x^j = b_{i-1} x^i + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} x^j (-nb_j + b_{j-1})$

Comparing the corresponding coefficients of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
gives, using
$b_{-1} = 0$
,

$ b_{-1} -nb_0 =  n^i(i-1)$

$ b_0 - nb_1 = -in^{i-1}$

$ b_j - n b_{j+1} = 0 $
for
$0 < j < i-1$

$ b_{i-1} = 1$

This nicely reduces to
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
for all
$0 < j \leq i-1$
. Thus we have successfully factored out
$(x-n)$
, leaving us with
$z_{n,i}(x)= (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j $
.

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
again:

Actually, we can factor out
$(x-n)$
again. This can be seen when solving
$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = 0$
by plugging in the values of the
$b_j$
's and checking that
$x=n$
is indeed a solution.
Plugging in
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j$

The equation now becomes:

$\hspace{2em}  -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = 0$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = in^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{-j} x^j = i$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} (\frac{x}{n})^j = i$

Plugging in
$x = n$
solves the equation, meaning that
$x = n$
is a double root of
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Therefore we can factor out
$(x-n)$
once again.

Using a similar procedure as described above, we can rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as
$ (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_k x^k$
. The
$c_k$
are conditioned by the following equality:

$\hspace{2em} (x-n)\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_kx^k = \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} b_kx^k$

Comparing coefficients of the same powers of x on both sides, gives:

$-nc_0 = b_0$

$c_0-nc_1 = b_1$

$c_k - nc_{k+1} = b_{k+1}$

$c_{i-2} = b_{i-1}$

Using values of the
$b_k$
's we derived earlier, we end up with
$c_k = n^{i-k-2}(i-k-2)$
for
$ 0 < k \leq i-2$
. Resultingly,

$\hspace{2em} z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} n^{i-k-2}(i-k-1) x^k$
.

Note:
If we would substitute this expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in equation (I), we would seem to run into problems with the upper bound of the sum in
$z_{n,i}(x)$
for
$i = 1$
, as the upperbound of
$i-2$
would be below the lower bound
$k = 0$
. However, this problem solves itself because
$z_{n,1}(x) = x^1-1\cdot n^{1-1}x + n^1(1-1) = x -x + 0 = 0$
. Therefore, we can substitute our expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in (I) which gives us after simplifying:

(II)
$\hspace{1em} \frac{(x-n)^2}{n^2} \sum_{i=2}^{N} i a_i  \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0 $

Looking for more roots

We are trying to find polynomials with the property that every tangent intersects the polynomial at least twice. This is equivalent to finding multiple distinct solutions for equation (I) and (II). We have found the double solution
$x = n$
, but it remains to be seen if we can find other real roots - irrespective of the initial choice of
$n$
. In what follows I will describe the (dead) ends that I have explored already.

First option:
The best option seems to be to look at the (possibly real) solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0$
. Note that for
$x \geq 0$
every summand is nonnegative, the first summand being
$(i-1)$
(which is always greater or equal than 1 as
$i$
ranges from 2 to N) so that the sum cannot possibly equal
$0$
. Therefore, take
$x < 0$
, Make sure
$x \neq n$
as we are looking for other solutions than
$x = n$
. Note that every negative
$x$
can be written as
$x = - \alpha n$
with
$\alpha > 0$
. This change of variables gives

$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1)  = \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (-\alpha)^{k}(i-k-1)$
. If we find an
$\alpha$
for which this equation holds, then
$x = -\alpha n$
is a solution of II.

If we call this last summation
$S$
, we get:

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2}$

Multiplying
$S$
with
$\alpha$
gives

$\hspace{2em} 0 = \alpha S = \alpha (i-1) - \alpha^2 (i-2) + \alpha^3 (i-3) - ... + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

Adding
$S$
and
$\alpha S$
together makes

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S(1+\alpha) = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha (i-1) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - \alpha^2 (i-2) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2} - (-1)^{i-3}\alpha^{i-2}\cdot 2 + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} = i - 1 + \alpha - \alpha^2 + \alpha^3 - ... - (-\alpha)^{i-1} $

$\hspace{2em} = i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k$

Which is equivalent to

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
with
$\alpha > 0$
.

This geometric series adds up to
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha}$
. Finding the solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
therefore means we need to find solutions of
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha} = i$
. Depending on whether
$i$
is even or odd, the resulting equation factor as follows:

\begin{cases}
\alpha^i+i\alpha+(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1}-\alpha^{i-2}+\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots-\alpha+(i-1)\right) & \text{for } i \text{ is even} \\
\alpha^i-i\alpha-(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1} -\alpha^{i-2} +\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots+\alpha-(i-1)\right) &\text{for } i \text{ is odd.}
\end{cases}

which in both cases can be written as

$(\alpha + 1)(i\cdot (-1)^{i-1} + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-1)^k\alpha^{i-1-k})$

The obvious root of this polynomial would be
$\alpha = -1$
, this however contradicts the definition that
$a > 0$
. Equating the other factor with
$0$
gives, after some relabeling, the equation that we started with:
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
.

At this point I don't know if and how I would be able to find values of
$\alpha$
such that
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
is satisfied. Help would be appreciated!

Second option:
The second direction that I have started exploring is that of inflection points. This more visual approach has had me conjecturing that every polynomial P(x) either has

no inflection points and is therefore totally convex/concave, resulting in (some) tangent lines that never intersect P more than once, or

one or more inflection points, of which there is at least one inflection point in which the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else.

In other words; I conjecture that there are no polynomials in which every tangent is also a secant. I have not yet found the tools to work this out rigorously.

Link between these options:
What I do not yet understand is how these two options (algebraic puzzling and inflection points) are related to each others. Maybe there is a link between
$z_{n,i}(x)$
and
$P''$
?

Any help would be appreciated!

in (I) which gives us after simplifying:

$\hspace{1em} \frac{(x-n)^2}{n^2} \sum_{i=2}^{N} i a_i  \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0 $

Looking for more roots

We are trying to find polynomials with the property that every tangent intersects the polynomial at least twice. This is equivalent to finding multiple distinct solutions for equation (I) and (II). We have found the double solution
$x = n$
, but it remains to be seen if we can find other real roots - irrespective of the initial choice of
$n$
. In what follows I will describe the (dead) ends that I have explored already.

First option:
The best option seems to be to look at the (possibly real) solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0$
. Note that for
$x \geq 0$
every summand is nonnegative, the first summand being
$(i-1)$
(which is always greater or equal than 1 as
$i$
ranges from 2 to N) so that the sum cannot possibly equal
$0$
. Therefore, take
$x < 0$
, Make sure
$x \neq n$
as we are looking for other solutions than
$x = n$
. Note that every negative
$x$
can be written as
$x = - \alpha n$
with
$\alpha > 0$
. This change of variables gives

$\sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1)  = \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (-\alpha)^{k}(i-k-1)$
. If we find an
$\alpha$
for which this equation holds, then
$x = -\alpha n$
is a solution of II.

If we call this last summation
$S$
, we get:

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2}$

Multiplying
$S$
with
$\alpha$
gives

$\hspace{2em} 0 = \alpha S = \alpha (i-1) - \alpha^2 (i-2) + \alpha^3 (i-3) - ... + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

Adding
$S$
and
$\alpha S$
together makes

$\hspace{2em} 0 = S(1+\alpha) = (i-1) - \alpha (i-2) + \alpha (i-1) + \alpha^2 (i-3) - \alpha^2 (i-2) - ... + (-\alpha)^{i-2} - (-1)^{i-3}\alpha^{i-2}\cdot 2 + (-1)^{i-2}\alpha^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} = i - 1 + \alpha - \alpha^2 + \alpha^3 - ... - (-\alpha)^{i-1} $

$\hspace{2em} = i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k$

Which is equivalent to

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
with
$\alpha > 0$
.

This geometric series adds up to
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha}$
. Finding the solutions of
$\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = i$
therefore means we need to find solutions of
$\frac{1-(-\alpha)^i}{1-\alpha} = i$
. Depending on whether
$i$
is even or odd, the resulting equation factor as follows:

\begin{cases}
\alpha^i+i\alpha+(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1}-\alpha^{i-2}+\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots-\alpha+(i-1)\right) & \text{for } i \text{ is even} \\
\alpha^i-i\alpha-(i-1)
=
(\alpha+1)
\left(\alpha^{i-1} -\alpha^{i-2} +\alpha^{i-3}-\cdots+\alpha-(i-1)\right) &\text{for } i \text{ is odd.}
\end{cases}

which in both cases can be written as

$(\alpha + 1)(i\cdot (-1)^{i-1} + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-1)^k\alpha^{i-1-k})$

The obvious root of this polynomial would be
$\alpha = -1$
, this however contradicts the definition that
$a > 0$
. Equating the other factor with
$0$
gives, after some relabeling, the equation that we started with:
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
.

At this point I don't know if and how I would be able to find values of
$\alpha$
such that
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
is satisfied. Help would be appreciated!

Second option:
The second direction that I have started exploring is that of inflection points. This more visual approach has had me conjecturing that every polynomial P(x) either has

no inflection points and is therefore totally convex/concave, resulting in (some) tangent lines that never intersect P more than once, or

one or more inflection points, of which there is at least one inflection point in which the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else.

In other words; I conjecture that there are no polynomials in which every tangent is also a secant. I have not yet found the tools to work this out rigorously.

Link between these options:
What I do not yet understand is how these two options (algebraic puzzling and inflection points) are related to each others. Maybe there is a link between
$z_{n,i}(x)$
and
$P''$
?

Any help would be appreciated!
answer:
"Every polynomial P(x) of odd degree has one or more inflection points, and there is at least one inflection point where the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else."

This is false for degree
$1$
polynomials.

Let the degree of
$P(x)$
be
$3$
or more, and odd. The
$P^\prime(x)$
is even degree at least
$2$
. It will have either a global minimum or global maximum at some point
$x=x_0$
.

Let's assume it's a global minimum. If not, the argument below still works with only minor changes.

This will be an inflection point of
$P(x)$
, since for some
$\epsilon>0$
,
$P^\prime(x)$
is increasing for
$x-\epsilon<x<x_0$
and decreasing for
$x_0<x<x+\epsilon$
, or vice-versa.

The tangent line is
$y=P^\prime(x_0)(x-x_0) + P(x_0)$
. This intersects
$P(x)$
at
$x=x_0$
. Suppose it also intersects
$P(x)$
at
$x=x_1$
.

Assume first
$x_1>x_0$
. If not, the following argument works with only minor changes.

We have

\begin{align}
P(x_1)-P(x_0) &= \left(P^\prime(x_0)(x_1-x_0) + P(x_0)\right)-\left(P^\prime(x_0)(x_0-x_0) + P(x_0)\right)\\
&=P^\prime(x_0)(x_1-x_0) + P(x_0)-0 - P(x_0)\\
&=P^\prime(x_0)(x_1-x_0)
\end{align}

But we also have, since
$P^\prime(x)$
is not constant in the interval
$[x_0,x_1]$
and has a global minimum at
$x=x_0$
,
We have

\begin{align}
P(x_1)-P(x_0) &= \int_{x_0}^{x_1}P^\prime(x)dx\\
&>\int_{x_0}^{x_1}P^\prime(x_0)dx\\
&=P^\prime(x_0)(x_1-x_0)
\end{align}

This is a contradiction, hence no second intersection point
$x=x_1$
exists.

Therefore, polynomials of odd degree 3 or more have a tangent that intersects the polynomial only once, at an infection point.

I'm doing math problems for fun. Currently stuck on this problem. My most urgent questions are listed first. Then i'll sketch what I've thought of so far. In the end, I list a few options that I suspect might be resourceful directions to work out. Would love feedback and ideas for solving this.

Open questions in this process

Given
$i > 2$
, how do I find values of
$\alpha \neq -1$
so that
$ i - \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (-\alpha)^k = 0$
is satisfied?

Proof that every polynomial P(x) of odd degree has one or more inflection points, of which there is at least one inflection point in which the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else.

Main idea

Goal is to find (or disprove the existence of) polynomials
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
such that any tangent line intersects
$P$
at least twice. In other words, the following equation must have multiple distinct real roots for every
$n \in$
ℝ:

$\hspace{2em} P(x)-L_n(x) = 0$

Here
$L_n(x)$
is the tangent to
$P$
in
$(n,P(n))$
, which we can write as
$L_n(x) = P'(n)x + P(n) - nP'(n)$
. This changes the equation above to

$\hspace{2em} \space \space P(x) - P'(n)x - P(n) + nP'(n) = 0$

Rewriting using
$P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i $
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i x^i - (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space x  - \sum_{i=0}^{N} a_i n^i + (\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i i n^{i-1})\space n = 0$

which is equivalent to

(I)
$\hspace{1em} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i (x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)) = 0$

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
:

In what follows we will focus on the polynomial inside the summands. The goal is find roots of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
. For convenience we call this polynomial
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Because the tangent in
$(n,P(n))$
intersects
$P(x)$
for
$x=n$
, we know that
$z_{n,i}(n)=0$
. This means we can factor out
$(x-n)$
and rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as follows:

$\hspace{2em}  z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$

To determine the value of the
$b_j$
's we can rewrite
$(x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j$
by taking the
$(x-n)$
inside the summand and reordering the terms according to the powers of
$x$
:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^{j+1} - n b_j x^j = b_{i-1} x^i + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} x^j (-nb_j + b_{j-1})$

Comparing the corresponding coefficients of
$x^i - i n^{i-1}x + n^i(i-1)$
gives, using
$b_{-1} = 0$
,

$ b_{-1} -nb_0 =  n^i(i-1)$

$ b_0 - nb_1 = -in^{i-1}$

$ b_j - n b_{j+1} = 0 $
for
$0 < j < i-1$

$ b_{i-1} = 1$

This nicely reduces to
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
for all
$0 < j \leq i-1$
. Thus we have successfully factored out
$(x-n)$
, leaving us with
$z_{n,i}(x)= (x-n) \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j $
.

Factoring out
$(x-n)$
again:

Actually, we can factor out
$(x-n)$
again. This can be seen when solving
$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = 0$
by plugging in the values of the
$b_j$
's and checking that
$x=n$
is indeed a solution.
Plugging in
$b_0 = n^{i-1}(1-i)$
and
$b_j = n^{i-j-1}$
gives:

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} b_j x^j = -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j$

The equation now becomes:

$\hspace{2em}  -in^{i-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = 0$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{i-j-1} x^j = in^{i-1}$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} n^{-j} x^j = i$

$\hspace{2em} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} (\frac{x}{n})^j = i$

Plugging in
$x = n$
solves the equation, meaning that
$x = n$
is a double root of
$z_{n,i}(x)$
. Therefore we can factor out
$(x-n)$
once again.

Using a similar procedure as described above, we can rewrite
$z_{n,i}(x)$
as
$ (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_k x^k$
. The
$c_k$
are conditioned by the following equality:

$\hspace{2em} (x-n)\sum_{k=0}^{i-1} c_kx^k = \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} b_kx^k$

Comparing coefficients of the same powers of x on both sides, gives:

$-nc_0 = b_0$

$c_0-nc_1 = b_1$

$c_k - nc_{k+1} = b_{k+1}$

$c_{i-2} = b_{i-1}$

Using values of the
$b_k$
's we derived earlier, we end up with
$c_k = n^{i-k-2}(i-k-2)$
for
$ 0 < k \leq i-2$
. Resultingly,

$\hspace{2em} z_{n,i}(x) = (x-n)^2 \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} n^{i-k-2}(i-k-1) x^k$
.

Note:
If we would substitute this expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in equation (I), we would seem to run into problems with the upper bound of the sum in
$z_{n,i}(x)$
for
$i = 1$
, as the upperbound of
$i-2$
would be below the lower bound
$k = 0$
. However, this problem solves itself because
$z_{n,1}(x) = x^1-1\cdot n^{1-1}x + n^1(1-1) = x -x + 0 = 0$
. Therefore, we can substitute our expression for
$z_{n,i}(x)$
in (I) which gives us after simplifying:

(II)
$\hspace{1em} \frac{(x-n)^2}{n^2} \sum_{i=2}^{N} i a_i  \sum_{k=0}^{i-2} (\frac{x}{n})^{k}(i-k-1) = 0 $

Looking for more roots

"Every polynomial P(x) of odd degree has one or more inflection points, and there is at least one inflection point where the tangent line only intersects P in that point and nowhere else."

This is false for degree
$1$
polynomials.

Let the degree of
$P(x)$
be
$3$
or more, and odd. The
$P^\prime(x)$
is even degree at least
$2$
. It will have either a global minimum or global maximum at some point
$x=x_0$
.

Let's assume it's a global minimum. If not, the argument below still works with only minor changes.

This will be an inflection point of
$P(x)$
, since for some
$\epsilon>0$
,
$P^\prime(x)$
is increasing for
$x-\epsilon<x<x_0$
and decreasing for
$x_0<x<x+\epsilon$
, or vice-versa.

The tangent line is
$y=P^\prime(x_0)(x-x_0) + P(x_0)$
. This intersects
$P(x)$
at
$x=x_0$
. Suppose it also intersects
$P(x)$
at
$x=x_1$
.

$x_1>x_0$
. If not, the following argument works with only minor changes.

\begin{align}

&=P^\prime(x_0)(x_1-x_0) + P(x_0)-0 - P(x_0)\\

is not constant in the interval
