title: Is this attempt of a generalization of the Christoffel symbols well-defined?
question:
I am trying to generalize the concept of connections to other orders (than 2).

The idea for "a covariant velocity" is the same as for covariant acceleration, namely that we can write the covariant velocity as a tangent vector:

$$
\begin{align*}
\sum_{ik} \left[ \dot x^k(0) + \Gamma^k_{i}(p) x^i(0) \right] \partial_k.
\end{align*}
$$


In two different charts, we will then have


$$
\begin{align*}
\sum_{ik} \left[ \dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i}\right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i  \right] (\partial_k)_{(U_1, h_1)} = \sum_{ik} \left[ \dot y^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \left(\partial_k\right)_{(U_2, h_2)}.
\end{align*}
$$


For the expression in
$(U_2, h_2)$
, noting we are renaming
$k \to j$
indices on the RHS:


$$
\begin{align*}
\left[ \dot y^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \left(\partial_k\right)_{(U_2, h_2)} = \left[ \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \left(\partial_k \right)_{(U_1, h_1)}.
\end{align*}
$$


First compare the coefficients of the
$\partial_k$
's:


$$
\begin{align*}
\dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i &= \left[ \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \delta_l^k \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}.
\end{align*}
$$


We therefore get


$$
\begin{align*}
\left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}.
\end{align*}
$$


I have verified that this relation is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.


For example, the proof of transitivity it as follows: assume


$$
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^l_j \right)_{(U_3, h_3)} z^j = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j}, \\
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^a_b \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^b = \left(\Gamma_c^b \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^a}{\partial x^b}.
\end{cases}
$$


Then


$$
\begin{align*}
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^l_j \right)_{(U_3, h_3)} z^j &= \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} = \\
&= \left( \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^i} \right) \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^i} \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial x^i}.
\end{align*}
$$


So we have transitivity.


What I cannot successfully do is to determine
if the equation can be solved in the first place
.


Are there unique smooth solutions to this equation? I.e., can we in a fixed pair of overlapping charts find smooth
$\left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)}$
and
$\left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)}$
such that


$$
\begin{align*}
\left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}
\end{align*}
$$

holds?
answer:
This is not possible unless the additional term
$\Gamma^k_i x^i$
is identically zero, so your covariant velocity is just equal to ordinary velocity.
In your proposed transformation law,

$$
\begin{align*}
\left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}.
\end{align*}
$$

if there is a point
$p$
where the left-hand side is nonzero, we can always choose
$y$
-coordinates such that the
$y$
-coordinate of
$p$
is zero, and then the right-hand side will be zero.

From a more philosophical point of view, the problem with the idea of a "covariant velocity" is that ordinary velocity is already covariant -- for a smooth curve in a manifold, if we express the curve in two different coordinate systems, then the two expressions

$$
\dot x^i(t) \left. \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_{x(t)}
\text{ and }
\dot y^i(t) \left. \frac{\partial}{\partial y^i}\right|_{y(t)}
$$

will be equal to the same tangent vector. Thus the term you're adding, namely

$$
\Gamma^k_{i}(p) x^i(0) \frac{\partial}{ \partial x_k}
$$

(which depends only on the point
$p$
, since I think you're assuming
$x(t)$
is a curve such that
$x(0)=p$
), would have to be a well-defined vector field on the manifold if this expression is to make coordinate-independent sense. But your expression has to vanish at the origin in
$x$
-coordinates. Since every point can be made the origin in some coordinate system, the additional term has to be identically zero in order to be covariant.

conversation.content:
I am trying to generalize the concept of connections to other orders (than 2).

The idea for "a covariant velocity" is the same as for covariant acceleration, namely that we can write the covariant velocity as a tangent vector:

$$
\begin{align*}
\sum_{ik} \left[ \dot x^k(0) + \Gamma^k_{i}(p) x^i(0) \right] \partial_k.
\end{align*}
$$


In two different charts, we will then have


$$
\begin{align*}
\sum_{ik} \left[ \dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i}\right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i  \right] (\partial_k)_{(U_1, h_1)} = \sum_{ik} \left[ \dot y^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \left(\partial_k\right)_{(U_2, h_2)}.
\end{align*}
$$


For the expression in
$(U_2, h_2)$
, noting we are renaming
$k \to j$
indices on the RHS:


$$
\begin{align*}
\left[ \dot y^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \left(\partial_k\right)_{(U_2, h_2)} = \left[ \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \left(\partial_k \right)_{(U_1, h_1)}.
\end{align*}
$$


First compare the coefficients of the
$\partial_k$
's:


$$
\begin{align*}
\dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i &= \left[ \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \right] \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^l} \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \delta_l^k \dot x^l + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \dot x^k + \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}.
\end{align*}
$$


We therefore get


$$
\begin{align*}
\left(\Gamma^k_{i} \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^i = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial x^k}{\partial y^j}.
\end{align*}
$$


I have verified that this relation is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.


For example, the proof of transitivity it as follows: assume


$$
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^l_j \right)_{(U_3, h_3)} z^j = \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j}, \\
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^a_b \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^b = \left(\Gamma_c^b \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^a}{\partial x^b}.
\end{cases}
$$


Then


$$
\begin{align*}
\displaystyle
\left(\Gamma^l_j \right)_{(U_3, h_3)} z^j &= \left(\Gamma^j_{i} \right)_{(U_2, h_2)} y^i \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} = \\
&= \left( \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^i} \right) \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^i} \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial y^j} \\
&= \left(\Gamma^i_c \right)_{(U_1, h_1)} x^c \frac{\partial z^l}{\partial x^i}.
\end{align*}
$$


So we have transitivity.


What I cannot successfully do is to determine

title: Another question regarding generators in an amalgamated group
question:
This is essentially a refinement of a question I asked
here
.

Let
$G$
be a infinite group, let
$A,B\leq G$
be subgroups with
$G$
being the amalgama of
$A,B$
along their intersection i.e.
$G\simeq A*_C B$
with
$C:= A\cap B$
. The following should hold:

(1)
Assume
$A$
is generated by the families
$\{a_i\}_{i\in I}\cup \{c_k\}_{k\in K}$
with
$a_i\in A\setminus B$
and
$c_k\in C$
.

(2)
Also assume
$B$
is generated by the families
$\{b_j\}_{j\in J}\cup \{c_k\}_{k\in K}$
with
$b_j\in B\setminus A$
and
$c_k\in C$
.

(3)
And lastly
$$\text{For all }x\in \langle a_i:\text{ } i\in I\rangle\text{ we have }  x\notin \langle b_j,c_k:\text{ } j\in J,k\in K\rangle$$


$$\text{For all }x\in \langle b_j:\text{ } j\in J\rangle\text{ we have }  x\notin \langle a_i,c_k:\text{ } i\in I,k\in K\rangle$$


$$\text{For all }x\in \langle c_k:\text{ } k\in K\rangle\text{ we have }  x\notin \langle a_i,b_j \text{ } i\in I,j\in J\rangle .$$


So no word with letters in
$\{a_i\}_{i\in I}$
can be written as a word with letters only in
$\{b_j\}_{j\in J}\cup \{c_k\}_{k\in K}$
etc. I intuitively feel the families should be very distinct, and that the following should hold
$$C=\langle c_k:\text{ }k\in K \rangle .$$

I have tried using induction over minimal length of words with letters in
$\{a_i\}_{i\in I}\cup \{b_j\}_{j\in J}\cup \{c_k\}_{k\in K}$
, also using that
$x\in C$
implies the length of
$x$
as an element of
$G$
should be zero. But have not come up with a proper solution as of yet.
answer:
OK, how about
$A=B \cong A_4$
with
$C = \langle c,d \rangle \cong C_2 \times C_2$
,
$A=\langle a,c \rangle$
and
$B = \langle b,c \rangle$
, where
$a$
and
$b$
have order
$3$
.

I think all of your conditions are satisfied with generators
$\{ a \}$
,
$\{ b \}$
, and
$\{ c \}$
, but
$C$
is not generated by
$c$
.

Note that
$a$
and
$b$
generate the free product
$\langle a \rangle * \langle b \rangle \cong C_3 * C_3$
, which has trivial intersection with
$C$
.

title: Examples of persistence modules
question:
I have a typical question, because I don't have an intuitive feel for the definition.

Can you give me typical simple examples of a persistence module?

E.g:

Let
$V_1 =\mathbb{R}^2$
and
$V_2 = \mathbb{R}^1=\mathbb{R}$

And
$F_{12} : V_1 \to V_2$
by the rule:
$F [(x\  y)^T] = x$
.

Or vice versa:

Let
$V_1 =\mathbb{R}^1$
, and
$V_2 = \mathbb{R}^2$

And
$F_{12} : V_1 \to V_2$
given by:
$F[x] = (x\ 0)^T \in \mathbb{R}^2$

Are these correct examples of persistence modules?

Thanks a lot!!
answer:
These are both essentially persistence modules.

A persistence module is defined over a totally ordered index set. In both of your examples, this is left implicit. If we specify the totally ordered set
$1\to 2$
as our index set, then the examples given are indeed persistence modules.

title: How can I find generalized discriminant formula?
question:
Given a polynomial (
$a_1$$x^{n}+a_1 x^{n-1} + \ldots + a_n$
),
if
$r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_n$
are real roots of this
$n$
-th power equation, show that the generalized discriminant formula is product of

$($
a1
$)^{2n-2}×$
(
$r_1$
-$r_2)^{2} \ldots \times (r_1-r_n)^{2} \times (r_2-r_3)^{2} \times (\ldots(r_2 - r_n)^{2} \times \ldots \times (r_{n-1}-r_n)^{2} $$

I had proved for quadratic and cubic discriminant formula but how can we prove for higher degree polynomial discriminant formula ?
answer:
This is not a fully rigorous proof, but perhaps will provide you some intuition to develop ideas and your proof.

$$(-1)^{2(n-1)}\prod_{i=0}^n \prod_{j=i+1}^n (r_i - r_j)^2$$

Observe that if any
$r_i = r_j$
for
$i \ne j$
, we must have a
$0$
as the result of our product. Ie, the polynomial has at least one repeated root.

Considering that we are given
$n$
real roots for a
$n$
degree polynomial, we must therefore have no complex roots by the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

Furthermore, observe that
$(-1)^{2(n-1)} = \left((-1)^2\right)^{n-1} = 1^{n-1} = 1$
. Thus, for all given
$n$
and with
$n$
real roots, we will have a value that
is positive or zero.

Depending on your definition of discriminant, you may define it as a function that gives information on the multiplicity of roots and number of real complex roots in relation to each other. Observe that this function does satisfy this definition.

title: A Diameter Condition Forcing a Long Cycle in a Finite Graph
question:
Let
$d>1$
be an integer, and let
$G$
be a finite simple graph with

$\operatorname{Diam}(G)=d$
, such that for every vertex
$v \in V(G)$
, there exists a vertex
$u \in V(G)$
satisfying
$\operatorname{Dist}(u,v)=d$
.
Prove that
$G$
contains a cycle of length at least
$2d$
.

(A. N. Kolmogorov Memory Cup 2016)

Here are some ideas and partial observations:

It clearly suffices to consider the case when
$G$
is connected, so assume throughout that
$G$
is connected. Assume for the sake of contradiction that
$G$
does not contain such a cycle.

Lemma 1:

Every vertex of
$G$
has degree at least

$2$
.

Proof sketch:

Assume that some vertex
$v$
has degree

$1$
, and let
$u$
be its unique neighbor. By the hypothesis, there exists a vertex
$w$
such that
$\operatorname{Dist}(u,w)=d$
. Since
$d>1$
, we have
$v \ne w$
, hence
$\operatorname{Dist}(v,w)=d+1$
(why?), a contradiction.

Lemma 2:

The graph
$G$
contains a cycle.

Proof sketch:
Assume for the sake of contradiction that
$G$
does not contain a cycle, then
$G$
is a tree, thus it contains a vertex of degree
$1$
, contradicting Lemma 1.

Lemma 3:

For every vertex
$v \in V(G)$
, there exist at least two vertices at distance exactly
$d$
from
$v$
.

Proof sketch:

Suppose instead that
$u$
is the unique vertex satisfying
$\operatorname{Dist}(u,v)=d$
. Root the graph at
$v$
, partitioning the vertices into layers according to their distance from
$v$
. Then the
$d$
th layer consists only of
$u$
.

Now choose a vertex
$w$
distinct from
$u$
and
$v$
and let
$w_1$
be a vertex at distance
$d$
from it (obviously
$w_1$
is not
$u$
or
$v$
). Consider the two natural walks from
$w$
to
$w_1$
, one passing through
$u$
, and the other through
$v$
. Their total length is
$2d$
, thus both walks must actually be paths of length
$d$
(why?). Moreover, they are internally disjoint (why?), so their union forms a cycle of length
$2d$
, contradicting the assumption that no such cycle exists.

According to Lemma 2,
$G$
has a cycle; let
$C:= v_1v_2 \ldots v_nv_1$
be one of the longest cycles in
$G$
, where
$3 \le n \le 2d-1$
. For any two vertices
$v, u$
in
$G$
, the distance between
$u, v$
is less than
$d$
thus for any vertex
$x$
in
$C$
, there exists a vertex
$v_x$
in
$G$
but not in
$C$
which satisfies
$\operatorname{Dist}(x, v_x)=d$
.

I would appreciate either a complete proof or a different approach.
answer:
First note that the graph is connected since it has finite diameter. Now, assume we do not have a cut-vertex. A
cut-vertex
is a vertex
$v$
such that
$G\setminus \{v\}$
is disconnected. Let us prove the conclusion under this assumption. Take a vertex
$v_0$
, and find
$v_d$
such that
$d(v_0,v_d)=d$
and we have the path
$v_0,v_1,\ldots,v_d$
with
$v_i\sim v_{i+1}$
, where
$x\sim y$
implies
$x$
is adjacent to
$y$
.

Now, we use
Menger’s theorem
which is the following:

Menger’s Theorem:
A graph is
$k$
-vertex-connected if and only if every pair of vertices has at least
$k$
internally disjoint paths in between.

Menger’s Theorem:
A graph is
$k$
-vertex-connected if and only if every pair of vertices has at least
$k$
internally disjoint paths in between.

We do not have a cut-vertex, is the same as saying, we are
$2$
-vertex-connected. Hence, we observe that we have at least
$2$
internally disjoint path between
$v_0,v_d$
, which means the new path does not use the vertices
$v_1,\ldots,v_{d-1}$
. Now, observe that this path has be at least length
$d$
, why? Because, otherwise
$d(v_0,v_d)\leq d-1$
. Hence, suppose the new path is
$v_0,u_1,\ldots,u_{d-1},v_d$
. Now, we concatanete the two paths to get the
$2d$
-cycle: namely,
$v_0,v_1,\ldots,v_{d-1},v_d,u_{d-1},\ldots,u_1,v_0$
. Thus finishes the proof conditional on the assumption that we have no cut-vertex.

Now, let us prove that claim.

Suppose there is a cut-vertex
$v$
, so that it disconnects the graph into two parts:
$G\setminus \{v\}=V_1\cup V_2$
and
$V_1\cap V_2=\emptyset$
, and we need both to be non-empty of course. Now, let
$u$
be a vertex such that
$d(v,u)=d$
and let
$v\in V_1$
. Picking a vertex in
$t\in V_2$
, we observe that the distance from
$v$
to
$t$
is greater than
$d$
. This contradicts the fact that the diameter was
$d$
. Hence, this finishes the proof of the claim.

Now, to come to your first “why?”: Suppose you have
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)\leq d$
. Then, since you know this path must through the vertex
$u$
(there is no other way to reach
$v$
), this implies
$\operatorname{dist}(u,w)\leq d$
, which is a contradiction. Hence,
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)\geq d+1$
, but using
$u-w$
path, we already have a path of distance exactly
$d+1$
, so
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)=d+1$
.

$w$
such that
$\operatorname{Dist}(u,w)=d$
. Since
$d>1$
, we have
$v \ne w$
, hence
$\operatorname{Dist}(v,w)=d+1$
(why?), a contradiction.

Lemma 2:

The graph
$G$
contains a cycle.

Proof sketch:
Assume for the sake of contradiction that
$G$
does not contain a cycle, then
$G$
is a tree, thus it contains a vertex of degree
$1$
, contradicting Lemma 1.

Lemma 3:

For every vertex
$v \in V(G)$
, there exist at least two vertices at distance exactly
$d$
from
$v$
.

Proof sketch:

Suppose instead that
$u$
is the unique vertex satisfying
$\operatorname{Dist}(u,v)=d$
. Root the graph at
$v$
, partitioning the vertices into layers according to their distance from
$v$
. Then the
$d$
th layer consists only of
$u$
.

Now choose a vertex
$w$
distinct from
$u$
and
$v$
and let
$w_1$
be a vertex at distance
$d$
from it (obviously
$w_1$
is not
$u$
or
$v$
). Consider the two natural walks from
$w$
to
$w_1$
, one passing through
$u$
, and the other through
$v$
. Their total length is
$2d$
, thus both walks must actually be paths of length
$d$
(why?). Moreover, they are internally disjoint (why?), so their union forms a cycle of length
$2d$
, contradicting the assumption that no such cycle exists.

According to Lemma 2,
$G$
has a cycle; let
$C:= v_1v_2 \ldots v_nv_1$
be one of the longest cycles in
$G$
, where
$3 \le n \le 2d-1$
. For any two vertices
$v, u$
in
$G$
, the distance between
$u, v$
is less than
$d$
thus for any vertex
$x$
in
$C$
, there exists a vertex
$v_x$
in
$G$
but not in
$C$
which satisfies
$\operatorname{Dist}(x, v_x)=d$
.

I would appreciate either a complete proof or a different approach.

First note that the graph is connected since it has finite diameter. Now, assume we do not have a cut-vertex. A
cut-vertex
is a vertex
$v$
such that
$G\setminus \{v\}$
is disconnected. Let us prove the conclusion under this assumption. Take a vertex
$v_0$
, and find
$v_d$
such that
$d(v_0,v_d)=d$
and we have the path
$v_0,v_1,\ldots,v_d$
with
$v_i\sim v_{i+1}$
, where
$x\sim y$
implies
$x$
is adjacent to
$y$
.

Now, we use
Menger’s theorem
which is the following:

Menger’s Theorem:
A graph is
$k$
-vertex-connected if and only if every pair of vertices has at least
$k$
internally disjoint paths in between.

Menger’s Theorem:
A graph is
$k$
-vertex-connected if and only if every pair of vertices has at least
$k$
internally disjoint paths in between.

We do not have a cut-vertex, is the same as saying, we are
$2$
-vertex-connected. Hence, we observe that we have at least
$2$
internally disjoint path between
$v_0,v_d$
, which means the new path does not use the vertices
$v_1,\ldots,v_{d-1}$
. Now, observe that this path has be at least length
$d$
, why? Because, otherwise
$d(v_0,v_d)\leq d-1$
. Hence, suppose the new path is
$v_0,u_1,\ldots,u_{d-1},v_d$
. Now, we concatanete the two paths to get the
$2d$
-cycle: namely,
$v_0,v_1,\ldots,v_{d-1},v_d,u_{d-1},\ldots,u_1,v_0$
. Thus finishes the proof conditional on the assumption that we have no cut-vertex.

Now, let us prove that claim.

Suppose there is a cut-vertex
$v$
, so that it disconnects the graph into two parts:
$G\setminus \{v\}=V_1\cup V_2$
and
$V_1\cap V_2=\emptyset$
, and we need both to be non-empty of course. Now, let
$u$
be a vertex such that
$d(v,u)=d$
and let
$v\in V_1$
. Picking a vertex in
$t\in V_2$
, we observe that the distance from
$v$
to
$t$
is greater than
$d$
. This contradicts the fact that the diameter was
$d$
. Hence, this finishes the proof of the claim.

Now, to come to your first “why?”: Suppose you have
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)\leq d$
. Then, since you know this path must through the vertex
$u$
(there is no other way to reach
$v$
), this implies
$\operatorname{dist}(u,w)\leq d$
, which is a contradiction. Hence,
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)\geq d+1$
, but using
$u-w$
path, we already have a path of distance exactly
$d+1$
, so
$\operatorname{dist}(v,w)=d+1$
.

title: Oscillatory integral with nonstationary phase
question:
Let

$$I(\lambda) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^n} e^{i \lambda \phi(\xi)} a(\xi) d\xi, $$


be a general oscillatory integral, with
$\phi \in C^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)$
and
$a \in C^\infty_c(\mathbb{R}^n)$
. The method of non-stationary phase says that if
$\phi$
has no critical points in the support of
$a$
, then
$I$
decays fast at infinity, i.e.


$$I(\lambda) = O(\lambda^{-N}), $$


for every
$N \geq 0$
. This is obtained in general using repeated integration by parts. In what follows, I will always assume that
$\phi$
has no critical point. My question are:




What can we say about the case
$a = 1$
? In other words, if the phase has no critical points on the whole space, can we still get the same decay?


What can we say about the case when the amplitude is replaced by
$1-a$
?


There exists any possibility to have an asymptotic expansion as
$\lambda \to \infty$
, as in the case when the phase has a critical point?

I think this cases with non-compact amplitude can be treated by Littlewood-Paley partition of unity but I was not able find a satisfactory answer yet. Thank you for your help.
answer:
$1.$
When
$a=1$
we have

$$I(\lambda) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^n} e^{i \lambda \phi(\xi)}d\xi, $$

Take
$\phi(\xi)=\xi$
and
$n=1$
. This is simply the Fourier transform of
$1$
which we know is Dirac delta distribution. So there is no constant
$C$
that you can find s.t.
$\delta(\xi)\leq C\lambda^{-N}$
. However this holds outside
$0$
.

Take
$\phi(\xi)=e^\xi$
and
$n=1$
. Then the integral is

$$I(\lambda) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} e^{i \lambda e^\xi}d\xi= \int_{0}^\infty \frac{1}{x}e^{i \lambda x}dx $$

This is probably
$\infty$
for all
$\lambda$
.

$2.$
Same thing as case
$1.$
. Outside of
$a$
support we will have
$1$
so...

$3.5$
I will take a detour to provide some context. The answer kind of lies in the proof of the statement provided by the op. Below are the hints/ideas for the proof.

Idea for
$n=1$
:
Note that
$e^{i\lambda\phi(\xi)}a(\xi)=\frac{a(\xi)}{i\lambda \phi'(\xi)}\frac{d}{d\xi}e^{i\lambda\phi(\xi)}$
then use integration by parts and the properties of
$a$
and
$\phi$
. Notice that you can not use this if
$\phi'(\xi)=0$
somewhere.

Idea for any dimension:
We need to find an operator
$L$
such that
$Le^{i\lambda\phi(\xi)}=e^{i\lambda\phi(\xi)}a(\xi)$

$$L=\frac{1}{i\lambda |\nabla\phi|^2} \sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \xi_j} \frac{\partial}{\partial \xi_j}.$$

$$L=\frac{1}{i\lambda |\nabla\phi|^2} \sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \xi_j} \frac{\partial}{\partial \xi_j}.$$

Reference:
nonstationary-phase-method-for-oscillatory-integral

$3.$
Let
$n=1$
,
$a=1$
on
$[-\pi,\pi]$
and
$0$
outside. (We can just mollify this but this is much easier to work with.) And I will take
$\phi(\xi)$
to be
$\sin(\xi)$
. Without too much effort you can see that this is going to give you (2 times) Bessel function of order
$0$
. And you can see here:
Bessel
that it decays like
$O(\lambda^{1/2})$
. However no
$O(\lambda^{-N}$
.

One idea might be the following. If the effects of the critical points "cancel each other out", which depends on what
$a$
is, then you can still get a decay like above. For example, if in the above example I choose

\begin{align}
a(\xi)=
\begin{cases}
\ \ \ 1&  \text{on } \ [-\pi,\pi/4] \\
-1&  \text{on } \ [\pi/4,\pi]
\end{cases}
\end{align}

then I would say you will still have
$O(\lambda^{-N})$
.

We can also modify the proof to obtain a similarish result. Let
$\xi_1,\,\dots,\, \xi_n$
be the critical points of
$\phi$
inside the domain of
$a$
.

Remark:
We can have actually have uncountably many critical points since
$\phi$
could be a constant on a certain set. For this case we might need to consider connected sets of critical points
$\Xi_1,\, \dots,\, \Xi_n$
. But this is just a hunch.

Then we divide the integral into

$$I(\lambda) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^n-\bigcup^n B_\epsilon(x_i)} e^{i \lambda \phi(\xi)} a(\xi) d\xi + \sum^n \int_{B_\epsilon(x_i)} e^{i \lambda \phi(\xi)} a(\xi) d\xi$$

For the LHS we can apply the previous result. Hence in the end we expect

$$I(\lambda)\leq C_1(\epsilon, \lambda) \lambda^{-N}+C_2\epsilon$$

and
$C_1$
is an increasing function of both
$\epsilon$
and
$\lambda$
.

title: Asymptotic behavior of $\int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{N\sqrt{x}-x^2} \, dx$ as $N \to \infty$
question:
While working out an expected value computation, I encountered the following integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{N\sqrt{x}-x^2} \, dx$$


I am especially interested in its asymptotic behavior as
$N \to \infty$
.


For a simpler baseline, if the integral were
$\int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{Nx-x^2} \, dx$
, Wolfram|Alpha provides an exact closed-form solution:


$$\frac{1}{4} \left(2 + e^{N^2/4} N \sqrt{\pi} \left(1 + \operatorname{erf}\left(\frac{N}{2}\right)\right)\right)$$


From this, it is straightforward to see that the leading-order asymptotic term grows like
$C N e^{N^2/4}$
for some constant
$C$
.


I am looking to find a similar leading-order term (the main asymptotic behavior) for the original integral involving
$\sqrt{x}$
. Wolfram|Alpha does not return a helpful result for this case, presumably due to the lack of a closed-form expression in terms of standard special functions.

Any suggestions on how to extract the leading behavior, or references to similar integrals, would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!
answer:
Define the original integral as
$I(N) = \int_0^{\infty} x e^{\phi_N(x)}dx$
where the function
$\phi_N(x) = N\sqrt{x} - x^2$
. Then
$\phi_N'(x) = \frac{N}{2\sqrt{x}} - 2x$
and
$\phi_N''(x) = -\frac{N}{4x^{3/2}} - 2$
. This implies that the maximum
$\phi_N$
attains is
$3\left(\frac{N}{4}\right)^{4/3}$
at
$x_0=\left(\frac{N}{4}\right)^{2/3}$
(with
$\phi_N''(x_0) = -3 < 0$
). This implies that from
Laplace's method

$
I(N) \sim x_0 e^{\phi_N\left(x_0\right)} \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi}{|\phi_N''(x_0) |}}
$
. Thus,

$$
I(N) \sim \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi}{3}}\left(\frac{N}{4}\right)^{2 / 3} \exp \left(3\left(\frac{N}{4}\right)^{4 / 3}\right)\quad \text{as} \quad N \to \infty.
$$

Edit : This worked because the change of variables
$x = N^{2/3}y$
rewrites the integral in the form required by Laplace's method. Indeed
$N \sqrt{x}-x^2=N \sqrt{N^{2 / 3} y}-N^{4 / 3} y^2=N^{4 / 3}\left(\sqrt{y}-y^2\right)$
and

$$
I(N)=N^{4 / 3} \int_0^{\infty} y e^{N^{4 / 3} f(y)} d y, \quad f(y)=\sqrt{y}-y^2.$$

There may be more general conditions on
$\phi_N(x)$
under which Laplace's method can be applied. However, in our case, the easiest way to justify its use is through the scaling above.

question:

Any suggestions on how to extract the leading behavior, or references to similar integrals, would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!
answer:
$$I=\int_{0}^{\infty} x \,e^{N\sqrt{x}-x^2} \, dx$$

According to
Mathematica
, the antiderivative does not seem to exist but the definite integral is given in terms of hypergeometric functions
$_0F_2(.)$
and
$_1F_3(.)$
hypergeometric functions where the last argument is
$\frac{N^4}{256}$
(take a look
here
).

Using asymptotics, with
$\color{red}{M=\sqrt[3]{2 N^4}} $
, truncated to
$O\left(\frac{1}{M^5}\right)$
, it write

$$I\sim \frac{24}{M^3}+\sqrt{\frac{\pi M}{12}}\,\exp\Big(\frac {3M} 8\Big)\Bigg(1+\sum_{k=1}^5 \frac {a_k}{M^k}   \Bigg)$$

the coefficients being

$$\left\{\frac{7}{18},\frac{385}{648},\frac{39655}{34992},\frac{665665}{2519424},-\frac{1375739365}{45349632},-\frac{2053160864755}{9795520512}\right\}$$

For
$N=10$
, this truncated expression gives
$\color{red}{71298.}67$
(absolute error of
$0.11$
).

Edit

In fact,

$$I=\frac{24}{M^3}\Bigg(1-\sum_{k=1}^\infty (-1)^{k+1}\, \frac {\alpha_k}{M^{3k}}  \Bigg)+\sqrt{\frac{\pi M}{12}}\,\exp\Big(\frac {3M} 8\Big)\Bigg(1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac {\beta_k}{M^k}   \Bigg)$$

Limiting the expansion to
$O\left(\frac{1}{M^{20}}\right)$
, for
$N=10$
it gives
$I=\color{red}{71298.55}996$
(absolute error of
$8.19\times 10^{-4}$
).

$$I=\frac{24}{M^3}\Bigg(1-\sum_{k=1}^\infty (-1)^{k+1}\, \frac {\alpha_k}{M^{3k}}  \Bigg)+\sqrt{\frac{\pi M}{12}}\,\exp\Big(\frac {3M} 8\Big)\Bigg(1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac {\beta_k}{M^k}   \Bigg)$$

Limiting the expansion to
$O\left(\frac{1}{M^{20}}\right)$
, for
$N=10$
it gives
$I=\color{red}{71298.55}996$
(absolute error of
$8.19\times 10^{-4}$
).

title: For which $f\in A:=\mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]$ is $A/(f)$ the cohomology ring of some topological space $X$?
question:
For which homogeneous polynomials
$f \in A := \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]$
is
$A / (f)$
the cohomology ring of some topological space
$X$
, where the generators
$x_i$
have degree 2?

It is well known that the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^\infty$
is given by
$\mathbb Z[x]$
where
$x$
has degree 2, the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^n$
by
$\mathbb Z[x]/(x^{n+1})$
and (using the
Künneth theorem for cohomology
) the cohomology of a product of finitely many of them by the (graded) tensor product

$$\bigotimes_{i=1}^{n-1}H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty)\otimes H^*(\mathbb CP^{m-1})\cong \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]/(x_n^m),$$

so
$f$
can be any power of a single variable.


Moreover, we have

$$
H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty \vee \mathbb CP^\infty) \cong \mathbb Z[x,y]/(xy),
$$

so
$f$
can also be
$x_i x_j$
with
$i \neq j$
.


What other options for
$f$
are possible? As a bonus question, can we say something more generally when the
$x_i$
have arbitrary (even) degree?

Any help would be highly appreciated!
answer:
I have kind of a complicated proof of a necessary and sufficient condition for
$f$
, hopefully nothing goes wrong. I have tried to be somewhat detailed so that it's easy to verify and spot if anything is awry. (And I will add the necessity side a little later, it is a bit more involved so I need to cross check a few things).

Let's start with the simpler direction, the sufficient condition.

Theorem:

Let
$A = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
be a graded polynomial ring where each generator
$x_i$
has degree 2. Let
$f \in A$
be a homogeneous polynomial of degree
$2m$
(meaning
$m$
is the polynomial degree) that factors completely into homogeneous linear polynomials over
$\mathbb{Z}$
. Then there exists a topological space
$X$
such that
$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong A/(f)$
as graded rings.

Proof:
The infinite complex projective space
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
can be constructed as a CW complex with exactly one cell in every even dimension
$2k$
(for
$k \ge 0$
) and no cells in odd dimensions. Because the cellular chain complex has
$C_{2k} \cong \mathbb{Z}$
and
$C_{2k-1} = 0$
, all boundary maps must be zero. Therefore, the cellular cohomology (which is isomorphic to singular cohomology) is simply
$H^{2k}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$
and
$H^{2k-1}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
. Let
$x \in H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
be a choice of generator. The cup product structure gives
$H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x]$
. Define the topological space
$K$
as the Cartesian product of
$n$
copies of
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, that is,
$K = \prod_{i=1}^n \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
.

We now use the Kunneth Theorem for ordinary cohomology. Since
$H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
is a free abelian group in every degree, the torsion group
$\text{Tor}_1^\mathbb{Z}(-,-)$
vanishes completely. The exact sequence collapses, leaving an isomorphism given by the cross product. By induction on
$n$
, we obtain an isomorphism of graded rings

$$H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \bigotimes_{i=1}^n H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \bigotimes_{i=1}^n \mathbb{Z}[x_i] \cong \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$$

Since
$H^{\text{odd}}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
, any tensor product of terms resulting in an odd total degree must contain at least one factor in an odd degree, which is zero. Thus,
$H^{\text{odd}}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

Let us now focus on how to use
$f$
. By hypothesis,
$f = L_1 L_2 \dots L_m$
, where each
$L_j$
is a homogeneous linear polynomial in
$\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. Thus,
$L_j \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
. The space
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the classifying space
$BU(1)$
for principal
$U(1)$
-bundles, which equivalently classify complex line bundles. There is a universal complex line bundle
$\gamma^1 \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. The first Chern class
$c_1(\gamma^1)$
generates
$H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
.

It is known that for any paracompact Hausdorff space (such as the CW-complex
$K$
), the set of isomorphism classes of complex line bundles over
$K$
is in natural bijection with the set of homotopy classes of maps
$[K, \mathbb{CP}^\infty]$
. Furthermore, the map
$[K, \mathbb{CP}^\infty] \to H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
given by
$[g] \mapsto g^*(x)$
is a group isomorphism (see for example
here
or example 3.8
here
). Therefore, for each linear form
$L_j \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
, there exists a continuous map
$g_j: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
such that
$g_j^*(x) = L_j$
.

We define the complex line bundle
$\mathcal{L}_j \to K$
as the pullback bundle
$\mathcal{L}_j = g_j^*(\gamma^1)$
. By the naturality of Chern classes

$$c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = c_1(g_j^*(\gamma^1)) = g_j^*(c_1(\gamma^1)) = g_j^*(x) = L_j$$

Define the complex vector bundle
$E \to K$
as the Whitney sum
$E = \mathcal{L}_1 \oplus \mathcal{L}_2 \oplus \dots \oplus \mathcal{L}_m$
. Then
$E$
has complex rank
$m$
, and thus it is an oriented real vector bundle of rank
$2m$
. By the Whitney product formula for Chern classes, the total Chern class
$c(E) \in H^*(K; \mathbb{Z})$
is

$$c(E) = c(\mathcal{L}_1) \smile c(\mathcal{L}_2) \smile \dots \smile c(\mathcal{L}_m)$$

Since
$c(\mathcal{L}_j) = 1 + c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = 1 + L_j$
, we have

$$c(E) = \prod_{j=1}^m (1 + L_j) = 1 + \dots + \prod_{j=1}^m L_j$$

The top Chern class
$c_m(E)$
is exactly the term of degree
$2m$
, which is
$\prod_{j=1}^m L_j = f$
. Since for a complex vector bundle, the top Chern class is equal to the Euler class
$e(E)$
of the underlying oriented real vector bundle, hence
$e(E) = f \in H^{2m}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
.

We now equip
$E$
with a Hermitian metric. Let
$D(E)$
be the closed unit disk bundle and
$S(E)$
be the unit sphere bundle. Note that
$S(E)$
is the space
$X$
we are trying to construct. The bundle projection
$\pi: D(E) \to K$
is a homotopy equivalence (since the fibers are contractible disks).

Because
$E$
is an oriented real vector bundle of rank
$2m$
, the Thom Isomorphism Theorem states there exists a unique class
$U \in H^{2m}(D(E), S(E); \mathbb{Z})$
(the Thom class) such that the map

$$\Phi: H^k(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^{k+2m}(D(E), S(E); \mathbb{Z})$$

defined by
$\Phi(\alpha) = (\pi^*\alpha) \smile U$
is an isomorphism for all
$k$
.

Consider the long exact sequence in cohomology for the pair
$(D(E), S(E))$

$$\cdots \to H^k(D(E), S(E)) \xrightarrow{j^*} H^k(D(E)) \xrightarrow{i^*} H^k(S(E)) \xrightarrow{\delta} H^{k+1}(D(E), S(E)) \to \cdots$$

where
$j: D(E) \hookrightarrow (D(E), S(E))$
and
$i: S(E) \hookrightarrow D(E)$
are the natural inclusions. We apply three substitutions to this sequence:

Using the Thom isomorphism, we replace
$H^k(D(E), S(E))$
with
$H^{k-2m}(K)$
.


Because
$\pi: D(E) \to K$
is a homotopy equivalence,
$\pi^*: H^k(K) \to H^k(D(E))$
is an isomorphism. We replace
$H^k(D(E))$
with
$H^k(K)$
.


Let
$X = S(E)$
. We define
$\pi_X = \pi \circ i: X \to K$
.

Let's analyze the map corresponding to
$j^*$
under these isomorphisms. For any
$\alpha \in H^{k-2m}(K)$
, its image under the Thom isomorphism is
$(\pi^*\alpha) \smile U$
. Applying
$j^*$
yields

$$j^*( (\pi^*\alpha) \smile U ) = (\pi^*\alpha) \smile j^*U$$

By definition of the Euler class, it is the pullback of the Thom class along the zero section (or the disk bundle, up to homotopy equivalence). So
$e(E) = z^*(j^*U)$
implies
$j^*U = \pi^*(e(E))$
by applying
$\pi^*$
to both sides. So, the image is
$(\pi^*\alpha) \smile \pi^*(e(E)) = \pi^*(\alpha \smile e(E))$
. Under the identification
$H^*(D(E)) \cong H^*(K)$
via
$\pi^*$
, this map is exactly the cup product with
$e(E)$

$$\alpha \mapsto \alpha \smile e(E)$$

Substituting these identifications into the long exact sequence yields the Gysin sequence

$$\cdots \to H^{k-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile e(E)} H^k(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^k(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{k-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to \cdots$$

Substitute
$e(E) = f$
into the Gysin sequence. Let
$A = H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. We established previously that
$H^{\text{odd}}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

To determine the odd cohomology of
$X$
, we examine the exact Gysin sequence terminating at
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z})$
and extending one map past it.Substitute
$k = 2r+1$
into the sequence

$$H^{2r+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

Because
$K = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^n$
, its cohomology vanishes in all odd degrees. Therefore,
$H^{2r+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
. The map
$(\smile f)$
is given by polynomial multiplication by
$f$
in the ring
$A = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. Because
$A$
is an integral domain and
$f \neq 0$
, multiplication by
$f$
cannot annihilate any non-zero polynomial. Therefore, the map
$(\smile f)$
is injective, which means
$\ker(\smile f) = 0$
.

Substituting the zero into the exact sequence, we get

$$0 \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

Then exactness at
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z})$
gives
$\ker(\delta') = \text{im}(\pi_X^*)$
and since the domain of
$\pi_X^*$
is
$0$
, its image is
$0$
. Thus
$\ker(\delta') = 0$
, meaning
$\delta'$
is an injective map. Similarly, exactness at
$H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
gives
$\text{im}(\delta') = \ker(\smile f)$
. Since
$\ker(\smile f) = 0$
therefore
$\text{im}(\delta') = 0$
. These two facts imply that
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

Now consider the segment for an even degree
$k = 2r$

$$H^{2r-1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

We know
$H^{2r-1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
and
$H^{2r-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
(since
$K$
has no odd cohomology). The sequence collapses into a family of short exact sequences for each even integer
$2r$

$$0 \to H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to 0$$

We are almost there! The map
$\pi_X: X \to K$
is a continuous map of topological spaces, so the induced map on total cohomology:
$$\pi_X^*: H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^*(X; \mathbb{Z})$$
is a homomorphism of graded rings.

Because the sequence

$$H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to 0$$

is exact for every even degree
$2r$
, the map
$\pi_X^*$
is surjective in all even degrees. Since
$H^{\text{odd}}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
,
$\pi_X^*$
is a surjective ring homomorphism.

By exactness at
$H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
, the kernel of
$\pi_X^*$
in degree
$2r$
is exactly the image of the cup product with
$f$

$$\ker(\pi_X^*) \cap H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \{ \alpha \smile f \mid \alpha \in H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \}$$

Since
$\alpha \smile f$
is simply polynomial multiplication in
$A$
, taking the direct sum over all degrees implies that the full kernel of the ring homomorphism
$\pi_X^*$
is the set of all polynomial multiples of
$f$
. By definition, this is exactly the principal ideal
$(f)$
generated by
$f$
in the ring
$A$
, that is,
$\ker(\pi_X^*) = (f)$
.

By the first isomorphism theorem for rings, we have an isomorphism of graded rings

$$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) / \ker(\pi_X^*) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n] / (f)$$

where the grading is preserved since
$\pi_X^*$
preserves degrees and
$f$
is homogeneous.
$\square$

It's quite instructive to see this construction in action. Let's do a few examples.

Example 1:
Let's do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x], f=x^m$
.

Our base space is
$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, which has
$H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Z}[x]$
. Since
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the classifying space
$BS^1$
for principal
$S^1$
-bundles, we can construct it explicitly by taking the infinite-dimensional unit sphere
$S^\infty \subset \mathbb{C}^\infty$
. The space
$S^\infty$
is strictly contractible. The circle group
$S^1 = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} \mid |\lambda| = 1 \}$
acts freely on
$S^\infty$
via coordinate-wise scalar multiplication

$$\lambda \cdot (w_1, w_2, \dots) = (\lambda w_1, \lambda w_2, \dots)$$

The quotient space of this free action is
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty = S^\infty / S^1$
. The projection map
$S^\infty \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the universal principal
$S^1$
-bundle, often denoted
$ES^1 \to BS^1$
.

In our construction, the polynomial
$f = x^m$
factors into exactly
$m$
copies of the linear form
$x$
. For the linear form
$x$
, our proof instructs us to take a line bundle
$\gamma \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
with
$c_1(\gamma) = x$
. This is the standard universal complex line bundle. Geometrically,
$\gamma$
is constructed as the associated vector bundle via the Borel construction
$\gamma = S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}$
. Here, we take the product
$S^\infty \times \mathbb{C}$
and quotient out by the diagonal
$S^1$
-action
$\lambda \cdot (w, z) = (\lambda w, \lambda z)$
. A point in
$\gamma$
is an equivalence class
$[w, z]$
.

Our proof instructs us to now take the Whitney sum of
$m$
copies of this line bundle

$$E = \gamma \oplus \gamma \oplus \dots \oplus \gamma \quad \text{(} m \text{ times)}$$

By the properties of associated bundles, taking the Whitney sum of
$m$
copies of
$S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}$
is equivalent to using the
$m$
-dimensional complex representation
$E = S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}^m$
where the
$S^1$
-action on
$\mathbb{C}^m$
is
$\lambda \cdot (z_1, \dots, z_m) = (\lambda z_1, \dots, \lambda z_m)$
. Next, our proof tells us to take
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. The unit sphere in the fiber
$\mathbb{C}^m$
is exactly the standard real sphere
$S^{2m-1}$
. Restricting the fibers of
$E$
from
$\mathbb{C}^m$
to
$S^{2m-1}$
yields
$X = S^\infty \times_{S^1} S^{2m-1}$
.

We now have an explicit geometric model for our constructed space
$X$
. To see what this space actually is, we look at the natural projection map to the second factor

$$\pi_2: S^\infty \times S^{2m-1} \to S^{2m-1}$$

defined by
$\pi_2(w, z) = z$
.Because the
$S^1$
-action on
$S^\infty \times S^{2m-1}$
is diagonal (
$\lambda \cdot (w, z) = (\lambda w, \lambda z)$
), this projection map is
$S^1$
-equivariant. Therefore, it descends to a well-defined continuous map between the quotient spaces

$$\overline{\pi_2}: \left( S^\infty \times_{S^1} S^{2m-1} \right) \to \left( S^{2m-1} / S^1 \right)$$
$$[w, z] \mapsto [z]$$

Now the space
$S^{2m-1} / S^1$
under scalar multiplication is just taking the unit sphere in
$\mathbb{C}^m$
and quotienting by the phase
$S^1$
, and is the exact geometric construction of the complex projective space
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. The preimage of
$[z]$
consists of all equivalence classes
$[w, \lambda z]$
for all possible
$\lambda \in S^1$
and
$w \in S^\infty$
. By the definition of our equivalence relation,
$[w, \lambda z] = [\lambda^{-1} w, z]$
. Since
$\lambda^{-1} w$
traces out the entirety of
$S^\infty$
(because
$S^1$
acts transitively on the
$S^1$
-orbits of
$S^\infty$
), the fiber over
$[z]$
is exactly homeomorphic to
$S^\infty$
.Thus we have a fiber bundle

$$S^\infty \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\overline{\pi_2}} \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

Because
$S^\infty$
is contractible (all its homotopy groups are trivial), the long exact sequence of homotopy groups for this fibration collapses. This guarantees that
$\overline{\pi_2}$
induces an isomorphism on all homotopy groups. By Whitehead's Theorem (since these spaces have the homotopy type of CW complexes),
$\overline{\pi_2}$
is a homotopy equivalence.

The space
$X = S(E)$
generated by our construction is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. Because homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic cohomology rings, we obtain:
$$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong H^*(\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^m)$$

Example 2:
Let us now do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n], f=x_n^m$
.

Our base space is the
$n$
-fold Cartesian product:
$$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \quad (n \text{ times})$$
Let's split this product to isolate the
$n$
-th factor. Let
$K' = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1}$
, so that
$K = K' \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
.

Let
$p_n: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the standard projection map onto the final factor. In cohomology, the generator
$x_n \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
is defined exactly as the pullback of the generator
$x \in H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
along this projection, this,
$x_n = p_n^*(x)$
.

Our polynomial is
$f = x_n^m$
, which means we have
$m$
identical linear forms
$L_1 = \dots = L_m = x_n$
. According to our construction, we need
$m$
line bundles
$\mathcal{L}_j \to K$
such that
$c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = x_n$
. Let
$\gamma \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the universal line bundle (which has
$c_1(\gamma) = x$
). By the naturality of Chern classes, pulling back
$\gamma$
along
$p_n$
gives a line bundle over
$K$
with the Chern class

$$c_1(p_n^*(\gamma)) = p_n^*(c_1(\gamma)) = p_n^*(x) = x_n$$

Thus,
$\mathcal{L}_j = p_n^*(\gamma)$
for all
$j=1, \dots, m$
. Our vector bundle
$E \to K$
is the Whitney sum of these
$m$
identical line bundles

$$E = p_n^*(\gamma) \oplus p_n^*(\gamma) \oplus \dots \oplus p_n^*(\gamma)$$

Because pullbacks commute with Whitney sums, we can factor the pullback map out

$$E = p_n^*(\gamma \oplus \dots \oplus \gamma) = p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})$$

We now need to build our space
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. Since
$E = p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})$
, we are taking the sphere bundle of a pullback. Since the sphere bundle of a pullback is canonically homeomorphic to the pullback of the sphere bundle
$$X = S(p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})) \cong p_n^*(S(\gamma^{\oplus m}))$$

What is this geometrically? Suppose we have a fiber bundle
$B \to Y$
, and we want to pull it back over a product space
$Z \times Y$
along the projection map
$\pi_Y: Z \times Y \to Y$
. By definition, the fiber of the pullback at a point
$(z, y)$
is exactly the fiber of
$B$
at
$y$
. The coordinate
$z$
just comes along for the ride. Therefore, the total space of the pullback bundle is exactly the Cartesian product
$Z \times B$
. Applying this to our situation, we have that the target space
$Y$
is
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, the "bystander" space
$Z$
is
$K' = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1}$
, the projection map is
$p_n: K' \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, and the bundle
$B \to Y$
is
$S(\gamma^{\oplus m}) \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. By the Cartesian pullback property, the total space of our pullback bundle is

$$X \cong K' \times S(\gamma^{\oplus m}) = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1} \times S(\gamma^{\oplus m})$$

In Example 1, we demonstrated that the space
$S(\gamma^{\oplus m})$
is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. Substituting this in, we find that our constructed space satisfies
$$X \simeq (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1} \times \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

Both of these examples reconstruct the known examples from the post. As a bonus, let's do one more example.

Example 3:
Let us try to do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n]$
,
$f = x_1^{e_1} \dots x_n^{e_n},\ e_i\ge 0$
.

Our base space is the Cartesian product
$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
(
$n$
times). Let
$p_i: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the projection onto the
$i$
-th factor.

The polynomial
$f$
factors into
$e_1$
copies of
$x_1$
,
$e_2$
copies of
$x_2$
, and so on. Our construction instructs us to build the vector bundle
$E \to K$
as the Whitney sum of the corresponding pullbacks of the universal line bundle
$\gamma$

$$E = p_1^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_1}) \oplus p_2^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_2}) \oplus \dots \oplus p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_n})$$

To understand
$E$
geometrically, let
$V_i$
denote the total space of the vector bundle
$\gamma^{\oplus e_i} \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. Then the pullback
$p_i^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_i})$
has total space

$$\{(x,v_i)\in K\times V_i\mid p_i(x)=\pi_i(v_i)\}$$

and a point of the Whitney sum
$\oplus_{i=1}^n p_i^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_i})$
is a tuple
$((x,v_1),\dots, (x,v_n))$
with a common basepoint
$x\in K$
. The map

$$((x,v_1),\dots,(x,v_n))\mapsto (v_1,\dots,v_n)$$

is a strict homeomorphism. To see this, we can construct its inverse. Because
$x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in K$
, the pullback condition
$p_i(x) = \pi_i(v_i)$
explicitly forces
$x_i = \pi_i(v_i)$
. Thus, setting
$x =(\pi_1(v_1),\dots,\pi_n(v_n))$
, the inverse is given exactly by

$$(v_1,\dots,v_n)\mapsto ((x, v_1),\dots,(x, v_n))$$

Therefore, the total space of
$E$
is canonically homeomorphic to the Cartesian product:
$$E_{\text{total}} \cong V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$$

Our construction outputs the topological space
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. The observation we will use here is that the unit sphere bundle of any vector bundle is a strong deformation retract of the punctured total space (the total space with the zero-section removed). The retraction is given simply by dividing every non-zero vector by its norm
$v \mapsto v/|v|$
. Therefore,
$X$
is homotopy equivalent to
$E_{\text{total}} \setminus \{0_E\}$
. What is the zero-section
$0_E$
? It is the Cartesian product of the zero-sections of each individual bundle

$$0_E = 0_{V_1} \times 0_{V_2} \times \dots \times 0_{V_n}$$

Thus, a point
$(v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n) \in E_{\text{total}}$
survives in our punctured space if and only if at least one
$v_i$
is not zero. We can express this "at least one non-zero" condition as a union of open subsets.Let
$U_i$
be the subspace of
$E_{\text{total}}$
where the
$i$
-th coordinate is strictly non-zero (
$v_i \neq 0_{V_i}$
)

$$U_i = V_1 \times \dots \times (V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}) \times \dots \times V_n$$

By definition, the punctured total space is exactly the union of these regions

$$X \simeq E_{\text{total}} \setminus \{0_E\} = \bigcup_{i=1}^n U_i$$

Consider the pair of spaces
$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i})$
for each coordinate. The total space
$V_i$
of a vector bundle deformation retracts to its zero section, meaning
$V_i \simeq \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. As shown in Example 1, the punctured vector bundle
$V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}$
deformation retracts to its unit sphere bundle, which is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1}$
.
(A small note: If
$e_i = 0$
, the bundle has rank 0, so the punctured space is the empty set
$\emptyset$
.)

Thus, we have a homotopy equivalence of pairs of CW-complexes

$$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}) \simeq (\mathbb{CP}^\infty, \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1})$$

Replacing each
$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i})$
by the homotopy equivalent
$(\mathbb{CP}^\infty, \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1})$
, the resulting diagram has cofibration inclusions, since each such pair is a CW-pair. Therefore the finite union is built by iterated adjunctions along closed cofibrations. By the Gluing Theorem for adjunction spaces (see Theorem 7.5.7 in Brown's
Topology and Groupoids
), gluing spaces along such cofibrations preserves object-wise homotopy equivalences. This yields

$$X \simeq \bigcup_{i=1}^n \left( \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1} \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \right)$$

Just as sanity checks:

If
$f = x_n^m$
, then
$e_n = m$
, and all other
$e_i = 0$
. Because
$e_i = 0$
yields the empty set for all regions
$U_i$
except the
$n$
-th one, the massive union collapses into a single term

$$X \simeq \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

If
$f = x_1 x_2$
, then
$e_1 = 1, e_2 = 1$
. The dimensions are
$\mathbb{CP}^{1-1} = \mathbb{CP}^0 = \{*\}$
(a single point).The union is

$$X \simeq (\{*\} \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty) \cup (\mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \{*\})$$

conversation.content:
For which homogeneous polynomials
$f \in A := \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]$
is
$A / (f)$
the cohomology ring of some topological space
$X$
, where the generators
$x_i$
have degree 2?

It is well known that the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^\infty$
is given by
$\mathbb Z[x]$
where
$x$
has degree 2, the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^n$
by
$\mathbb Z[x]/(x^{n+1})$
and (using the
Künneth theorem for cohomology
) the cohomology of a product of finitely many of them by the (graded) tensor product

$$\bigotimes_{i=1}^{n-1}H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty)\otimes H^*(\mathbb CP^{m-1})\cong \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]/(x_n^m),$$

so
$f$
can be any power of a single variable.


Moreover, we have

$$
H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty \vee \mathbb CP^\infty) \cong \mathbb Z[x,y]/(xy),
$$

so
$f$
can also be
$x_i x_j$
with
$i \neq j$
.


What other options for
$f$
are possible? As a bonus question, can we say something more generally when the
$x_i$
have arbitrary (even) degree?

Any help would be highly appreciated!

I have kind of a complicated proof of a necessary and sufficient condition for
$f$
, hopefully nothing goes wrong. I have tried to be somewhat detailed so that it's easy to verify and spot if anything is awry. (And I will add the necessity side a little later, it is a bit more involved so I need to cross check a few things).

Let's start with the simpler direction, the sufficient condition.

Theorem:

Let
$A = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
be a graded polynomial ring where each generator
$x_i$
has degree 2. Let
$f \in A$
be a homogeneous polynomial of degree
$2m$
(meaning
$m$
is the polynomial degree) that factors completely into homogeneous linear polynomials over
$\mathbb{Z}$
. Then there exists a topological space
$X$
such that
$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong A/(f)$
as graded rings.

Proof:
The infinite complex projective space
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
can be constructed as a CW complex with exactly one cell in every even dimension
$2k$
(for
$k \ge 0$
) and no cells in odd dimensions. Because the cellular chain complex has
$C_{2k} \cong \mathbb{Z}$
and
$C_{2k-1} = 0$
, all boundary maps must be zero. Therefore, the cellular cohomology (which is isomorphic to singular cohomology) is simply
$H^{2k}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$
and
$H^{2k-1}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
. Let
$x \in H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
be a choice of generator. The cup product structure gives
$H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x]$
. Define the topological space
$K$
as the Cartesian product of
$n$
copies of
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, that is,
$K = \prod_{i=1}^n \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
.

We now use the Kunneth Theorem for ordinary cohomology. Since
$H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
is a free abelian group in every degree, the torsion group
$\text{Tor}_1^\mathbb{Z}(-,-)$
vanishes completely. The exact sequence collapses, leaving an isomorphism given by the cross product. By induction on
$n$
, we obtain an isomorphism of graded rings

$$H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \bigotimes_{i=1}^n H^*(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \bigotimes_{i=1}^n \mathbb{Z}[x_i] \cong \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$$

Since
$H^{\text{odd}}(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
, any tensor product of terms resulting in an odd total degree must contain at least one factor in an odd degree, which is zero. Thus,
$H^{\text{odd}}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

Let us now focus on how to use
$f$
. By hypothesis,
$f = L_1 L_2 \dots L_m$
, where each
$L_j$
is a homogeneous linear polynomial in
$\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. Thus,
$L_j \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
. The space
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the classifying space
$BU(1)$
for principal
$U(1)$
-bundles, which equivalently classify complex line bundles. There is a universal complex line bundle
$\gamma^1 \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. The first Chern class
$c_1(\gamma^1)$
generates
$H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
.

It is known that for any paracompact Hausdorff space (such as the CW-complex
$K$
), the set of isomorphism classes of complex line bundles over
$K$
is in natural bijection with the set of homotopy classes of maps
$[K, \mathbb{CP}^\infty]$
. Furthermore, the map
$[K, \mathbb{CP}^\infty] \to H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
given by
$[g] \mapsto g^*(x)$
is a group isomorphism (see for example
here
or example 3.8
here
). Therefore, for each linear form
$L_j \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
, there exists a continuous map
$g_j: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
such that
$g_j^*(x) = L_j$
.

We define the complex line bundle
$\mathcal{L}_j \to K$
as the pullback bundle
$\mathcal{L}_j = g_j^*(\gamma^1)$
. By the naturality of Chern classes

$$c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = c_1(g_j^*(\gamma^1)) = g_j^*(c_1(\gamma^1)) = g_j^*(x) = L_j$$

Define the complex vector bundle
$E \to K$
as the Whitney sum
$E = \mathcal{L}_1 \oplus \mathcal{L}_2 \oplus \dots \oplus \mathcal{L}_m$
. Then
$E$
has complex rank
$m$
, and thus it is an oriented real vector bundle of rank
$2m$
. By the Whitney product formula for Chern classes, the total Chern class
$c(E) \in H^*(K; \mathbb{Z})$
is

$$c(E) = c(\mathcal{L}_1) \smile c(\mathcal{L}_2) \smile \dots \smile c(\mathcal{L}_m)$$

Since
$c(\mathcal{L}_j) = 1 + c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = 1 + L_j$
, we have

$$c(E) = \prod_{j=1}^m (1 + L_j) = 1 + \dots + \prod_{j=1}^m L_j$$

The top Chern class
$c_m(E)$
is exactly the term of degree
$2m$
, which is
$\prod_{j=1}^m L_j = f$
. Since for a complex vector bundle, the top Chern class is equal to the Euler class
$e(E)$
of the underlying oriented real vector bundle, hence
$e(E) = f \in H^{2m}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
.

We now equip
$E$
with a Hermitian metric. Let
$D(E)$
be the closed unit disk bundle and
$S(E)$
be the unit sphere bundle. Note that
$S(E)$
is the space
$X$
we are trying to construct. The bundle projection
$\pi: D(E) \to K$
is a homotopy equivalence (since the fibers are contractible disks).

Because
$E$
is an oriented real vector bundle of rank
$2m$
, the Thom Isomorphism Theorem states there exists a unique class
$U \in H^{2m}(D(E), S(E); \mathbb{Z})$
(the Thom class) such that the map

$$\Phi: H^k(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^{k+2m}(D(E), S(E); \mathbb{Z})$$

defined by
$\Phi(\alpha) = (\pi^*\alpha) \smile U$
is an isomorphism for all
$k$
.

Consider the long exact sequence in cohomology for the pair
$(D(E), S(E))$

$$\cdots \to H^k(D(E), S(E)) \xrightarrow{j^*} H^k(D(E)) \xrightarrow{i^*} H^k(S(E)) \xrightarrow{\delta} H^{k+1}(D(E), S(E)) \to \cdots$$

where
$j: D(E) \hookrightarrow (D(E), S(E))$
and
$i: S(E) \hookrightarrow D(E)$
are the natural inclusions. We apply three substitutions to this sequence:

Using the Thom isomorphism, we replace
$H^k(D(E), S(E))$
with
$H^{k-2m}(K)$
.


Because
$\pi: D(E) \to K$
is a homotopy equivalence,
$\pi^*: H^k(K) \to H^k(D(E))$
is an isomorphism. We replace
$H^k(D(E))$
with
$H^k(K)$
.


Let
$X = S(E)$
. We define
$\pi_X = \pi \circ i: X \to K$
.

Let's analyze the map corresponding to
$j^*$
under these isomorphisms. For any
$\alpha \in H^{k-2m}(K)$
, its image under the Thom isomorphism is
$(\pi^*\alpha) \smile U$
. Applying
$j^*$
yields

$$j^*( (\pi^*\alpha) \smile U ) = (\pi^*\alpha) \smile j^*U$$

By definition of the Euler class, it is the pullback of the Thom class along the zero section (or the disk bundle, up to homotopy equivalence). So
$e(E) = z^*(j^*U)$
implies
$j^*U = \pi^*(e(E))$
by applying
$\pi^*$
to both sides. So, the image is
$(\pi^*\alpha) \smile \pi^*(e(E)) = \pi^*(\alpha \smile e(E))$
. Under the identification
$H^*(D(E)) \cong H^*(K)$
via
$\pi^*$
, this map is exactly the cup product with
$e(E)$

$$\alpha \mapsto \alpha \smile e(E)$$

Substituting these identifications into the long exact sequence yields the Gysin sequence

$$\cdots \to H^{k-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile e(E)} H^k(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^k(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{k-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to \cdots$$

Substitute
$e(E) = f$
into the Gysin sequence. Let
$A = H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. We established previously that
$H^{\text{odd}}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

To determine the odd cohomology of
$X$
, we examine the exact Gysin sequence terminating at
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z})$
and extending one map past it.Substitute
$k = 2r+1$
into the sequence

$$H^{2r+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

Because
$K = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^n$
, its cohomology vanishes in all odd degrees. Therefore,
$H^{2r+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
. The map
$(\smile f)$
is given by polynomial multiplication by
$f$
in the ring
$A = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. Because
$A$
is an integral domain and
$f \neq 0$
, multiplication by
$f$
cannot annihilate any non-zero polynomial. Therefore, the map
$(\smile f)$
is injective, which means
$\ker(\smile f) = 0$
.

Substituting the zero into the exact sequence, we get

$$0 \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

Then exactness at
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z})$
gives
$\ker(\delta') = \text{im}(\pi_X^*)$
and since the domain of
$\pi_X^*$
is
$0$
, its image is
$0$
. Thus
$\ker(\delta') = 0$
, meaning
$\delta'$
is an injective map. Similarly, exactness at
$H^{2r-2m+2}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
gives
$\text{im}(\delta') = \ker(\smile f)$
. Since
$\ker(\smile f) = 0$
therefore
$\text{im}(\delta') = 0$
. These two facts imply that
$H^{2r+1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
.

Now consider the segment for an even degree
$k = 2r$

$$H^{2r-1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\delta'} H^{2r-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z})$$

We know
$H^{2r-1}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
and
$H^{2r-2m+1}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
(since
$K$
has no odd cohomology). The sequence collapses into a family of short exact sequences for each even integer
$2r$

$$0 \to H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\smile f} H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to 0$$

We are almost there! The map
$\pi_X: X \to K$
is a continuous map of topological spaces, so the induced map on total cohomology:
$$\pi_X^*: H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) \to H^*(X; \mathbb{Z})$$
is a homomorphism of graded rings.

Because the sequence

$$H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \xrightarrow{\pi_X^*} H^{2r}(X; \mathbb{Z}) \to 0$$

is exact for every even degree
$2r$
, the map
$\pi_X^*$
is surjective in all even degrees. Since
$H^{\text{odd}}(X; \mathbb{Z}) = 0$
,
$\pi_X^*$
is a surjective ring homomorphism.

By exactness at
$H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z})$
, the kernel of
$\pi_X^*$
in degree
$2r$
is exactly the image of the cup product with
$f$

$$\ker(\pi_X^*) \cap H^{2r}(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \{ \alpha \smile f \mid \alpha \in H^{2r-2m}(K; \mathbb{Z}) \}$$

Since
$\alpha \smile f$
is simply polynomial multiplication in
$A$
, taking the direct sum over all degrees implies that the full kernel of the ring homomorphism
$\pi_X^*$
is the set of all polynomial multiples of
$f$
. By definition, this is exactly the principal ideal
$(f)$
generated by
$f$
in the ring
$A$
, that is,
$\ker(\pi_X^*) = (f)$
.

By the first isomorphism theorem for rings, we have an isomorphism of graded rings

$$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) / \ker(\pi_X^*) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n] / (f)$$

where the grading is preserved since
$\pi_X^*$
preserves degrees and
$f$
is homogeneous.
$\square$

It's quite instructive to see this construction in action. Let's do a few examples.

Example 1:
Let's do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x], f=x^m$
.

Our base space is
$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, which has
$H^*(K; \mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Z}[x]$
. Since
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the classifying space
$BS^1$
for principal
$S^1$
-bundles, we can construct it explicitly by taking the infinite-dimensional unit sphere
$S^\infty \subset \mathbb{C}^\infty$
. The space
$S^\infty$
is strictly contractible. The circle group
$S^1 = \{ \lambda \in \mathbb{C} \mid |\lambda| = 1 \}$
acts freely on
$S^\infty$
via coordinate-wise scalar multiplication

$$\lambda \cdot (w_1, w_2, \dots) = (\lambda w_1, \lambda w_2, \dots)$$

The quotient space of this free action is
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty = S^\infty / S^1$
. The projection map
$S^\infty \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
is the universal principal
$S^1$
-bundle, often denoted
$ES^1 \to BS^1$
.

In our construction, the polynomial
$f = x^m$
factors into exactly
$m$
copies of the linear form
$x$
. For the linear form
$x$
, our proof instructs us to take a line bundle
$\gamma \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
with
$c_1(\gamma) = x$
. This is the standard universal complex line bundle. Geometrically,
$\gamma$
is constructed as the associated vector bundle via the Borel construction
$\gamma = S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}$
. Here, we take the product
$S^\infty \times \mathbb{C}$
and quotient out by the diagonal
$S^1$
-action
$\lambda \cdot (w, z) = (\lambda w, \lambda z)$
. A point in
$\gamma$
is an equivalence class
$[w, z]$
.

Our proof instructs us to now take the Whitney sum of
$m$
copies of this line bundle

$$E = \gamma \oplus \gamma \oplus \dots \oplus \gamma \quad \text{(} m \text{ times)}$$

By the properties of associated bundles, taking the Whitney sum of
$m$
copies of
$S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}$
is equivalent to using the
$m$
-dimensional complex representation
$E = S^\infty \times_{S^1} \mathbb{C}^m$
where the
$S^1$
-action on
$\mathbb{C}^m$
is
$\lambda \cdot (z_1, \dots, z_m) = (\lambda z_1, \dots, \lambda z_m)$
. Next, our proof tells us to take
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. The unit sphere in the fiber
$\mathbb{C}^m$
is exactly the standard real sphere
$S^{2m-1}$
. Restricting the fibers of
$E$
from
$\mathbb{C}^m$
to
$S^{2m-1}$
yields
$X = S^\infty \times_{S^1} S^{2m-1}$
.

We now have an explicit geometric model for our constructed space
$X$
. To see what this space actually is, we look at the natural projection map to the second factor

$$\pi_2: S^\infty \times S^{2m-1} \to S^{2m-1}$$

defined by
$\pi_2(w, z) = z$
.Because the
$S^1$
-action on
$S^\infty \times S^{2m-1}$
is diagonal (
$\lambda \cdot (w, z) = (\lambda w, \lambda z)$
), this projection map is
$S^1$
-equivariant. Therefore, it descends to a well-defined continuous map between the quotient spaces

$$\overline{\pi_2}: \left( S^\infty \times_{S^1} S^{2m-1} \right) \to \left( S^{2m-1} / S^1 \right)$$
$$[w, z] \mapsto [z]$$

Now the space
$S^{2m-1} / S^1$
under scalar multiplication is just taking the unit sphere in
$\mathbb{C}^m$
and quotienting by the phase
$S^1$
, and is the exact geometric construction of the complex projective space
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. The preimage of
$[z]$
consists of all equivalence classes
$[w, \lambda z]$
for all possible
$\lambda \in S^1$
and
$w \in S^\infty$
. By the definition of our equivalence relation,
$[w, \lambda z] = [\lambda^{-1} w, z]$
. Since
$\lambda^{-1} w$
traces out the entirety of
$S^\infty$
(because
$S^1$
acts transitively on the
$S^1$
-orbits of
$S^\infty$
), the fiber over
$[z]$
is exactly homeomorphic to
$S^\infty$
.Thus we have a fiber bundle

$$S^\infty \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\overline{\pi_2}} \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

Because
$S^\infty$
is contractible (all its homotopy groups are trivial), the long exact sequence of homotopy groups for this fibration collapses. This guarantees that
$\overline{\pi_2}$
induces an isomorphism on all homotopy groups. By Whitehead's Theorem (since these spaces have the homotopy type of CW complexes),
$\overline{\pi_2}$
is a homotopy equivalence.

The space
$X = S(E)$
generated by our construction is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. Because homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic cohomology rings, we obtain:
$$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong H^*(\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}; \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^m)$$

Example 2:
Let us now do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n], f=x_n^m$
.

Our base space is the
$n$
-fold Cartesian product:
$$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \quad (n \text{ times})$$
Let's split this product to isolate the
$n$
-th factor. Let
$K' = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1}$
, so that
$K = K' \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
.

Let
$p_n: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the standard projection map onto the final factor. In cohomology, the generator
$x_n \in H^2(K; \mathbb{Z})$
is defined exactly as the pullback of the generator
$x \in H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty; \mathbb{Z})$
along this projection, this,
$x_n = p_n^*(x)$
.

Our polynomial is
$f = x_n^m$
, which means we have
$m$
identical linear forms
$L_1 = \dots = L_m = x_n$
. According to our construction, we need
$m$
line bundles
$\mathcal{L}_j \to K$
such that
$c_1(\mathcal{L}_j) = x_n$
. Let
$\gamma \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the universal line bundle (which has
$c_1(\gamma) = x$
). By the naturality of Chern classes, pulling back
$\gamma$
along
$p_n$
gives a line bundle over
$K$
with the Chern class

$$c_1(p_n^*(\gamma)) = p_n^*(c_1(\gamma)) = p_n^*(x) = x_n$$

Thus,
$\mathcal{L}_j = p_n^*(\gamma)$
for all
$j=1, \dots, m$
. Our vector bundle
$E \to K$
is the Whitney sum of these
$m$
identical line bundles

$$E = p_n^*(\gamma) \oplus p_n^*(\gamma) \oplus \dots \oplus p_n^*(\gamma)$$

Because pullbacks commute with Whitney sums, we can factor the pullback map out

$$E = p_n^*(\gamma \oplus \dots \oplus \gamma) = p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})$$

We now need to build our space
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. Since
$E = p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})$
, we are taking the sphere bundle of a pullback. Since the sphere bundle of a pullback is canonically homeomorphic to the pullback of the sphere bundle
$$X = S(p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus m})) \cong p_n^*(S(\gamma^{\oplus m}))$$

What is this geometrically? Suppose we have a fiber bundle
$B \to Y$
, and we want to pull it back over a product space
$Z \times Y$
along the projection map
$\pi_Y: Z \times Y \to Y$
. By definition, the fiber of the pullback at a point
$(z, y)$
is exactly the fiber of
$B$
at
$y$
. The coordinate
$z$
just comes along for the ride. Therefore, the total space of the pullback bundle is exactly the Cartesian product
$Z \times B$
. Applying this to our situation, we have that the target space
$Y$
is
$\mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, the "bystander" space
$Z$
is
$K' = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1}$
, the projection map is
$p_n: K' \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
, and the bundle
$B \to Y$
is
$S(\gamma^{\oplus m}) \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. By the Cartesian pullback property, the total space of our pullback bundle is

$$X \cong K' \times S(\gamma^{\oplus m}) = (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1} \times S(\gamma^{\oplus m})$$

In Example 1, we demonstrated that the space
$S(\gamma^{\oplus m})$
is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$
. Substituting this in, we find that our constructed space satisfies
$$X \simeq (\mathbb{CP}^\infty)^{n-1} \times \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

Both of these examples reconstruct the known examples from the post. As a bonus, let's do one more example.

Example 3:
Let us try to do it for
$A=\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n]$
,
$f = x_1^{e_1} \dots x_n^{e_n},\ e_i\ge 0$
.

Our base space is the Cartesian product
$K = \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
(
$n$
times). Let
$p_i: K \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
be the projection onto the
$i$
-th factor.

The polynomial
$f$
factors into
$e_1$
copies of
$x_1$
,
$e_2$
copies of
$x_2$
, and so on. Our construction instructs us to build the vector bundle
$E \to K$
as the Whitney sum of the corresponding pullbacks of the universal line bundle
$\gamma$

$$E = p_1^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_1}) \oplus p_2^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_2}) \oplus \dots \oplus p_n^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_n})$$

To understand
$E$
geometrically, let
$V_i$
denote the total space of the vector bundle
$\gamma^{\oplus e_i} \to \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. Then the pullback
$p_i^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_i})$
has total space

$$\{(x,v_i)\in K\times V_i\mid p_i(x)=\pi_i(v_i)\}$$

and a point of the Whitney sum
$\oplus_{i=1}^n p_i^*(\gamma^{\oplus e_i})$
is a tuple
$((x,v_1),\dots, (x,v_n))$
with a common basepoint
$x\in K$
. The map

$$((x,v_1),\dots,(x,v_n))\mapsto (v_1,\dots,v_n)$$

is a strict homeomorphism. To see this, we can construct its inverse. Because
$x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in K$
, the pullback condition
$p_i(x) = \pi_i(v_i)$
explicitly forces
$x_i = \pi_i(v_i)$
. Thus, setting
$x =(\pi_1(v_1),\dots,\pi_n(v_n))$
, the inverse is given exactly by

$$(v_1,\dots,v_n)\mapsto ((x, v_1),\dots,(x, v_n))$$

Therefore, the total space of
$E$
is canonically homeomorphic to the Cartesian product:
$$E_{\text{total}} \cong V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$$

Our construction outputs the topological space
$X = S(E)$
, the unit sphere bundle of
$E$
. The observation we will use here is that the unit sphere bundle of any vector bundle is a strong deformation retract of the punctured total space (the total space with the zero-section removed). The retraction is given simply by dividing every non-zero vector by its norm
$v \mapsto v/|v|$
. Therefore,
$X$
is homotopy equivalent to
$E_{\text{total}} \setminus \{0_E\}$
. What is the zero-section
$0_E$
? It is the Cartesian product of the zero-sections of each individual bundle

$$0_E = 0_{V_1} \times 0_{V_2} \times \dots \times 0_{V_n}$$

Thus, a point
$(v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n) \in E_{\text{total}}$
survives in our punctured space if and only if at least one
$v_i$
is not zero. We can express this "at least one non-zero" condition as a union of open subsets.Let
$U_i$
be the subspace of
$E_{\text{total}}$
where the
$i$
-th coordinate is strictly non-zero (
$v_i \neq 0_{V_i}$
)

$$U_i = V_1 \times \dots \times (V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}) \times \dots \times V_n$$

By definition, the punctured total space is exactly the union of these regions

$$X \simeq E_{\text{total}} \setminus \{0_E\} = \bigcup_{i=1}^n U_i$$

Consider the pair of spaces
$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i})$
for each coordinate. The total space
$V_i$
of a vector bundle deformation retracts to its zero section, meaning
$V_i \simeq \mathbb{CP}^\infty$
. As shown in Example 1, the punctured vector bundle
$V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}$
deformation retracts to its unit sphere bundle, which is homotopy equivalent to
$\mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1}$
.
(A small note: If
$e_i = 0$
, the bundle has rank 0, so the punctured space is the empty set
$\emptyset$
.)

Thus, we have a homotopy equivalence of pairs of CW-complexes

$$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i}) \simeq (\mathbb{CP}^\infty, \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1})$$

Replacing each
$(V_i, V_i \setminus 0_{V_i})$
by the homotopy equivalent
$(\mathbb{CP}^\infty, \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1})$
, the resulting diagram has cofibration inclusions, since each such pair is a CW-pair. Therefore the finite union is built by iterated adjunctions along closed cofibrations. By the Gluing Theorem for adjunction spaces (see Theorem 7.5.7 in Brown's
Topology and Groupoids
), gluing spaces along such cofibrations preserves object-wise homotopy equivalences. This yields

$$X \simeq \bigcup_{i=1}^n \left( \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^{e_i - 1} \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \right)$$

Just as sanity checks:

If
$f = x_n^m$
, then
$e_n = m$
, and all other
$e_i = 0$
. Because
$e_i = 0$
yields the empty set for all regions
$U_i$
except the
$n$
-th one, the massive union collapses into a single term

$$X \simeq \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \dots \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \mathbb{CP}^{m-1}$$

If
$f = x_1 x_2$
, then
$e_1 = 1, e_2 = 1$
. The dimensions are
$\mathbb{CP}^{1-1} = \mathbb{CP}^0 = \{*\}$
(a single point).The union is

$$X \simeq (\{*\} \times \mathbb{CP}^\infty) \cup (\mathbb{CP}^\infty \times \{*\})$$

title: For which $f\in A:=\mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]$ is $A/(f)$ the cohomology ring of some topological space $X$?
question:
For which homogeneous polynomials
$f \in A := \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]$
is
$A / (f)$
the cohomology ring of some topological space
$X$
, where the generators
$x_i$
have degree 2?

It is well known that the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^\infty$
is given by
$\mathbb Z[x]$
where
$x$
has degree 2, the cohomology ring of
$\mathbb CP^n$
by
$\mathbb Z[x]/(x^{n+1})$
and (using the
Künneth theorem for cohomology
) the cohomology of a product of finitely many of them by the (graded) tensor product

$$\bigotimes_{i=1}^{n-1}H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty)\otimes H^*(\mathbb CP^{m-1})\cong \mathbb Z[x_1,\dots, x_n]/(x_n^m),$$

so
$f$
can be any power of a single variable.


Moreover, we have

$$
H^*(\mathbb CP^\infty \vee \mathbb CP^\infty) \cong \mathbb Z[x,y]/(xy),
$$

so
$f$
can also be
$x_i x_j$
with
$i \neq j$
.


What other options for
$f$
are possible? As a bonus question, can we say something more generally when the
$x_i$
have arbitrary (even) degree?

Any help would be highly appreciated!
answer:
Okay, I think I have the necessity side down as well. I'll skip over the arithmetic when they are mostly computations.

Theorem:
Let
$A = \mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
be a graded polynomial ring where each variable
$x_i$
has degree 2. Suppose there exists a topological space
$X$
such that its integral cohomology ring satisfies the graded ring isomorphism
$H^*(X; \mathbb{Z}) \cong A/(f)$
where
$f \in A$
is a non-zero homogeneous polynomial. Then every irreducible factor of
$f$
in the ring
$\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
has either degree
$1$
(a linear form) or degree
$0$
(a prime number). Equivalently,
$f$
must factor over
$\mathbb{Z}$
as
$f = c \cdot L_1 L_2 \dots L_m$
, where
$c \in \mathbb{Z}$
is an integer constant and each
$L_i$
is a linear form.

At this point, I would like to point out in a couple of lines how I came up with the proof (showing a proof is fine, but I feel like having an idea of the thought process helps even more). The idea was that if the polynomial quotient ring is coming from a topological space, the ideal must be stable under Steenrod. Since we have degree 2 generators, they look like derivations when looking modulo a prime. It's immediately clear that linear forms survive... and at this point, I went hunting for a proof that higher degree factors would lead to a contradiction. It took some time to realise that Chebotarev gives a proof if I am not being stubborn and just note that I can make the argument go through via
almost all primes
.

Anyways, the high level overview of the proof is basically as follows. Let
$c$
be the content of
$f$
. Consider a prime
$p$
that does not divide
$c$
. Then it's easy to see that
$A/(f)$
contains no
$p$
-torsion. An application of UCT then gives that
$H^*(X; \mathbb{F}_p) \cong \mathbb{F}_p[x_1, \dots, x_n] / (\bar{f})\cong \mathbb{F}_p[x_1, \dots, x_n] / (\bar{f'})$
where
$\bar{f}$
is
$f$
modulo
$p$
and
$\bar{f} = \bar{c} \cdot \bar{f'}$
. Now since we are working mod
$p$
, we can apply Steenrod operations to show that we can get hold of a derivation
$D_p$
such that
$\bar{f'}$
divides
$D_p(\bar{f'})$
in
$\mathbb{F}_p[x_1, \dots, x_n]$
. With these all set up, we assume (on the contrary) that
$f'$
contains an irreducible factor of degree
$2$
or more. A Bézout-style argument then shows that
$\bar{P}$
divides
$D_p(\bar{P}) \pmod p$
