title: Finding the probability density function for a range
question:
Problem:

Let
$Y_1,Y_2,..Y_n$
denote a random sample from the uniform distribution
$f(y) = 1$
,

$ 0 \leq y \leq 1$
. Find the probability density function for the range
$R = Y_{(n)} - Y_{(1)}$
.

Answer:

Note: In the book's notation
$Y_{(1)}$
is the smallest element in the list and
$Y_{(n)}$
is
the largest element in the list.

The following was proved in the text:

$$P( Y_{(1)} \leq y ) = 1 - \left(1 - F(y) \right)^n$$
where

$F(y)$
is the distribution function for
$y$
and
$Y_{(1)}$
is the smallest number
in the list of
$n$
numbers. Now we need to find the distribution function. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= \int_{0}^{y_0} 1 \,\, dy = y \Bigg|_{0}^{y_0} \\
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= y_0
\end{align*}

Hence our distribution function is:

$$ F(y) = \begin{cases}
0		& \text{ for } y \leq 0 \\
y		& \text{ for } 0 \leq y \leq 1 \\
1		& \text{ for } y \geq 1 \\
\end{cases} $$

Now we have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y_{(1)} \leq y ) = 1 - \left(1 - y \right)^n
\end{align*}

Now we need to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
which we will call
$g_n(y)$
. The general
formula is:

$$ g_n(y) = n \left( F(y) \right) ^{n-1}f(y) $$

Now we use this to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \left( 1 \right) \\
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \\
P( Y_{(n)} \leq y ) &= y^ n
\end{align*}

Now, I need a new approach. Let
$h$
be the probability density function for the random
value
$b - a$
. Let
$H$
be the distribution function for
$h$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 - P( b-a >= y_0 ) = \int_{b-a}^1 \,\,\, dy \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &=  y \bigg|_{b-a}^{1} = 1 - (b - a) \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 + a - b \\
\end{align*}

Now, I am thinking that this approach is wrong because
$b - a$
is not a uniformly distributed random variable. What is the right way to do this problem?


The answer is the book is:

$$ n(n-1)r^{n-2}(1-r) \text{ , } 0 \leq r \leq 1 $$
answer:
Let
$Y_1,\dots,Y_n$
be independent and identically distributed continuous random variables with common distribution function
$F$
and common density function
$f$
.

If
$1 \leq j<k \leq n$
, then the joint density of
$Y_{(j)}$
and
$Y_{(k)}$
is given by

\begin{align}
g_{(j)(k)}(y_j,y_k)
&= \frac{n!}{(j-1)!(k-1-j)!(n-k)!} [F(y_j)]^{j-1} \\
&\qquad \times [F(y_k)-F(y_j)]^{k-1-j} [1-F(y_k)]^{n-k} f(y_j)f(y_k)
\end{align}

for
$-\infty < y_j < y_k < \infty$
.

Let
$Y_1,\dots,Y_n$
be independent and identically distributed continuous random variables with common distribution function
$F$
and common density function
$f$
.

If
$1 \leq j<k \leq n$
, then the joint density of
$Y_{(j)}$
and
$Y_{(k)}$
is given by

\begin{align}
g_{(j)(k)}(y_j,y_k)
&= \frac{n!}{(j-1)!(k-1-j)!(n-k)!} [F(y_j)]^{j-1} \\
&\qquad \times [F(y_k)-F(y_j)]^{k-1-j} [1-F(y_k)]^{n-k} f(y_j)f(y_k)
\end{align}

for
$-\infty < y_j < y_k < \infty$
.

This is Theorem 6.1 (pp. 336-337) of the book you're using.

Thus, in your exercise, the joint density of
$Y_{(1)}$
and
$Y_{(n)}$
is given by

$$
g_{(1)(n)}(y_1,y_n) = n(n-1)(y_n-y_1)^{n-2}, \quad 0 \leq y_1<y_n \leq 1. \tag{1}
$$

Now, one way to compute the density of
$R = Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}$
is by first computing the joint density of
$R$
with some other variable, say,
$S = Y_{(1)}$
.

The transformation
$r=y_n-y_1$
and
$s=y_1$
has inverse
$y_1=s$
and
$y_n=r+s$
; moreover, the Jacobian is given by

$$
J = \det \begin{bmatrix}
\partial y_1/\partial r & \partial y_1/\partial s \\
\partial y_n/\partial r & \partial y_n/\partial s
\end{bmatrix} = \det \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1
\end{bmatrix} = -1.
$$

Therefore, the joint density of
$R$
and
$S$
is given by

$$
f_{R,S}(r,s) = g_{(1)(n)}(s,r+s)|{-}1|.
$$

Use the formula
$(1)$
and then compute
$f_R(r)$
from here.

$y$
and
$Y_{(1)}$
is the smallest number
in the list of
$n$
numbers. Now we need to find the distribution function. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= \int_{0}^{y_0} 1 \,\, dy = y \Bigg|_{0}^{y_0} \\
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= y_0
\end{align*}

Hence our distribution function is:

$$ F(y) = \begin{cases}
0		& \text{ for } y \leq 0 \\
y		& \text{ for } 0 \leq y \leq 1 \\
1		& \text{ for } y \geq 1 \\
\end{cases} $$

Now we have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y_{(1)} \leq y ) = 1 - \left(1 - y \right)^n
\end{align*}

Now we need to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
which we will call
$g_n(y)$
. The general
formula is:

$$ g_n(y) = n \left( F(y) \right) ^{n-1}f(y) $$

Now we use this to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \left( 1 \right) \\
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \\
P( Y_{(n)} \leq y ) &= y^ n
\end{align*}

Now, I need a new approach. Let
$h$
be the probability density function for the random
value
$b - a$
. Let
$H$
be the distribution function for
$h$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 - P( b-a >= y_0 ) = \int_{b-a}^1 \,\,\, dy \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &=  y \bigg|_{b-a}^{1} = 1 - (b - a) \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 + a - b \\
\end{align*}

Now, I am thinking that this approach is wrong because
$b - a$
is not a uniformly distributed random variable. What is the right way to do this problem?


The answer is the book is:

$$ n(n-1)r^{n-2}(1-r) \text{ , } 0 \leq r \leq 1 $$

Let
$Y_1,\dots,Y_n$
be independent and identically distributed continuous random variables with common distribution function
$F$
and common density function
$f$
.

If
$1 \leq j<k \leq n$
, then the joint density of
$Y_{(j)}$
and
$Y_{(k)}$
is given by

\begin{align}
g_{(j)(k)}(y_j,y_k)
&= \frac{n!}{(j-1)!(k-1-j)!(n-k)!} [F(y_j)]^{j-1} \\
&\qquad \times [F(y_k)-F(y_j)]^{k-1-j} [1-F(y_k)]^{n-k} f(y_j)f(y_k)
\end{align}

for
$-\infty < y_j < y_k < \infty$
.

Let
$Y_1,\dots,Y_n$
be independent and identically distributed continuous random variables with common distribution function
$F$
and common density function
$f$
.

If
$1 \leq j<k \leq n$
, then the joint density of
$Y_{(j)}$
and
$Y_{(k)}$
is given by

\begin{align}
g_{(j)(k)}(y_j,y_k)
&= \frac{n!}{(j-1)!(k-1-j)!(n-k)!} [F(y_j)]^{j-1} \\
&\qquad \times [F(y_k)-F(y_j)]^{k-1-j} [1-F(y_k)]^{n-k} f(y_j)f(y_k)
\end{align}

for
$-\infty < y_j < y_k < \infty$
.

This is Theorem 6.1 (pp. 336-337) of the book you're using.

Thus, in your exercise, the joint density of
$Y_{(1)}$
and
$Y_{(n)}$
is given by

$$
g_{(1)(n)}(y_1,y_n) = n(n-1)(y_n-y_1)^{n-2}, \quad 0 \leq y_1<y_n \leq 1. \tag{1}
$$

Now, one way to compute the density of
$R = Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}$
is by first computing the joint density of
$R$
with some other variable, say,
$S = Y_{(1)}$
.

The transformation
$r=y_n-y_1$
and
$s=y_1$
has inverse
$y_1=s$
and
$y_n=r+s$
; moreover, the Jacobian is given by

$$
J = \det \begin{bmatrix}
\partial y_1/\partial r & \partial y_1/\partial s \\
\partial y_n/\partial r & \partial y_n/\partial s
\end{bmatrix} = \det \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1
\end{bmatrix} = -1.
$$

Therefore, the joint density of
$R$
and
$S$
is given by

$$
f_{R,S}(r,s) = g_{(1)(n)}(s,r+s)|{-}1|.
$$

Use the formula
$(1)$
and then compute
$f_R(r)$
from here.

,

which we will call

\end{align*}

Now, I need a new approach. Let
$h$
be the probability density function for the random
value
$b - a$
. Let
$H$
be the distribution function for
$h$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 - P( b-a >= y_0 ) = \int_{b-a}^1 \,\,\, dy \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &=  y \bigg|_{b-a}^{1} = 1 - (b - a) \\

The answer is the book is:

$$ n(n-1)r^{n-2}(1-r) \text{ , } 0 \leq r \leq 1 $$
answer:
An informal line of reasoning to obtain the joint density of the sample minimum and sample maximum is as follows.

Consider the sample
$Y_1, \ldots, Y_n$
visualized on a number line, and pick points
$a$
and
$b$
such that
$0 \le a \le b \le 1$
. How many ways are there to arrange the points in the full interval
$[0,1]$
such that one of the observations equals
$a$
and another equals
$b$
? There are
$n(n-1)$
choices for
$a$
and
$b$
, disregarding any additional conditions (we ignore cases with zero probability in which not all of the
$Y_i$
are distinct).

Now, if we require all of the remaining
$n-2$
points to be within the interval
$[a,b]$
, such an event has probability
$(b-a)^{n-2}$
, because for a single observation to be in this interval, it is
$(b-a)/1$
.

So the joint probability density is
$$f_{Y_{(1)}, Y_{(n)}}(a,b) = n(n-1)(b-a)^{n-2}, \quad 0 \le a \le b \le 1, \quad n \in \{2, 3, \ldots \}.$$

Again, this is not a formal proof, just a way to think about how the joint density might be understood.  A more convincing argument may be found in the relevant
Wikipedia article
.

With this joint density, we may compute the distribution of
$R = Y_{(n)} - Y_{(1)}$
.  We have

$$
\Pr[R \le r] = \int_{a=0}^{1-r} \int_{b=a}^{a+r} f_{Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)}}(a,b) \, db \, da + \int_{a=1-r}^1 \int_{b=a}^1 f_{Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)}}(a,b) \, db \, da.$$

Ask yourself why this formula is correct, and perform the computation.  Alternatively--and this is the suggested approach--calculate instead

$$\Pr[R > r] = \int_{a=0}^{1-r} \int_{b=a+r}^1 f_{Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)}}(a,b) \, db \, da.$$

The answer is the book is:

$$ n(n-1)r^{n-2}(1-r) \text{ , } 0 \leq r \leq 1 $$
answer:
First derive the joint distribution of
$(Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)})$
.

Intuitively, see that if
$y>x$
, then for
$Y_{(n)}=y$
and
$Y_{(1)}=x$
to happen, exactly
$n-2$
of the
$n$
independent Unif
$[0,1]$
random variables must lie in the interval
$[x,y]$
. You can select these
$n-2$
variables in
$\binom{n}{n-2}$
ways and one can permute the two variables taking values
$x$
and
$y$
in
$2!$
ways.

Thus
$f_{(Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)})}(x,y)=\binom{n}{n-2}\cdot 2!\cdot (y-x)^{n-2}\cdot \mathbf{1}_{0<x<y<1}=n(n-2)(y-x)^{n-2}\cdot\mathbf{1}_{0<x<y<1}$

Now you can follow Letac Gerard's answer and make the transformation
$U=Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}$
and
$V=Y_{(1)}$
to get that

$$f_{(U,V)}(u,v)=n(n-2)u^{n-2}\quad 0<v<1-u\ , 0<u<1$$

Thus, integrating over
$v$
, one gets
$$f_{Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}}(u)=n(n-2)\cdot u^{n-2}(1-u)\quad ,0<u<1$$

.

Answer:

Note: In the book's notation
$Y_{(1)}$
is the smallest element in the list and
$Y_{(n)}$
is
the largest element in the list.

The following was proved in the text:

$$P( Y_{(1)} \leq y ) = 1 - \left(1 - F(y) \right)^n$$
where

$F(y)$
is the distribution function for
$y$
and
$Y_{(1)}$
is the smallest number
in the list of
$n$
numbers. Now we need to find the distribution function. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= \int_{0}^{y_0} 1 \,\, dy = y \Bigg|_{0}^{y_0} \\
P( Y \leq y_0 ) &= y_0
\end{align*}

Hence our distribution function is:

$$ F(y) = \begin{cases}
0		& \text{ for } y \leq 0 \\
y		& \text{ for } 0 \leq y \leq 1 \\
1		& \text{ for } y \geq 1 \\
\end{cases} $$

Now we have:

\begin{align*}
P( Y_{(1)} \leq y ) = 1 - \left(1 - y \right)^n
\end{align*}

Now we need to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
which we will call
$g_n(y)$
. The general
formula is:

$$ g_n(y) = n \left( F(y) \right) ^{n-1}f(y) $$

Now we use this to find the density function for
$Y_{(n)}$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \left( 1 \right) \\
g_n(y) &= n \left( y \right) ^{n-1} \\
P( Y_{(n)} \leq y ) &= y^ n
\end{align*}

Now, I need a new approach. Let
$h$
be the probability density function for the random
value
$b - a$
. Let
$H$
be the distribution function for
$h$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 - P( b-a >= y_0 ) = \int_{b-a}^1 \,\,\, dy \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &=  y \bigg|_{b-a}^{1} = 1 - (b - a) \\
P( b-a <= y_0 ) &= 1 + a - b \\
\end{align*}

Now, I am thinking that this approach is wrong because
$b - a$
is not a uniformly distributed random variable. What is the right way to do this problem?


The answer is the book is:

$$ n(n-1)r^{n-2}(1-r) \text{ , } 0 \leq r \leq 1 $$

First derive the joint distribution of
$(Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)})$
.

Intuitively, see that if
$y>x$
, then for
$Y_{(n)}=y$
and
$Y_{(1)}=x$
to happen, exactly
$n-2$
of the
$n$
independent Unif
$[0,1]$
random variables must lie in the interval
$[x,y]$
. You can select these
$n-2$
variables in
$\binom{n}{n-2}$
ways and one can permute the two variables taking values
$x$
and
$y$
in
$2!$
ways.

Thus
$f_{(Y_{(1)},Y_{(n)})}(x,y)=\binom{n}{n-2}\cdot 2!\cdot (y-x)^{n-2}\cdot \mathbf{1}_{0<x<y<1}=n(n-2)(y-x)^{n-2}\cdot\mathbf{1}_{0<x<y<1}$

Now you can follow Letac Gerard's answer and make the transformation
$U=Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}$
and
$V=Y_{(1)}$
to get that

$$f_{(U,V)}(u,v)=n(n-2)u^{n-2}\quad 0<v<1-u\ , 0<u<1$$

Thus, integrating over
$v$
, one gets
$$f_{Y_{(n)}-Y_{(1)}}(u)=n(n-2)\cdot u^{n-2}(1-u)\quad ,0<u<1$$

title: For Sakurai and Napolitano $\mathbf{(}1.237\mathbf{)}$, what interpretations of the abstract ket, $|\alpha \rangle$, are allowed?
question:
In Sakurai and Napolitano
$^1$
, physical states are represented by state vectors in a complex vector space. Such state vectors are called ‘kets’, and an arbitrary one is denoted by the symbol
$|\alpha \rangle$
, see Sakurai and Napolitano
$^1$
pg. 11.

In section 1.7.1, pg 47, they write about the ‘Position-Space Wave Function’,

In our formalism the inner product
$\langle x^\prime |\alpha \rangle$

is what is usually referred to as the
wave function

$\Psi_\alpha
(x^\prime)$
for state
$|\alpha \rangle$
:

\begin{equation*} \langle x^\prime |\alpha \rangle = \Psi_\alpha
(x^\prime) \tag{1.237} \end{equation*}

In applying
$\mathbf{(}1.237\mathbf{)}$
should we always be thinking, that the actual concrete Hilbert space the equation can be used for, is some
$L^2(S)$
type vector space, where the set
$S$
, is
$\mathbb{R}$
or some interval chosen from
$\mathbb{R}$
? Should we also insist, that any of the real numbers in any such set,
$S$
, be interpreted as a value of an x-coordinate, rather than a value of momentum or energy …

My question is: For Sakurai and Napolitano
$\mathbf{(}1.237\mathbf{)}$
, what interpretations of the abstract ket,
$|\alpha \rangle$
, are allowed?

Other Information

Other authors might, in place of an equation like
$\mathbf{(}1.237\mathbf{)}$
have something similar to the following

\begin{equation*}
\langle x^\prime |\Psi \rangle = \Psi (x^\prime) \tag{1}
\end{equation*}

where
$\Psi$
is used to label the abstract ket, as well as represent the name of the function that appears on the right hand side.

The essential part of my question, could be rephrased in terms of
$\mathbf{(}1\mathbf{)}$
and
$|\Psi \rangle$
, rather than
$\mathbf{(}1.237\mathbf{)}$
and
$| \alpha \rangle$
.

Reference:

1, J.J.Sakurai and Jim Napolitano, Modern Quantum Mechanics Third Edition, Cambridge University Press & Assessment (2021).
answer:
Typically in quantum mechanics one chooses a separable Hilbert space, that is then isomorphic to
$L^2$
, so yes you should picture that. As for your second question, we don't have to assign a concrete meaning to the variable
$x'$
. You may be aware that we can write the wavefunction in either momentum or physical space, so the source space for your
$L^2$
can be whatever. Note that it is costumary to choose
$L^2(\mathbb R ^3, dx)$
for the Hilbert space in physical space, and that often one finds that the Fourier transform is a unitary map
$$L^2(\mathbb R ^3, dx)\rightarrow L^2(\mathbb R ^3, dp/(2\pi)^3)$$

but of course nobody stops you from starting in momentum space.

Physicists begun thinking of states as labelled by quantum numbers. For instance,
$\alpha$
could be the first principal quantum number
$n$
, labelling the energies of the system. The choice to write
$\psi_\alpha(x)$
, with
$\alpha$
as a subscript, is reminiscent of that viewpoint. This means that what Sakurai is suggesting is you can think of
$|\alpha \rangle$
as an eigenvector for some observable, that is, a self-adjoint operator. But of course it is not limited to that. It can be any element of the Hilbert space. In fact, it can be any element of
the rigged hilbert space
. Two examples that you will almost certainly encounter are
$$\langle y | x \rangle=\delta(x-y)\\
\langle x|p\rangle =e^{ipx}$$

if
$[x,p]=i$
(forgive me the abuse of notation of writing x and p for both the operators, the labels, and the variables, but all of this is standard). Note that none of the wavefunctions in the rhs are actual square-integrable functions on the real line. In fact, the first is not even a function, but of course a
(tempered) distribution
. If you're not aware of what this is, you should either ask a separate question if you're interested or browse the either of the SE for what is at this point a very common topic and is bound to have many and good answers. Just for completeness, allow me to make this a bit concrete without going into many details.

The cases where
$\alpha$
doesn't label some actual element of the Hilbert space occur in quantum mechanics when we interpret it as the variable for some unbounded operator, such as position or momentum. This makes it such that their (would-be) eigenvectors are not vectors in the Hilbert space at all. However, we would still like to interpret them as states in some (often non-physical or ideal) sense (for example,
$| x \rangle$
is often interpreted as a state of definite position), and so we rig the Hilbert space with such additional elements (tempered distributions in the case of
$L^2$
). Then we still use braket notation for those elements, but what we're really doing is extending that notation to include the duality pairing of the distributions (that is,
$\langle \eta | f \rangle:=\eta(f)$
, where
$\eta$
is a distribution and
$f$
is a test function). We can do this because the Hilbert space can be continuously included in the distributions (picture for instance
$L^2$
; any square integrable function
$f$
defines a distribution
$s \mapsto \int fs d\mu$
). Then the canonical pairing is compatible with the Hilbert space's inner product, in the sense that it degenerates to it if the distribution is in the inclusion of the Hilbert space; because of this, we can keep using braket notation for both cases.

In summary,
$| \alpha \rangle$
can be interpreted as any physical or idealized state, that is, any vector in the Hilbert space or element in the rigged Hilbert space.

title: $\gcd$ is (almost) continuous in Furstenberg's topology in one argument (simple proof inside), but is it continuous in two variables simultaneously?
question:
Extend
$\gcd$
to all of
$\Bbb{Z}^2 \to \Bbb{Z}$
in the standard way, or whatever way makes this work out.

Now we know that the topology
$\phi$
on
$\Bbb{Z}$
generated by open balls of the form
$a +b\Bbb{Z}$
for
$a,b \in \Bbb{Z}$
and
$b\neq 0$
, forms the profinite integer topology restricted to
$\Bbb{Z}$
, but here we've called it the Furstenberg topology.

We know that
$(\Bbb{Z}, \phi)$
forms a topological ring and that functions such as
$\lfloor \frac{x- c}{n}\rfloor$
are continuous in
$x$
for fixed
$n, c \in \Bbb{Z}, n \neq 0$
.

So, taking this further, defining
$(x\bmod n) := x - n\lfloor\frac{x}{n}\rfloor$
, we see that for fixed modulus
$n$
, the usual definition of
$x \bmod n$
that takes the standard residue in
$\{0, 1,2,\dots, n-1\}$
is also continuous, since we wrote it as a composition of continuous operations (we're in a topological ring).

Now consider the function
$\Bbb{Z} \to \Bbb{Z}$
given by
$\gcd(p^k,x)$
for fixed prime
$p$
and
$k \in \Bbb{N}$
, and varying
$x\in\Bbb{Z}$
. Now if
$k = 1$
for simplicity, we may write
$\gcd(p, x)$
as:

$$
\gcd(p, x) = (1 - (x\bmod p)^{p-1})p + (x \bmod p)^{p-1} \in \{1, p\}
$$


since
$\gcd(p, x)$
is either going to be
$1$
or
$p$
, and we used Fermat's little theorem trick (FLT) in order to induce the 0-1 switch behavior in the formula.  Since
$p$
is fixed throughout, we get that even the powers are to a fixed number
$p-1$
so the whole thing is a continuous function, being a composition of continuous maps.


Now, I don't know how yet to extend this formulaic method to handle
$k \geq 2$
, so I have have two questions, one basic and one advanced.




What is the FLT-based formula for
$k \geq 2$
that proves continuity of
$\gcd(p^k, x)$
for fixed
$x$
?




Is
$\gcd$
actually continuous from the product topology on
$\Bbb{Z}^2 \to \Bbb{Z}$
induced by the Furstenberg topology?  I.e. is it continuous as a binary function?






If we can answer the first question in the affirmative, then using
$\gcd(y,x)\gcd(z,x) = \gcd(yz, x)$
for all
$z$
coprime with
$y$
, we would immediately get that
$\gcd(y,x)$
is continous in
$x$
for any fixed
$y \in \Bbb{Z}$
, by first forming
$y = \pm \prod_{i} p_i^{k_i}$
using the unique prime factor decomposition of an integer.

I have a doubt that the answer to the second question is yes, but maybe you could prove my intuition wrong!
answer:
I think it is easier to just work with the definition of the open sets in this topology.

The
$\gcd$
function is continuous at
$(0,0)$
: Consider any open neighborhood of
$\gcd(0,0)=0$
, w.l.o.g.
$k \mathbb{Z}$
(with
$k > 0$
). Then
$\gcd$
maps the open neighborhood
$k \mathbb{Z}  \times k \mathbb{Z}$
of
$(0,0)$
to
$k \mathbb{Z}$
.

However, the
$\gcd$
function is
not
continuous at
$(1,0)$
. Continuity would mean that for any open neighborhood of
$\gcd(1,0)=1$
, say
$1 + k \mathbb{Z}$
(with
$k > 0$
), there are neighborhoods
$1 + u \mathbb{Z}$
of
$1$

and
$v \mathbb{Z}$
of
$0$
(where
$u,v >  0$
) such that
$\gcd$
maps
$( 1+ u \mathbb{Z}) \times v \mathbb{Z}$
to
$1 + k \mathbb{Z}$
. In other words:

$$x \equiv 1 \bmod u, ~~ y \equiv 0 \bmod v \implies \gcd(x,y) \equiv 1 \bmod k \tag{1}$$

This works fine for
$k=2$
(take
$u=2$
,
$v=1$
), but it fails
$^1$
for
$k=3$
:

Choose a prime
$q$
with
$q \equiv 2 \bmod 3$
and
$q \not\mid u$
. There is some
$a \in \mathbb{Z}$
with
$aq \equiv 1 \bmod u$
. In particular,
$a,u$
are coprime. By Dirichlet's Theorem on arithmetic progressions, there is a prime
$p$
with
$p > v$
and
$p \equiv a \bmod u$
. Now define

$$x := pq, \quad y := vq$$

Modulo
$u$
, we have
$x \equiv aq \equiv 1$
. Modulo
$v$
, we have
$y \equiv 0$
. Hence,
$(1)$
would give us
$\gcd(x,y) \equiv 1 \bmod 3$
. However,

$$\gcd(x,y) = \gcd(p,v) \cdot q = q \equiv 2 \bmod 3.$$

This proof shows a slightly stronger statement, namely that
$\gcd : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$
is not continuous at
$(0,1)$
(where
$\mathbb{N}$
carries the subspace topology).

Added.
I am not sure at the moment how to canonically extend
$\gcd$
to negative integers, but regarding
$\gcd : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$
, we can safely say that it is continuous in each argument separately, i.e. that for every
$a \geq 0$
the map
$\gcd(a,-) : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$
is continuous.

Proof.
For
$a = 0$
it is the identity map, hence continuous. For
$a > 0$
the map is even locally constant because for every
$b \geq 0$
we have

$$\gcd(a, (b + a \mathbb{Z}) \cap \mathbb{N}) = \{\gcd(a,b)\}.$$

$^1$
Disclaimer.
The proof for
$k=3$
has been found by Google Gemini Pro. I have checked every detail thoroughly and formulated the proof with my own words.

For
$a = 0$
it is the identity map, hence continuous. For
$a > 0$
the map is even locally constant because for every
$b \geq 0$
we have

$$\gcd(a, (b + a \mathbb{Z}) \cap \mathbb{N}) = \{\gcd(a,b)\}.$$

$^1$
Disclaimer.
The proof for
$k=3$
has been found by Google Gemini Pro. I have checked every detail thoroughly and formulated the proof with my own words.

title: Pathological problem for OLS overfitting?
question:
I'm trying to understand when and why overfitting occurs in OLS (Ordinary Least Squares), and when exactly ridge regression, which adds a regularization term to the loss function, offers the most benefit. The most popular answer to this question online is that this occurs when there are a large number of features and/or the features are highly correlated with each other. However, this is not specific enough for me. I will ask my more specific question in a moment, but first some technical setup.

Suppose
$\mathbf{w}\sim\mathcal W$
is a random vector of weights
$\in\mathbb R^n$
distributed according to some distribution
$\mathcal W$
, that
$X$
is an
$N\times n$
matrix whose rows are IID row vectors (feature vectors) distributed according to some other distribution
$\mathcal F$
, and that
$\mathbf{y} = X\mathbf{w}+\epsilon$
where
$\epsilon\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)_{N\times 1}$
is the noise. The vector
$\mathbf{w}$
represents the hidden parameter in this parameter estimation problem, the matrix
$X$
together with
$\mathbf{y}$
represents the training data and the vector
$\epsilon$
represents the noise. If some learning method is used to compute an estimator
$\hat{\mathbf{w}} = f(X,\mathbf{y})$
for the weights, then the least-squares risk is defined as
$R(f) = \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{x}\cdot \hat{\mathbf{w}} - \mathbf{x}\cdot \mathbf{w}-\epsilon)^2]_{\mathbf{x},X,\epsilon}$
where
$\mathbf{x}\sim \mathcal F$
and
$\epsilon\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$
again.

As far as I understand, this is how we formulate a general linear parameter estimation problem (with some lack of generality due to the error being assumed normal, which I'm fine with). If
$f(X,\mathbf{y}) = (X^\ast X)^{-1}X^\ast \mathbf{y}$
, then this is OLS (Ordinary Least Squares), whereas if
$f(X,\mathbf{y}) = (X^\ast X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^\ast \mathbf{y}$
we are using Ridge Regression with parameter
$\lambda$
. It is known that as
$\lambda \to 0^+$
, the Ridge Regression solution approaches OLS, while as
$\lambda\to\infty$
, I believe it approaches the constant estimator with
$\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0}$
, which would yield an error of
$\mathbb E[y^2]$
.

Question:
can anyone suggest (possibly pathological) distributions
$\mathcal W$
and
$\mathcal F$
that make OLS very likely to overfit, so that
$R_{\text{OLS}}(f)$
is very high, while ridge regression for a suitable choice of the parameter
$\lambda$
performs much better? Note that it is easy to come up with trivial examples, for instance
$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$
, for which a large
$\lambda$
improves the model simply because it decreases the learned weights. I am not interested in these examples, so to make my question more precise, I am looking for distributions which make the following ratio as small as possible:
$$\frac{R_{\lambda=\lambda\text{opt}}}{\min(R_{\lambda=0}, R_{\lambda\to\infty})}$$
That is, the optimal
$\lambda$
value in Ridge Regression should offer the best possible improvement over
both
the OLS (
$\lambda=0$
) training method and the constant-estimate method. Graphically, if the Ridge risk were plotted as a function of
$\lambda$
, we should see as deep of gulf between the minimum risk and both the y-intercept on the left and the asymptote on the right.


I realize that it's probably infeasible to calculate optimal
$\lambda$
values analytically / in closed form in most cases, so I would also welcome examples that are supported by
numerical evidence
rather than a proof. :-) Really I'm just trying to get an intuition for "what shapes of distributions" that are most susceptible to overfitting by OLS. I am especially interested in examples that achieve this even without a huge number of features, particularly
$n < N$
.
answer:
Consider the following simple model:

$z$
,
$u$
and
$v$
are independent standard normal variates

The dependent variable
$y$
is given by
$y=z + u$

The two independent variables observed by the researcher are
$z$
and
$x=z+0.1*v$

Intuitively,
$x$
is just a noisy version of
$z$
, and should be ignored and our estimate should just be
$\hat y \approx z$
. If we estimate the OLS regression
$y = a + bx + cz$
using a large enough sample (say
$N=1000$
), we will get
$a \approx b \approx 0$
and
$c \approx 1$
in accordance with this intuition.

The situation is very different in small samples (say
$N=50$
). By pure chance, we could get a sample correlation (
$r$
) of
$\pm 0.2$
between
$u$
and
$v$
(this can be seen from a simple calculation using Fisher's normal approximation to the
$atanh$
transformation of the correlation coefficient). What would OLS do now? OLS can improve on the theoretical estimate
$\hat y \approx z$
by adding an estimate
$\hat u$
obtained from the correlated variable
$v$
. The sample correlation
$r$
implies that
$\hat u\approx r v$
. Of course, OLS does not have
$v$
directly available to it, but since
$x=z+0.1v$
, it can compute
$v=10(x-z)$
. So it can form
$\hat u \approx 10 r (x-z)$
. Suppose
$10r \approx 1.5$
, OLS would estimate
$y = a + bx + cz$
by solving the two equations
$b-c \approx 1.5$
and
$b+c \approx 1$
in order to achieve
$\hat y = z + \hat u$
. In other words, it would output
$\hat b \approx 1.25, \hat c \approx -0.25$
. With a negative sample correlation
$r\approx -0.2$
, it could output
$\hat b \approx -2, \hat c \approx 3$
.

How does ridge regression solve this problem? The over fitting achieved by large
$b$
and
$c$
offsetting each other is inhibited by the penalty on
$b^2+c^2$
. When we use ridge regression, we still have
$b+c \approx 1$
but both
$b$
and
$c$
are positive.

I suggest some simulations using this simple model to understand what is going on.

title: Relationship between characteristic functions and measures
question:
I am reading Axler's
Measure, Integration, & Real Analysis
, and, as usual, it is awesome. However, I can't seem to follow the intuition on a proof relating characteristic functions and measures.

In 3.13 (integral-type sums for simple functions), he states:

Suppose
$(X, \mathcal{S}, \mu)$
is a measure space. Suppose
$a_{1}, \ldots, a_{m}, b_{1}, \ldots, b_{n} \in [0, \infty]$
and
$A_{1}, \ldots, A_{m}, B_{1}, \ldots, B_{n} \in \mathcal{S}$
are such that
$\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\mathcal{\chi}_{A_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}b_{k}\mathcal{\chi}_{B_k}$
. Then

$$\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\mu(A_{j}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n}b_{k}\mu(B_{k})$$


I think what's tripping me up is the characteristic functions.  It's unclear to me how to interpret the
$\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\mathcal{\chi}_{A_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}b_{k}\mathcal{\chi}_{B_k}$
part of the statement.  Aren't these sums dependent on which
$A_{j}$
and
$B_{k}$
we choose?


Thanks


Edit 1:  I think I can state my confusion.  The issue is that summing indicator functions requires a choice of
$x$
, whereas summing measures does not.  For example, suppose
$f(x) \equiv 2\mathcal{\chi}_{[0, 1]}$
+
$5\mathcal{\chi}_{[2, 4]}$
.  Then
$f(0.5) = 2$
but
$f(2.5) = 5$
.  Thus, how can I make sense of the term
$\sum_{j=1}^{m}c_{j}\mathcal{\chi}_{A_{j}}$
when there is no
$x$
explicitly defined to resolve the value of
$\mathcal{\chi}_{A_{j}}$
?


Edit 2:  Thank you all for the input. There is another lemma/theorem just before this that helped me (3.7):


Suppose
$(X, \mathcal{S}, \mu)$
is a measure space,
$E_{1}, \ldots, E_{n}$
are disjoin sets in
$\mathcal{S}$
, and
$c_{1}, \ldots, c_{n} \in [0, \infty]$
.  Then


$$\int \bigg(\sum_{k=1}^{n}c_{k}\chi_{E_{k}}\bigg) \;d\mu = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{k}\mu(E_{k})$$

This result is in fact used in the next lemma (3.15) to show that the representation does not matter.
answer:
A natural way to define Lebesgue integral of an indicator function of a set
$A$
is to give the value
$\mu(A)$
. Intuitively, and since we would like the integral to be linear, we want to define the integral of
$\sum_{j=1}^n a_j\mathbf{1}_{A_j}$
as
$\sum_{j=1}^n a_j\mu(A_j)$
. However, it is possible to represent such a linear combination of indicator function in several way. For example,

$$\mathbf{1}_{[0,2]}+2\mathbf{1}_{[1,3]}=\mathbf{1}_{[0,1)}+3\mathbf{1}_{[1,2]}+2\mathbf{1}_{(2,3]}$$

and there are infinitely many expressions. We would like the integral to be independent of the representation and this is exactly the purpose of the statement 3.13.

The equality
$\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\mathbf{1}_{A_j}=\sum_{k=1}^nb_k\mathbf{1}_ {B_k}$
is understood in the following way: for each
$x\in X$
,
$\sum_{j=1}^m a_j\mathbf{1}_{A_j}(x)=\sum_{k=1}^nb_k\mathbf{1}_ {B_k}(x)$
.

I think what's tripping me up is the characteristic functions.  It's unclear to me how to interpret the
$\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\mathcal{\chi}_{A_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}b_{k}\mathcal{\chi}_{B_k}$
part of the statement.  Aren't these sums dependent on which
$A_{j}$

we choose?

?


Edit 2:  Thank you all for the input. There is another lemma/theorem just before this that helped me (3.7):


Suppose

answer:
The author writes
$\sum_{j=1}^{m}a_{j}\chi_{A_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}b_{k}\chi_{B_k}$
, where
$\chi_A$
denotes the characteristic function of
$A$
. The "abbreviation" in your post is confusing and unnecessary, and apparently it is your own creation. As mentioned in the other answer, the purpose of the statement is to prove that integration for simple functions is independent from the chosen decomposition.

.  Then
$f(0.5) = 2$

$\sum_{j=1}^{m}c_{j}\mathcal{\chi}_{A_{j}}$
when there is no
$x$
explicitly defined to resolve the value of
$\mathcal{\chi}_{A_{j}}$

Suppose
$(X, \mathcal{S}, \mu)$
is a measure space,
$E_{1}, \ldots, E_{n}$
are disjoin sets in
$\mathcal{S}$
, and
$c_{1}, \ldots, c_{n} \in [0, \infty]$
.  Then


$$\int \bigg(\sum_{k=1}^{n}c_{k}\chi_{E_{k}}\bigg) \;d\mu = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{k}\mu(E_{k})$$

This result is in fact used in the next lemma (3.15) to show that the representation does not matter.
answer:
Equality of functions doesn't depend on a choice of point, it means that both sides of the equation have equal values when evaluating at
all
points
$x$

for example,

$$\chi_{[0,3]}+\chi_{(1,2)}
=
\chi_{[0,1]}+2\chi_{(1,2)}+\chi_{[2,3]}.
$$

title: How can we find rational (or integer) parametrization using only "pen and paper"-based calculation for an elliptic curve $Y^2=X^3-3c^4X-(c^{10}+c^2)$?
question:
This type of elliptic curve arises from Euler's
4th power taxicab number
solution. I will suggest an attempt at a solution
$$P^4+Q^4=R^4+S^4$$

$$\begin{cases} P=(a+b) \\ Q=(c-d) \\ R=(a-b) \\ S=(c+d) \end{cases}$$


$$ab(a^2+b^2)=cd(c^2+d^2)$$


$$\frac{a}{c}=\frac{dc^2+d^3}{ba^2+b^3}=m$$




We'll make a replacement,
$a=cm$
we will get

$$dc^2+d^3 =bm^3c^2+mb^3$$


$$c^2(bm^3-d) =(d^3-mb^3)$$

if
$d=bm^3-1$

then

$$c^2 = (m^9-m)b^3-3m^6b^2+3m^3b-1$$


$$\begin{cases} c= Y/(m^9-m)\\ b= X/(m^9-m) \end{cases}$$


Now we will get this elliptic curve Weierstrass form

$$\boxed{Y^2=X^3-3c^4X-(c^{10}+c^2)}$$


I have searched in
Google
for finding integer solution in terms of
$X(c), Y(c)$
. I have seen there exists a parametrization for this elliptic curve

$$\begin{cases} X(c) =c^4+c^2+1 \\ Y(c)=c^6+c^4+c^2+1 \end{cases}$$

My question
is whether there is a "pen and paper"-based calculation method of finding this integer point parametrization. If it exists, how can we find them with a step by step derivation?
answer:
The curve comes from the transformation

$$C^2=(t^9-t)b^3-3t^6b^2+3t^3b-1.$$

Put

$$A=t^9-t,\qquad U=Ab,\qquad Y=AC.$$

Then the equation becomes

$$Y^2=U^3-3t^6U^2+3t^3AU-A^2.$$

Now shift the variable by

$$X=U-t^6.$$

After simplification, this gives

$$Y^2=X^3-3t^4X-(t^{10}+t^2).$$

The point is found by looking at the simplest branch of the original fourth-power identity. Take the symmetric choice

$$C=b,\qquad d=a.$$

Since

$$a=tC,\qquad d=t^3b-1,$$

this gives

$$tC=t^3C-1.$$

With
$C=b$
, we obtain

$$b=\frac{1}{t^3-t}.$$

Therefore

$$U=Ab=(t^9-t)\frac{1}{t^3-t}=t^6+t^4+t^2+1.$$

Since
$X=U-t^6$
, we get

$$X=t^4+t^2+1.$$

Also,

$$Y=AC=(t^9-t)\frac{1}{t^3-t}=t^6+t^4+t^2+1.$$

Hence

$$\boxed{(X,Y)=\bigl(t^4+t^2+1,\;t^6+t^4+t^2+1\bigr)}$$

is a rational point on the elliptic curve.

The reason this works is simple: the point is not guessed from nowhere. It is the image of the most symmetric, almost trivial branch of the original fourth-power equation under the change of variables that converts the problem into Weierstrass form. Thus the closed-form point is a natural section of the elliptic family, not a full parametrization of all its rational points.

Hope this is helpful!

title: Matrix equation - similar transformations leads to different results
question:
Let's start with general pinhole camera mapping using homogeneous coordinates

$$ \pmb{x} = K R[I|C] \pmb{X} \tag{1} $$


where




$\pmb{x} = \begin{bmatrix} fx & fy & f \end{bmatrix}^\top$
are coordinates in the image plane,




$\pmb{X} = \begin{bmatrix} X & Y & Z & 1 \end{bmatrix}^\top$
are the coordinates in the world frame,




$K$
is the camera calibration matrix of form
$
\begin{bmatrix}
f & 0 & p_x \\
0 & f & p_y \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
\end{bmatrix}
$
, it is invertible,
$K^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}
1/f & 0 & -p_x/f \\
0 & 1/f & -p_y/f \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
\end{bmatrix}$




$R$
is a rotation matrix,




$C=(C_x,C_y,C_z)^T$
,
$-C$
is the location of camera projection center in the world frame,
$(p_x,p_y)$
is the projection of this center on the image plane,




$I=I_3$
- identity matrix




$[I|C]$
is matrix of size (3,4).






The goal is to find a rotation
$R$
that compensates for the shift in the camera projection center for the same image. It should be valid for any
$\pmb{x},\pmb{X}$
pair, so
$$K_0R_0[I|C_0]=K_1R_1[I|C_1]$$

Let camera 0 be in world center (
$C_0=(0,0,0)$
) and
$R_0=I$
, so


$$K_0[I|0]=K_1R[I|C_1] \tag{2}$$


$[I|C]$
has rank 3 because of
$I$
so
$[I|C][I|C]^+=I$
(here
$[.]^+$
stands for pseudoinverse) and we can write
$$R=K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0][I|C_1]^+ \tag{3}$$


In the same manner we can find
$R^{-1}$

$$R^{-1}=[I|C_1][I|0]^+K_0^{-1}K_1$$

but now we can notice that
$[I|0]^+=[\frac{I}{0}]$
and
$[I|C_1][\frac{I}{0}]=I$
so
$R^{-1}=K_0^{-1}K_1$
and finally
$$R=K_1^{-1}K_0 \tag{4}$$
which is different from (3).

Numerical experiment says that (3) is right and (4) is wrong.

The question is where is the fault in the calculations leading to (4) ?

UPD:

I wrote a simple example to illustrate my question. The idea is to map points from the world frame to the image plane using different mappings and compare the coordinates on the image plane, as the goal could be interpreted as obtaining the same or similar image with different mappings.

import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

def mxK(f,px,py):
return np.array([
[f, 0, px],
[0, f, py],
[0, 0, 1]])

def mxIC(c):
c = np.array(c)
c.shape = (3,1)
return np.hstack((np.eye(3),c))

def mxorthosvd(m):
U, S, Vh = np.linalg.svd(m, full_matrices=True)
return U @ Vh

def plotdiff2d(xx1,xx2,title):
xy1 = xx1[:2,:]/xx1[2,:]
xy2 = xx2[:2,:]/xx2[2,:]
dxy = xy2 - xy1
d = np.sum(dxy**2, 0)**.5 / f
md = np.mean(d)
sout = title + f': M[d]/f = {md:.3f}'
ax = plt.figure().gca()
ax.plot(d)
ax.grid(True)
ax.set_title(sout)
print(sout)

rng = np.random.default_rng()

d = np.tan(1/180*np.pi) # 1 degree field of view

f = 1e5
px0 = 0
py0 = 0
px1 = -d/2*f
py1 = -d/2*f

nx = 200
XX = np.vstack((rng.uniform(-d,d,(2,nx)), np.ones((2,nx))))

K0 = mxK(f,px0,py0)
K1 = mxK(f,px1,py1)

IC0 = mxIC([0,0,0])
IC1 = mxIC([px1-px0,py1-py0,0])

R30 = np.linalg.inv(K1) @ K0 @ IC0 @ np.linalg.pinv(IC1)
R3 = mxorthosvd(R30)
R40 = np.linalg.inv(K1) @ K0
R4 = mxorthosvd(R40)

xx0 = K0 @ IC0 @ XX
xx10 = K1 @ IC1 @ XX
xx13 = K1 @ R3 @ IC1 @ XX
xx14 = K1 @ R4 @ IC1 @ XX

plotdiff2d(xx0, xx10, 'xx10')
plotdiff2d(xx0, xx13, 'xx13')
plotdiff2d(xx0, xx14, 'xx14')

The result, aside from graphs, says:

Shift without compensating rotation:

xx10: M[d]/f = 1234.272

Shift with R (3):

xx13: M[d]/f = 76.043

Shift with R (4):

xx14: M[d]/f = 143.249

Now it is obvious that using of right/wrong categories was an exaggeration, but still (3) is better than (4). So question 'Why?' remains.
answer:
Edit: Sorry for my misunderstanding, referencing my scratch work rather than your post directly. Let's try again:

The equation
$K_0[I\mid 0]=K_1R[I\mid C_1]$
has no exact solution for
$R$

when
$C_1\ne 0$
. Indeed,

$$
K_0[I\mid 0]=[\,K_0\mid 0\,],
\qquad
K_1R[I\mid C_1]=[\,K_1R\mid K_1RC_1\,],
$$

so equality would force

$$
K_1R=K_0 \qquad\text{and}\qquad K_1RC_1=0.
$$

Since
$K_1R=K_0$
is invertible, the second condition forces
$C_1=0$
. Thus a
translated camera center cannot be compensated by a single rotation for all points.

The pseudoinverse computation in (3) yields the least-squares solution,
among all
$3\times 3$
matrices
$R$
, of

$$
R[I\mid C_1]=K_1^{-1}K_0[I\mid 0].
$$

Writing
$M=K_1^{-1}K_0$
gives

$$
R_{(3)}=M[I\mid 0][I\mid C_1]^+.
$$

Since

$$
[I\mid C_1]^+=\begin{bmatrix}I\\C_1^T\end{bmatrix}(I+C_1C_1^T)^{-1},
$$

we obtain

$$
R_{(3)}=M(I+C_1C_1^T)^{-1}.
$$

On the other hand, (4) is obtained by right-multiplying instead by

$[I\mid 0]^+$
. This is a different manipulation of the same inconsistent
equation, and it yields

$$
R_{(4)}=M=K_1^{-1}K_0.
$$

Hence (3) and (4) come from applying pseudoinverses to different

$3\times 4$
matrices in an equation that has no exact solution. They
therefore need not agree, and neither solves the original camera equation
unless
$C_1=0$
. Note also that neither
$R_{(3)}$
nor
$R_{(4)}$
is orthogonal
in general, so neither is actually a rotation in general.

The goal is to find a rotation
$R$
that compensates for the shift in the camera projection center for the same image. It should be valid for any
$\pmb{x},\pmb{X}$
pair, so
$$K_0R_0[I|C_0]=K_1R_1[I|C_1]$$

Let camera 0 be in world center (
$C_0=(0,0,0)$
) and
$R_0=I$
, so


$$K_0[I|0]=K_1R[I|C_1] \tag{2}$$


$[I|C]$
has rank 3 because of
$I$
so
$[I|C][I|C]^+=I$
(here
$[.]^+$
stands for pseudoinverse) and we can write
$$R=K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0][I|C_1]^+ \tag{3}$$


In the same manner we can find
$R^{-1}$

$$R^{-1}=[I|C_1][I|0]^+K_0^{-1}K_1$$

but now we can notice that
$[I|0]^+=[\frac{I}{0}]$
and
$[I|C_1][\frac{I}{0}]=I$
so
$R^{-1}=K_0^{-1}K_1$
and finally
$$R=K_1^{-1}K_0 \tag{4}$$
which is different from (3).

Numerical experiment says that (3) is right and (4) is wrong.

The question is where is the fault in the calculations leading to (4) ?

UPD:

I wrote a simple example to illustrate my question. The idea is to map points from the world frame to the image plane using different mappings and compare the coordinates on the image plane, as the goal could be interpreted as obtaining the same or similar image with different mappings.

import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

def mxK(f,px,py):
return np.array([
[f, 0, px],
[0, f, py],
[0, 0, 1]])

def mxIC(c):
c = np.array(c)
c.shape = (3,1)
return np.hstack((np.eye(3),c))

def mxorthosvd(m):
U, S, Vh = np.linalg.svd(m, full_matrices=True)
return U @ Vh

def plotdiff2d(xx1,xx2,title):
xy1 = xx1[:2,:]/xx1[2,:]
xy2 = xx2[:2,:]/xx2[2,:]
dxy = xy2 - xy1
d = np.sum(dxy**2, 0)**.5 / f
md = np.mean(d)
sout = title + f': M[d]/f = {md:.3f}'
ax = plt.figure().gca()
ax.plot(d)
ax.grid(True)
ax.set_title(sout)
print(sout)

rng = np.random.default_rng()

d = np.tan(1/180*np.pi) # 1 degree field of view

f = 1e5
px0 = 0
py0 = 0
px1 = -d/2*f
py1 = -d/2*f

nx = 200
XX = np.vstack((rng.uniform(-d,d,(2,nx)), np.ones((2,nx))))

K0 = mxK(f,px0,py0)
K1 = mxK(f,px1,py1)

IC0 = mxIC([0,0,0])
IC1 = mxIC([px1-px0,py1-py0,0])

R30 = np.linalg.inv(K1) @ K0 @ IC0 @ np.linalg.pinv(IC1)
R3 = mxorthosvd(R30)
R40 = np.linalg.inv(K1) @ K0
R4 = mxorthosvd(R40)

xx0 = K0 @ IC0 @ XX
xx10 = K1 @ IC1 @ XX
xx13 = K1 @ R3 @ IC1 @ XX
xx14 = K1 @ R4 @ IC1 @ XX

plotdiff2d(xx0, xx10, 'xx10')
plotdiff2d(xx0, xx13, 'xx13')
plotdiff2d(xx0, xx14, 'xx14')

The result, aside from graphs, says:

Shift without compensating rotation:

xx10: M[d]/f = 1234.272

Shift with R (3):

xx13: M[d]/f = 76.043

Shift with R (4):

xx14: M[d]/f = 143.249

Now it is obvious that using of right/wrong categories was an exaggeration, but still (3) is better than (4). So question 'Why?' remains.
answer:
(3) and (4) are consistent. In fact, if (2) is solvable, we must have

$$
[K_0|0]=K_0[I|0]=K_1R[I|C_1]=[K_1R|K_1RC_1].
$$

Therefore
$K_0=K_1R$
and
$K_1RC_1=0$
. In turn,
$R=K_1^{-1}K_0$
and
$C_1=0$
. Hence (3) boils down to (4).

If
$C_1\ne0$
, equation (2) is not solvable. In this case, one can only look for an
$R$
that makes
$K_1R[I|C_1]$
somewhat close to
$K_0[I|0]$
. The resultant
$R$
is not a solution to
$(2)$
(and it cannot be, as the equation is insolvable), but a solution to a minimisation problem.

In particular, your
$R$
in
$(3)$
is a solution that minimises
$\big\|K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0]-R[I|C_1]\big\|_F$
over all
$3\times3$
matrices, but it is not a rotation matrix in general.

To minimise
$\big\|K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0]-R[I|C_1]\big\|_F$
over all rotation matrices
$R$
, the problem becomes an
orthogonal Procrustes problem
and its solution is well-known. First, let
$M=K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0][I|C_1]^T=K_1^{-1}K_0$
). Then compute a singular value decomposition
$M=U\Sigma V^T$
and take
$R=U\operatorname{diag}\left(1,1,\det(UV^T)\right)V^T$
.

There is a catch, though. Note that the value of
$M$
in the above does not depend on
$C_1$
, because

$\big\|K_1^{-1}K_0[I|0]-R[I|C_1]\big\|_F^2=-2\operatorname{tr}\left(R^TK_1^{-1}K_0\right)+\text{constant}$
. So, if one uses the orthogonal Procrustes problem as a surrogate to
$(2)$
, the solution is tractable but one loses information about
$C_1$
and the solution is always the same regardless of
$C_1$
.

One may also opt to minimise
$\big\|K_0[I|0]-K_1R[I|C_1]\big\|_F$
over all rotation matrices
$R$
. In this case, to my knowledge, there is no explicit solution for
$R$
and one can only solve the problem by numerical methods.

title: What is the best notation for subsequences?
question:
EDIT (after the closure):
While I agree the question looks opinion-based, it actually asks for a reasonable solution which could be used as a convention - and in fact the answers confirm this aspect. For this reason, I think the question should be reopened as still relevant for this site.

By the way, I also changed the brackets from
$\{\}$
to
$()$
- they are in fact more suitable for sequences as vectors with countably many entries.

I have just had a discussion with some of my friends about the best (perhaps the correct) way to denote subsequences. The summary is the following:

Given a sequence
$x:\mathbb N \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
, what is more appropriate between
$(x_{n_k}) _{k \in \mathbb N}$
and
$(x_{k_n})_{n \in \mathbb N}$
to denote a subsequence?

At first glance it may look like a pure change of letters, but actually the meaning of these two notations is more subtle. The issue becomes clearer introducing two copies of
$\mathbb N$
. Let me denote them
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

The approach I prefer -
$(x_{n_k})$
- and why:

Let
$x:\mathbb N_1 \to \mathbb R$
be such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
and
$\nu:\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb N_1$
be strictly increasing and such that
$k \mapsto \nu_k$
. I call
$x \circ \nu :\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_{\nu_k}$
a subsequence.

The main advantage of this approach is that here you always deal with
$k$
. For example, you would say that
$(x_{2k})$
and
$(x_{2k+1})$
are subsequences of
$(x_k)$
, and I think this is very easy to read. Furthermore, the second notation seems ambiguous to me in the sense that
$k$
should be the index moving through
$\mathbb N_1$
, so it might be read as
$(x_{1_n},x_{2_n},\dots)$
, which makes no sense. Of course the meaning here is that you let
$n$
vary across
$\mathbb N_2$
and find indices
$\{k_1 < k_2 < \cdots\} \subset \mathbb N_1$
, but still, I don't find this approach very natural.

This approach is followed in the Italian book "Analisi 1" by Giuseppe De Marco, which is widely considered one of the best books for Italian students attending a first course in Real Analysis.

The approach one of my friends prefer -
$(x_{k_n})$
- and why:

Needless to say
He prefers the second notation. Here are his arguments:

He prefers to use an index for each copy of
$\mathbb N$
. An alleged flaw in my notation is that going from
$(x_k)$
to
$(x_{n_k})$
means that I am using
$k$
both for
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

More generally, if you have a function
$x : A \to B, a \mapsto x(a)$
, you may want to consider a restriction to a subset of
$A$
via a composition with a map
$a: I \to A, t \mapsto a(t)$
. Now you get
$x \circ a : I \to B, t \to x(a(t))$
. Replace
$A=\mathbb N_1, B = \mathbb R, I = \mathbb N_2, k=a$
to get the second notation (the one I prefer does not work because
$k$
and
$n$
are switched).

Many authors, such as Rudin, use this notation instead.

While I agree that his notation is more general, I feel much more comfortable with mine and I really find the other one difficult to read, at least for sequences.

From the examples I reported above, it looks that both these approaches are accepted.
If you had to choose one to make a convention, what would you pick, and why?
I would appreciate other arguments supporting one or the other point of view, which would encourage others to adopt the suggested convention.
answer:
If the original sequence is indexed by
$k$
, I would prefer the subsequence be indexed by
$k_n$
rather than
$n_k$
. That way, the
$x$
's themselves stay indexed by
$k$
, except now you have picked out a particular subset of all
$k$
's to use.

But ultimately, it is a preference and while you might possibly argue that they carry subtly different connotations, purely mathematically there is no difference between them.

$n$
to denote values in
$\mathbb N_2$
, functions of the form
$\mathbb N_2 \mapsto \mathbb N_1$
can be denoted
$n \mapsto k(n)$
although the notational standard is usually
$n \mapsto k_n$
(although when needed to avoid double sub/superscripts I try to use
$k(n)$
). This is quite like ordinary Cartesian coordinate notation for functions, where one uses the "independent variable"
$x$
for values on the horizontal axis and the "dependent variable"
$y$
for values on the vertical axis, allowing functions to be written in the form
$x \mapsto y(x)$
.

One thing that is going on here is that a sequence is a function, and a subsequence is composition of two functions, and the
$x_k$
and
$x_{k_n}$
notation enforces that. When composing functions of real variables, sometimes we write
$y=f(x)$
and
$z=g(y)$
and then
$z=g(f(x))$
; but then, if we wish to avoid introducing the separate function symbols
$f$
and
$g$
, we will write
$y=y(x)$
and
$z=z(y)$
and then
$z=z(y(x))$
. The natural way to extend these functional notation concepts to sequences, in a way that avoids separate function symbols, would be to write
$k = k(n)$
and
$x=x(k)$
and then
$x=x(k(n))$
. Of course we almost never use "parenthesis" notation for those functions that we call sequences, and so this becomes instead
$k=k_n$
and
$x=x_k$
and then
$x=x_{k_n}$
.

conversation.content:
EDIT (after the closure):
While I agree the question looks opinion-based, it actually asks for a reasonable solution which could be used as a convention - and in fact the answers confirm this aspect. For this reason, I think the question should be reopened as still relevant for this site.

By the way, I also changed the brackets from
$\{\}$
to
$()$
- they are in fact more suitable for sequences as vectors with countably many entries.

I have just had a discussion with some of my friends about the best (perhaps the correct) way to denote subsequences. The summary is the following:

Given a sequence
$x:\mathbb N \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
, what is more appropriate between
$(x_{n_k}) _{k \in \mathbb N}$
and
$(x_{k_n})_{n \in \mathbb N}$
to denote a subsequence?

At first glance it may look like a pure change of letters, but actually the meaning of these two notations is more subtle. The issue becomes clearer introducing two copies of
$\mathbb N$
. Let me denote them
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

The approach I prefer -
$(x_{n_k})$
- and why:

Let
$x:\mathbb N_1 \to \mathbb R$
be such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
and
$\nu:\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb N_1$
be strictly increasing and such that
$k \mapsto \nu_k$
. I call
$x \circ \nu :\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_{\nu_k}$
a subsequence.

The main advantage of this approach is that here you always deal with
$k$
. For example, you would say that
$(x_{2k})$
and
$(x_{2k+1})$
are subsequences of
$(x_k)$
, and I think this is very easy to read. Furthermore, the second notation seems ambiguous to me in the sense that
$k$
should be the index moving through
$\mathbb N_1$
, so it might be read as
$(x_{1_n},x_{2_n},\dots)$
, which makes no sense. Of course the meaning here is that you let
$n$
vary across
$\mathbb N_2$
and find indices
$\{k_1 < k_2 < \cdots\} \subset \mathbb N_1$
, but still, I don't find this approach very natural.

This approach is followed in the Italian book "Analisi 1" by Giuseppe De Marco, which is widely considered one of the best books for Italian students attending a first course in Real Analysis.

The approach one of my friends prefer -
$(x_{k_n})$
- and why:

Needless to say
He prefers the second notation. Here are his arguments:

He prefers to use an index for each copy of
$\mathbb N$
. An alleged flaw in my notation is that going from
$(x_k)$
to
$(x_{n_k})$
means that I am using
$k$
both for
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

More generally, if you have a function
$x : A \to B, a \mapsto x(a)$
, you may want to consider a restriction to a subset of
$A$
via a composition with a map
$a: I \to A, t \mapsto a(t)$
. Now you get
$x \circ a : I \to B, t \to x(a(t))$
. Replace
$A=\mathbb N_1, B = \mathbb R, I = \mathbb N_2, k=a$
to get the second notation (the one I prefer does not work because
$k$
and
$n$
are switched).

Many authors, such as Rudin, use this notation instead.

While I agree that his notation is more general, I feel much more comfortable with mine and I really find the other one difficult to read, at least for sequences.

From the examples I reported above, it looks that both these approaches are accepted.
If you had to choose one to make a convention, what would you pick, and why?
I would appreciate other arguments supporting one or the other point of view, which would encourage others to adopt the suggested convention.

I would say that the opposing approach is the standard.

$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
seems, ironically, to strengthen the opposing approach: using
$k$
to denote values in
$\mathbb N_1$
, and
$n$
to denote values in
$\mathbb N_2$

and
$z=g(y)$
and then
$z=g(f(x))$

$f$
and
$g$

$y=y(x)$
and
$z=z(y)$

$z=z(y(x))$

and then
$x=x_{k_n}$
.

answer:
"The main advantage of this approach is that here you always deal with k"

"The main advantage of this approach is that here you always deal with k"

But.... why on earth would you
WANT
to deal with
$k$
?

The way I see it is you have a seqence
$\{M,\pi, Philip, \sqrt{2}, peanut\ butter,..\}$
and you index it as
$\{x_{\color{green}1},x_{\color{green}2},x_{\color{green}3},x_{\color{green}4},x_{\color{green}5}...\}$
where the index using the letter
$\color{green}k$
refers to which one in the list.

The subsequence is, say,
$\{\pi, Philip, peanut\ butter\}=\{x_{\color{green}2},x_{\color{green}3}, x_{\color{green}5},....\}$
. Where you are dealing with some selection of the green
$k$
's but not all of them.

You want to index the sub sequence as
$\{x_{\color{red}{INDEX}_{\color{blue}1}},x_{\color{red}{INDEX}_{\color{blue}2}},x_{\color{red}{INDEX}_{\color{blue}3}}\}$
. Where the index
${}_{\color{red}{INDEX}_{\color{blue}m}}$
refer to whick of the green
${}_{\color{green}2},{}_{\color{green}3},{}_{\color{green}5}$
we want.

As we are
indexing
the
$\color{green}k$
's we sure as fudgecycles don't want to index them with
$k$
s.

So... I'm with the other way. In fact... I really can't see any other option that makes sense.

answer:
My personal opinion is that: for a given sequence
$(x_n)_n \subset X$
, a
subsequence
should be a function picking out the indices forming the subsequence.

So if
$(x_n)_n \subset X$
is the sequence, then
$\left((x_{f(n)}\right))_n \subset X$
is a particular subsequence.

The question is now how to write down the function
$f \colon n \to f(n)$
. Here I find calling
$k_n := f(n)$
much more reasonable; the "original" indices are all
$k$
's and you pick some
$k$
's out, dependent on some
$n$
. This is clearer, because one index refers to the original sequence and the other to the choice of the indices.

Sequences like
$\left(x_{2n}\right)_n \subset X$
and
$\left(x_{2n+1}\right) \subset X$
makes sense with the choices
$k_n = 2n$
and
$\tilde{k_n} = 2n+1$
.

- they are in fact more suitable for sequences as vectors with countably many entries.

I have just had a discussion with some of my friends about the best (perhaps the correct) way to denote subsequences. The summary is the following:

Given a sequence
$x:\mathbb N \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
, what is more appropriate between
$(x_{n_k}) _{k \in \mathbb N}$
and
$(x_{k_n})_{n \in \mathbb N}$
to denote a subsequence?

At first glance it may look like a pure change of letters, but actually the meaning of these two notations is more subtle. The issue becomes clearer introducing two copies of
$\mathbb N$
. Let me denote them
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

The approach I prefer -
$(x_{n_k})$
- and why:

Let
$x:\mathbb N_1 \to \mathbb R$
be such that
$k \mapsto x_k$
and
$\nu:\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb N_1$
be strictly increasing and such that
$k \mapsto \nu_k$
. I call
$x \circ \nu :\mathbb N_2 \to \mathbb R$
such that
$k \mapsto x_{\nu_k}$
a subsequence.

The main advantage of this approach is that here you always deal with
$k$
. For example, you would say that
$(x_{2k})$
and
$(x_{2k+1})$
are subsequences of
$(x_k)$
, and I think this is very easy to read. Furthermore, the second notation seems ambiguous to me in the sense that
$k$
should be the index moving through
$\mathbb N_1$
, so it might be read as
$(x_{1_n},x_{2_n},\dots)$
, which makes no sense. Of course the meaning here is that you let
$n$
vary across
$\mathbb N_2$
and find indices
$\{k_1 < k_2 < \cdots\} \subset \mathbb N_1$
, but still, I don't find this approach very natural.

This approach is followed in the Italian book "Analisi 1" by Giuseppe De Marco, which is widely considered one of the best books for Italian students attending a first course in Real Analysis.

The approach one of my friends prefer -
$(x_{k_n})$
- and why:

Needless to say
He prefers the second notation. Here are his arguments:

He prefers to use an index for each copy of
$\mathbb N$
. An alleged flaw in my notation is that going from
$(x_k)$
to
$(x_{n_k})$
means that I am using
$k$
both for
$\mathbb N_1$
and
$\mathbb N_2$
.

More generally, if you have a function
$x : A \to B, a \mapsto x(a)$
, you may want to consider a restriction to a subset of
$A$
via a composition with a map
$a: I \to A, t \mapsto a(t)$
. Now you get
$x \circ a : I \to B, t \to x(a(t))$
. Replace
$A=\mathbb N_1, B = \mathbb R, I = \mathbb N_2, k=a$
to get the second notation (the one I prefer does not work because
$k$
and
$n$
are switched).

Many authors, such as Rudin, use this notation instead.

While I agree that his notation is more general, I feel much more comfortable with mine and I really find the other one difficult to read, at least for sequences.

From the examples I reported above, it looks that both these approaches are accepted.
If you had to choose one to make a convention, what would you pick, and why?
I would appreciate other arguments supporting one or the other point of view, which would encourage others to adopt the suggested convention.
answer:
Personally, I’d argue there’s no one “correct” approach in this, only the more natural approach in a given context. I’ll give two different contexts here so that the more natural approach is different between the two:

Taking limits of sequences in a topological space:
Here, we are considering a sequence
$\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$
(or more generally
$\mathbb{N} \to X$
where
$X$
is a topological space) as just a single object in isolation and wish to take its limit. In this case, I’d argue
$(x_{k_n})_n$
is the more natural approach because this notation readily generalizes to subnets. The definition of subnets necessarily allows the usage of a different index set. That is to say, a subnet of
$(I, \leq_I) \ni i \mapsto x_i \in X$
is the composition of
$i \mapsto x_i$
with a (monotone, cofinal) map
$(J, \leq_J) \to (I, \leq_I)$
, where
$J$
can have no connection whatsoever with
$I$
. Writing
$(x_{j_i})_i$
would be strange in this context, since it looks like we are applying the function
$I \ni i \mapsto x_i \in X$
to
$j_i$
, but
$j_i$
looks like it should be in
$J$
instead of
$I$
. Specializing to subsequences, this means the more natural notion is
$(x_{k_n})_n$
here. (This is roughly Lee Mosher’s argument as well. I believe mentioning subnets makes it clearer why considering subsequences in this way is more natural in certain contexts.)

Considering sequences as elements in a sequence space:
There are plenty of situations where one wishes to consider a collection of sequences as a space in its totality, for example, various direct product/direct sum constructions and various sequence spaces in functional analysis. In the specific case of sequences
$\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$
, all such sequences form a real linear space
$\mathbb{R}^\mathbb{N}$
(even a real Fréchet space if we equip it with the product topology). And all classical sequence Banach spaces
$\ell_p$
are its subspaces. In this case, taking subsequences can be understood as an operator on these spaces, in which case
$(x_{n_k})_k$
is arguably more natural. The point here is that such sequence spaces only work if the index set is fixed, so the subsequence should be indexed by the same letter as the original sequence. This allows us to, say, write the sum of a sequence and a subsequence of it by just
$(x_k)_k + (x_{n_k})_k = (x_k + x_{n_k})_k$
, which cannot work if we use the notation
$(x_{k_n})_n$
instead. It is especially convenient if
$n_k$
has a specific form, such as
$n_k = 2k$
, in which case we can write
$(x_k)_k + (x_{2k})_k = (x_k + x_{2k})_k$
. (This is related to the OP’s own argument that this notation allows us to “always deal with
$k$
”. I believe mentioning sequence spaces makes it clearer why considering subsequences this way is more natural in some contexts.)

As a concrete example of the situation above, consider the backwards unilateral shift
$S^\ast: \ell_2 \to \ell_2$
. This is an example of “taking subsequences as an operator on a sequence space”. In fact, it is exactly sending the sequence
$(x_k)_k$
to the subsequence
$(x_{k + 1})_k$
. If one wants to study, say, the spectrum of
$S^\ast$
, then it is natural to consider the operator
$I + S^\ast$
(or
$cI + S^\ast$
for scalars
$c$
in general). Evaluating
$I + S^\ast$
at a sequence
$(x_k)_k$
is exactly
$(x_k)_k + (x_{k + 1})_k = (x_k + x_{k + 1})_k$
. Also note that this is not unique to the subsequence
$(x_{k + 1})_k$
. Any subsequence
$k \mapsto n_k$
yields a co-isometry on
$\ell_2$
.

title: The angle formed by the lines $AI$ and $EF$
question:
In a right-angled
$\triangle ABC$
with
$\angle A = 90^\circ$
, we construct the altitude
$AD$
where
$D \in BC$
. Let
$E$
be the intersection point of the angle bisectors of
$\angle ABD$
and
$\angle BAD$
, and let
$F$
be the intersection point of the angle bisectors of
$\angle ACD$
and
$\angle ADC$
. Let I be the intersection point of the lines
$BE$
and
$CF$
. Determine the measure of the angle formed by the lines
$AI$
and
$EF$
.

I noted
$\angle B = 2b$
and
$\angle C = 2c$
, and I did the angle calculations just like in the drawing, but I don't know how to start.
answer:
(Forgive me for labelling the angle which was to be found as
$90^{\circ}$
from the start.)

Let
$\color{green}{\angle{ABC}=\alpha} \implies \color{\red}{\angle{ACB}=90^{\circ}-\alpha}$

Now,by angle chasing (simple angle sum property), we determine that
$\angle{AJC}=90^{\circ}$
(See
$\triangle{AJC}$
, in the diagram below), and similarly that the
$\angle{EKA}=90^{\circ}$
(See
$\triangle{BAK}$
in the last diagram).

Now, we see that
$I$
seems to be the intersection point of the altitudes of the
$\triangle{EAF}$
, which in short, says that
$I$
is the orthocenter.

$\therefore, \angle{AGE}=90^{\circ}.$

$AI$

$EF$
.

I noted
$\angle B = 2b$
and
$\angle C = 2c$
, and I did the angle calculations just like in the drawing, but I don't know how to start.
answer:
You can also draw the incircle. In the picture we have:

$\angle IAC=\angle IAB$

$\angle IAD=\angle IAF$

$\rightarrow \angle FAP=\angle DAB$

$\angle EAD=\angle MAF$

this means that AM is the reflection of AE over AN. AN is coincident to a diameter of the incircle because it passes through the incenter I. Therefore it is perpendicular to the chord EM that is
$AI\bot EF$
.

Observe that the figure defined by the points

is similar to the figure defined by the points
$CADF.$

This makes it possible to say something about the sides of the triangle
$DEF,$

From that point on the rest can be done by a little angle chasing.

title: Minimum side length of an equilateral triangle for the Second Player to win a circle-packing game
question:
I am considering a combinatorial game played on an equilateral triangle
$T$
with side length
$a > 2\sqrt{3}$
.

Rules of the game:

Two players take turns placing a unit circle (radius
$r=1$
) inside
$T$
.

Every new circle must be disjoint from all previously placed circles (their interiors do not overlap, meaning the distance between any two centers must be at least
$2$
).

Each circle must lie entirely within the boundary of
$T$
.

The game follows the
normal play convention
: the last player to move wins, and the first player unable to make a move loses.

Reduction to Point-Placing:

This problem can be simplified by considering the centers of the circles. A unit circle fits in
$T$
if and only if its center lies within a smaller equilateral triangle
$T'$
with side length
$L = a - 2\sqrt{3}$
. The condition that circles are disjoint is equivalent to saying the distance between any two points (centers) in
$T'$
must be at least
$d = 2$
.

The Question:

For what minimum value of
$a$
does the
second player
have a winning strategy?

My Progress:

In many symmetric domains (like circles or squares), the first player can often win by choosing the center of symmetry. However, the equilateral triangle does not have a center of symmetry.

I have reason to believe that the threshold is
$a = \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
.
At this value, the inner triangle
$T'$
has side length
$L = \frac{8\sqrt{3}}{3}$
and altitude
$H = 4$
.

Note that
$H=4$
is exactly twice the minimum distance
$d=2$
required between points. If
$a < \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
, the first player seems to have an advantage by using axial symmetry or specific placements along the median to prevent the second player from mirroring.

Could someone provide a formal proof or a reference for why
$a = \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
is the critical value for the second player's victory? Any insights into the winning strategy for the second player at this specific
$H=4$
threshold would be greatly appreciated.
answer:
This is not a complete solution, but it gives a reasonably strong lower bound and explains why the proposed value is still not proved.

Pass to the triangle of possible centres and scale all lengths by
$2$
, so that legal centres must be at mutual distance at least
$1$
. Let
$h$
be the altitude of this scaled triangle. If the original equilateral triangle has side length
$a$
, then

$$
h=\frac{a\sqrt3-6}{4},
$$

or equivalently

$$
a=2\sqrt3+\frac{4h}{\sqrt3}.
$$

The proposed value

$$
a=\frac{14\sqrt3}{3}
$$

corresponds exactly to

$$
h=2.
$$

I will show that the first player wins whenever

$$
h<\frac{8\sqrt3}{7}.
$$

This does not reach
$h=2$
, so it does not fully resolve the question.

Put the scaled triangle in coordinates

$$
A=\left(-\frac h{\sqrt3},0\right),\qquad
B=\left(\frac h{\sqrt3},0\right),\qquad
C=(0,h).
$$

Let

$$
M=\left(0,\frac h2\right).
$$

The first player plays
$M$
.  Suppose the second player has a legal reply
$Q$
.  By symmetry, assume that
$Q$
lies in the right half of the triangle.  Write

$$
w=\frac h{\sqrt3},
\qquad
x_0=\sqrt{1-\frac{h^2}{4}}.
$$

For
$0\leq x<x_0$
, every point of the triangle with that
$x$
-coordinate is within distance
$1$
of
$M$
.  Hence a legal reply in the right half must lie in the right-hand corner region, with
$x\geq x_0$
.

The first player now plays
$A$
.  This is legal: every point in the right-hand corner region has distance at least
$w+x_0$
from
$A$
, and for
$0<h<2$
one has

$$
w+x_0>1.
$$

It remains to see that no fourth move is possible.  We first bound the diameter of the right-hand region outside the unit disk about
$M$
.

Use polar coordinates centred at
$B$
.  A point in the right corner may be written as

$$
P=B+r(-\cos\theta,\sin\theta),
\qquad
0\leq \theta\leq \frac{\pi}{3}.
$$

Since

$$
M=\left(0,\frac h2\right)=\left(0,\frac{\sqrt3 w}{2}\right),
$$

we get

$$
|P-M|^2
=
r^2-wr(2\cos\theta+\sqrt3\sin\theta)+\frac{7w^2}{4}.
$$

For fixed
$\theta$
, the part of the ray outside the unit disk about
$M$
and near
$B$
has length at most the smaller positive root of

$$
r^2-wr(2\cos\theta+\sqrt3\sin\theta)+\frac{7w^2}{4}=1.
$$

Now

$$
2\cos\theta+\sqrt3\sin\theta\geq 2
$$

for
$0\leq\theta\leq \pi/3$
, and the smaller root decreases as this coefficient increases.  Therefore the largest possible value of
$r$
occurs when
$\theta=0$
, namely on the base.  Thus the right-hand remaining region is contained in the sector at
$B$
of radius

$$
s=w-x_0
=
\frac h{\sqrt3}-\sqrt{1-\frac{h^2}{4}}.
$$

Since the sector has angle
$\pi/3$
, any two points in it at distance at most
$s$
from
$B$
are themselves at distance at most
$s$
.  Therefore the right-hand remaining region has diameter at most
$s$
.

Now

$$
s<1
$$

is equivalent to

$$
\frac h{\sqrt3}-\sqrt{1-\frac{h^2}{4}}<1.
$$

The equality case gives

$$
h=\frac{8\sqrt3}{7}.
$$

Thus, if

$$
h<\frac{8\sqrt3}{7},
$$

the right-hand remaining region has diameter less than
$1$
.  Since the second player's point
$Q$
lies in that same region, every possible later point in the right-hand region is within distance less than
$1$
of
$Q$
.

The left-hand remaining region is similarly contained in a corner of diameter less than
$1$
about
$A$
, so after the first player plays
$A$
, no legal point remains there either.

Hence no fourth move is possible, and the first player wins.

Converting back to the original side length, this proves that the second player cannot win when

$$
a<2\sqrt3+\frac{32}{7}.
$$

Numerically,

$$
2\sqrt3+\frac{32}{7}\approx 8.03553,
$$

whereas the proposed value is

$$
\frac{14\sqrt3}{3}\approx 8.08290.
$$

So the proposed threshold may still be correct, but this argument does not prove it.  It only proves the lower bound

$$
a\geq 2\sqrt3+\frac{32}{7}
$$

for any possible second-player win.  The remaining gap is the interval

$$
2\sqrt3+\frac{32}{7}
\leq a \leq
\frac{14\sqrt3}{3}.
$$

Closing this gap would require a genuine game-theoretic argument, not just a packing-number argument.

I am considering a combinatorial game played on an equilateral triangle
$T$
with side length
$a > 2\sqrt{3}$
.

Rules of the game:

Two players take turns placing a unit circle (radius
$r=1$
) inside
$T$
.

Every new circle must be disjoint from all previously placed circles (their interiors do not overlap, meaning the distance between any two centers must be at least
$2$
).

Each circle must lie entirely within the boundary of
$T$
.

The game follows the
normal play convention
: the last player to move wins, and the first player unable to make a move loses.

Reduction to Point-Placing:

This problem can be simplified by considering the centers of the circles. A unit circle fits in
$T$
if and only if its center lies within a smaller equilateral triangle
$T'$
with side length
$L = a - 2\sqrt{3}$
. The condition that circles are disjoint is equivalent to saying the distance between any two points (centers) in
$T'$
must be at least
$d = 2$
.

The Question:

For what minimum value of
$a$
does the
second player
have a winning strategy?

My Progress:

In many symmetric domains (like circles or squares), the first player can often win by choosing the center of symmetry. However, the equilateral triangle does not have a center of symmetry.

I have reason to believe that the threshold is
$a = \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
.
At this value, the inner triangle
$T'$
has side length
$L = \frac{8\sqrt{3}}{3}$
and altitude
$H = 4$
.

Note that
$H=4$
is exactly twice the minimum distance
$d=2$
required between points. If
$a < \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
, the first player seems to have an advantage by using axial symmetry or specific placements along the median to prevent the second player from mirroring.

Could someone provide a formal proof or a reference for why
$a = \frac{14\sqrt{3}}{3}$
is the critical value for the second player's victory? Any insights into the winning strategy for the second player at this specific
$H=4$
threshold would be greatly appreciated.

$h=2$

$x$

$$

for

, and the smaller root decreases as this coefficient increases.  Therefore the largest possible value of
$r$
occurs when
$\theta=0$
, namely on the base.  Thus the right-hand remaining region is contained in the sector at
$B$
of radius

$$
s=w-x_0
=
\frac h{\sqrt3}-\sqrt{1-\frac{h^2}{4}}.
$$

Since the sector has angle
$\pi/3$
, any two points in it at distance at most
$s$
from
$B$
are themselves at distance at most
$s$
.  Therefore the right-hand remaining region has diameter at most
$s$
.

$$

$$

$$

the right-hand remaining region has diameter less than

.  Since the second player's point

of

about
$A$

$A$
